The FS4K does start up faster than the Roku but, once running, the Roku runs circles around the FS4K.And the biggest complaint is the horrible WiFi in the FS4K. I've got full GB up/down and I check it often and I normally get nothing short of 800Mbps. Problem is, the WiFi can't handle those speeds--I don't really expect it to--and I get about 250Mbps on the FS4K. I have difficulties streaming 4K shows/movies--which, honestly, should be fast enough--which is frustrating.The Roku allows you to connect via WiFi or wired connection. The FS4K doesn't have this option. I'd prefer to have it wired so I get the full bandwidth available to me. Apparently, there are products, which allow you to connect the older FS via wired connection--but they are extra--and I can't find whether any of these work with the FS4K. Not to mention, they should include this with the FS4K at the price--which is similar to the price of the Roku Ultra which DOES have it included.Don't bother with the FS4K, get another streaming device. If you're an Amazon person, the other devices all have the Amazon app so you'll still have that but you'll have a much better streaming device.

title: Major Audio Issues on 2nd stick too
body: Note: This was my original review below, but notice how it changes over time...Love it so far. No problems and setup was a breeze. Also love the new remote capabilities to control on/off and volume with all of my devices (including my Denon receiver). No more reaching for several remotes.The picture is excellent. With HDR /Dolby Vision turned on, it really compliments the pic on my LG OLED TV.Note: HDR is defaulted to ‘always on’ with the stick, but you can change that in settings to only use that processing with movies that are natively in HDR and/or Dolby Vision.There is only one downfall with this stick, which is the reason I gave it 4 stars… still no Vudu, which has an abundance of movies in HDR/Dolby Vision and Dolby Atmos. Com’on Amazon!11/15/18Update: I dropped my rating from 4 to 3 stars. For the first week it worked great. After a couple of weeks issues began to show up. First issue was random audio drop outs for about a second or 2. Second issue began shortly after when apps suddenly started downloading very slow. After five minutes of waiting for an app to download I gave up. I tried a factory reset, twice, but to no avail. I'm returning this one and will try one more. Hopfully I just got a buggy stick.12/3/2018Update: I'm now dropping my rating from 4 to 2 stars... I'm on my second stick and am having the same random audio drop-out problems as the first one, only this time it's even worse. Sometimes audio looses it's synch with picture and I have to shut it down & restart to re-synch auto. I'm returning stick and purchasing a Roku. I've been using an older Roku for several years on same tv with no issues.

title: i dont thinks it compatible to a 4k UHD T.V
body: i have a 4k UHD samsung 75 in T.V with a Samsung sound bar (Bluetooth). this Amazon FireTV 4k gets me no sound. i trouble shooted it myself. nothing still sound. i disconnected my surround sound bar system to see that was the issue. nope still no sound. i dont think this device is 4k UHD compatible to samasung. even if it worked without my sound bar. i would give this device a bad rating. how doesnt want a theater experience. the device does work with a none 4k T.V tho. i get sound and works better. to me this is False advertisement. All tho i am currently working with the Amazon FireTV developers on this issue. very nice people and know how to keep in communication. Hopefully we can fix this problem. it would suck if i wont be able to use my sound bar surround system. if i cant use it the way i would like will be sending this product back to Amazon. if you have a none 4k TV it works Great. no its not my HDMI ports i checked them they all work. just doesnt work correctly with the (4k amazon fireTV with Alexa device). Alexa doesnt work right either even with the regular flat screen TV. i think this device is way over valued should be sold no more than 15 dollars. if we can fix this device to work how it should with my sound bar. than yea this device should be sold no less than 70 dollars. which in current price is under valued. will update if the amazon developers and i can resolve this issue. sure hope so. Stand by for update. im sure we can fix this issue. still thoK. So they didnt call me back. I haf to reach out to them after the 48 HR wait. They couldnt fix it. So this product doesnt work on my my 4k T.V. way over valued should be 10 to 15 dollars. Not 50.

title: Intermittent Poor Video Quality worsened by the Extension !
body: I was enjoying the Fire Stick itself for the most part for the fact that you are able to use so many different apps and it has more space than most older TVs. For the first few weeks I was having some major video quality issues in the form of shadowing and flickering in random spots sporadically mostly during Amazon Prime 4K Ultra programming. Having done my research online and not finding any complaints or answers and troubleshooting with samsung, I know it is not my TV because I have watched the same stuff directly through my TV and it looks great, no video issues at all, crystal clear. I thought I finally figured out that the problem was not the stick itself but the piece of junk extension that comes with the stick. As a matter of fact I had replaced the stick (and the extension, just in case) thinking it was either the stick or possibly my HDMI port, until narrowing it down to that piece just yesterday. I was glad after 3 weeks that I finally tried it without and it looked so much better! I thought the problem was totally gone. But after watching for several hours, lo and behold, the shadowing/ flickering has reared it's evil head again. 😐 Amazon, you really need to work on that extension. But I fear that is not the only problem. It definitely exacerbates the problem, but the problem still exists in the stick itself. 😐 I will be keeping it, but only because I need it for the space and because some apps don't run well on my older samsung. The quality just is not up to par. 😕 I really hope they work on fixing the glitches in the stick and especially in the extension, if you can run the stick without it, I highly recommend it. It really takes away from the quality.

title: Doesn't live up to the hype
body: I purchased this in hopes of replacing my parents 1st Gen Fire Stick for Christmas this year. I set it up at my own home first so that I could 1. Install all of their apps for them and 2. See if it would be worth switching away from Roku.I am in my late 20's and consider myself to be very tech savvy for someone who doesn't work in a tech related field. Setting this up was very easy. The directions were straightforward and I encountered no issues.My issues arouse once I was ready to stream. The homescreen is cluttered with Amazon promoted content and not all of your apps are displayed right there on the main page. I understand that it's an Amazon product and they want to promote their content, but at least let me see all my apps that I personally selected because I use them frequently. My other issue with this is trying to explain to my parents that they need to look a little further for their apps. It's just not quite as simple as the competition.I watched a few shows on Netflix, Hulu, and Plex. Netflix worked fine. The device restarted 3 separate times while watching Hulu. Plex was abysmal at best. The Plex app was absolutely the worst out of the three. Downloading subtitles would fail repeatedly and sometimes if I were already watching the show I would have to go back to the episode page and then load them. There was also a bizarre 5 minutes where the Fire remote kept turning my soundbar on and off and on and off that seemed to resolve once we rebooted the Fire TV.Overall I am not impressed. I'll be returning this device and going with a different streaming stick that works and has a more user friendly interface instead.

title: Streaming issues and Amazon control over customer desires
body: I have had six 4K Fire sticks in six rooms of my house for the last eleven months. Buffering has been a huge problem with AT&T TV Now and Hallmark Movies Now on all of them. I immediately purchased a new large home router, added a network extender, tried both 2G and 5G networks (5G is recommended by Amazon for this problem, but the result is worse), and tried to calm my wife's frustration with them. The home screen is way too busy for our desired use-we only need and want the apps we use to be on the home screen and not be cluttered up with everything . We don't want the Amazon Photos app, but you can not uninstall it. We don't want the Amazon Freetime app because it is way too restrictive for our family use (tried it on our great grandkids Fire HD tablets) and have uninstalled it many, many times, but it always reappears again. We should have total control over what apps we want on our purchases-not Amazon! Amazon apparently just updated , 11/1/2019, one of my fire sticks and added a "News" app which is mostly their founders liberal ideology sources. Once again, they do not allow us to uninstall what they want us to use!!We have just purchased a Roku streaming stick and find it much more satisfactory to our wants and desires-home screen is much simpler, we are able to only have the apps we want on it (made wife very happy), and so far, no buffering issues (again, made wife very happy). If buffering continues to not be a problem, we plan to replace all six Amazon Fire Sticks with Roku sticks which will also open up the possible use of the Spectrum and Disney streaming services that are not available with Amazon sticks.

title: Where's the 4K Movies and TV Shows? And where are all my Purchased Videos?
body: OK so this is a very good product. That said there are some Real Big Problems with the Firestick 4K TV. Not anything to do with how good it works or how great the picture is no it has to do with the setup and display of what is available. The 2 Biggest Gripes are the Fact that to see all my owned movies on Amazon that I Bought On Amazon I Must load up the Amazon Prime Video App on the Amazon Firestick 4K TV to see all of my owned Movies! This is a Big Problem due to the fact that the App has a not as equal pic quality. I was going to count how many movies it shows but I have over 50 movies and to see the last 3/4 of them I have to load the Amazon app to launch them then let them play then get out of the app and then start the movie on the Firestick this does NOT Work and Must be Addressed. The Second Big problem is well it says 4K... So why Must I search for 4K programing it has no section on the 4K Firestick TV and even when I see a movie and let's say I want to buy it let's take The Avengers (Any movie that's out in 4K) and I search for it it does show up but when bringing up the purchase menu there is no listing for UHD/4K only SD or HD, no if you want to buy 4K 9 times out 10 you Must Search For the movie in 4K to try and buy it. Now I find this strange seeing as how 4K is in the name and A Big selling point so why is it so Hard to find 4K on your Amazon 4K Firestick TV hmmm Amazon? And why to see my purchases must I load up the app on the Firestick 4K TV and Yes it was linked to my Amazon Prime Acoumt By Amazon when it was bought. Those 2 Big reasons is why this Did NOT GET 5 Stars fix it and you will see 5 stars here.

title: Disappointing that my Amazon Video Library won't show within Amazon - SEE BELOW
body: UPDATE: 2 HOURS AFTER PRIOR REVIEW. I'm not removing my 2 stars. The my Video Library DOES exist. But the "tech" product support people - both of those I spoke with checked with senior specialists and came back to me and said, "Nope. No Video Library." It's there. I had to find it myself. It's not within the Amazon app - I mean, it sort of is.It's located on the APP screen with all the other channels. You have to put the cursor over the Amazon app and the Video library option appears at the top of the screen. Amazon needs to train their employees better. Especially the tech employees.I'm totally perplexed by this lack of oversight. I've seen many others with the same complaint. The movies are there but you have to find them. "Your Video Library" does NOT show up.Also, unlike with ROKU, there is no banner at the top of movies to indicate a "PRIME MOVIE", meaning included with subscription vs. movies you have to pay for. You have to search for movies, then click on the movie to see purchase options, including if it's PRIME or you have to pay for it.So disappointing. I called Amazon Customer Service, the division that handles digital devices and they were as shocked as I was about the absence of the Video Library as well as the banner that indicates which movies are prime vs. those which are not.My workaround is embarrassing...for Amazon. I will need to go into my Amazon account on my computer and reference to the movies that I own. I have over 50 of them so remembering them all isn't practical.Come on, Amazon. This was totally misleading.

title: Voice command did not work for YouTube.tv
body: On the positive side, the streaming quality (@ 25 mb) was very good and the choice of live channels on YouTube.tv would have met our preferences. The Fire controller was easy to set up and paired with the tv easily. My biggest disappointment was that Alexa did not work at all for YouTube.tv. When I asked for a channel or program, Alexa always activated the YouTube video library, not live tv. We were evaluating streaming services as compared with Dish satellite, which we currently use and found the navigation to get to a particular channel awkward at best and confusing at worst. For example on the Dish controller, once you know the channel number you simply enter three or four digits and you get the channel. On the Fire Stick you either scroll through the channel listing, or go to the screen key pad and point the cursor to each letter in the name of the program or channel (awkward!). If you happen to hit a wrong key or control on the stick you could end up in netherland. More than once, I had to restart the fire stick because the home button didn't get me to the beginning. Another annoying thing was that when the home button worked, which in fairness was most of the time, the Fire Stick home screen always showed a variety of streaming options. It would have been nice if you could control what the Fire home screen showed through the Settings function. If the Alexa voice command had worked on the YouTube.tv streaming service, much of this inconvenience would have been avoided. In the end we decided to not use the Fire Stick with streaming services.

title: New Home Screen is BAD
body: 5/30/2021 [UPDATE] I despise the new user interface update. Clearly, it was designed to make Prime the center of your streaming universe but they don’t have enough content for that to be so. What once took a click or two now takes multiple clicks because you can only display a handful of apps on the Home Screen no matter how big you TV. The rest of the screen is filled with Prime suggestions. I also use Netflix, PBS, AT&T, Plex, HDHOMERUN, Paramount +, Peacock, VUDU, Spotify and others. All but a handful are now buried in a submenu. To add insult to injury, they no longer support Peacock, not even as a side loaded app. I’m finished with these Firesticks. I threw them in the trash and replaced them with Apple TV boxes, where all of my apps can be viewed on the Home Screen and I even have more control over my privacy.[Original] The stick was easy to get started with. It was already linked to my account so I just had to go through the process of providing credentials to the various services I subscribe to (AppleTV+, Netflix, HBO, Showtime, PBS) but Prime was already good to go. Streaming quality has been very good, data buffering is effective to keep the show going, and the user interface is pretty easy to learn. My spouse is a technophobe but she picked it up pretty quickly. My only peeve, a minor one, is you have to use the back arrow multiple times to get back to the home screen from many of the apps. I'd like to see the Home button reprogrammed to do what it should do, take you back to the home screen from anywhere in one click.

title: Really? No sleep timer?
body: Pros: It's cheap.Cons: No sleep timer and a general lack of options normally available in video web players.Here's the deal, I needed a way to play back web video on my basic HDTV, nothing fancy. This device does that but it's super basic. I like to fall asleep watching something and since there is no sleep timer on the fire stick it means I have to go outside the device ecosystem for functionality that's been available since the time of the first IR TV remote control. Ludicrous!Second, I really don't care about Alexa and this device really tries to push Alexa. For some reason I also can't turn off auto play for Amazon's TV shows which means combined with a lack of a sleep function means I won't be watching anything by Amazon for falling asleep because it'll continue playing season after season until I wake up to turn it off. Just really stupid stuff.Lastly, I have poor internet situation. Technically I have unlimited but it's 4G so it can be prioritized after 20 GB or so. For this reason I'd like to stream most shows in SD quality but apps like Hulu don't allow that option to be selected manually, unlike the player in their web browser. Amazon does for their video content, thankfully, and somewhat surprisingly.Overall I have mixed feelings about the Fire TV 4k to the point where I'll probably try some of the competing products to see if they fit my needs better. As per usual devices like this are designed in house with very little consideration for unconventional lifestyles.

title: Overall Excellent Device With One Glaring Problem
body: First of all, I own two of these, and the Fire TV AKA Firestick is a very good device. I highly recommend it overall for all the reasons that many other reviewers are singing its praises. It does many, many things very well. It gets 5 stars for voice recognition, stream quality, and Alexa integration. I love having all my content available on one home screen. Roku does a very good, job as do the other competitors to Firestick, but Amazon's device is clearly the best of the bunch, in my opinion. Though I will say that the others are getting better, too.By the way, a lot of people complain about there being so many advertisements on the Home Screen and other content screens on the Firestick. But I disagree with this. Because it isn't like Amazon is spamming us with ads for dishwashers and outdoor BBQ's lol. They have simply created a huge landscape with tons of channels and content that in many cases I would not have even known about were it not for the ads and icons for them. To me, this is a plus not a minus. I have some minor niggles with the Alexa integration and sometimes the wonky connectivity of either the Firestick remote (in my bedroom) or the Logitech Harmony Elite I use in the living room. BUT these things, in my estimation, are part of the natural evolution of the platform, and they have been getting steadily better since I started using Firestick a few years ago. So I am being patient about this.I love the fact that I can simply add all my cable channels that I currently subscribe to and watch them with corresponding apps. I subscribe to ALL the channels through Optimum Online, and pay the monthly subscriptions, but it is incredibly convenient to have the apps on various devices, especially the Firestick, so that the term "cutting the cord" is really true, as cliché as it sounds. But this brings me to my problem. It may not be entirely Amazon's fault, either, but Amazon has to accept some blame here, too.The problem is that I pay my monthly fee for Cinemax, just as I do for all the HBO's, Showtime services, and pretty much everything except for the adult channels like Spice or Playboy. Yet, mysteriously there is no MaxGo or Cinemax app on the Firestick.Now anyone in the know will likely get snippy now and say that its not Amazon's fault because Cinemax, HBO's sister channel, needs to make their app compatible with the Firestick platform, and so far they have refused. So Amazon cannot allow Firestick or Prime members to download an app that the devs on the Cinemax team have not created for the Firestick, right? True, and you would be 100% correct to point this out to me.But hear me out first before flaming the review lol (I wish I could make that bold)Where Amazon is dropping the ball is by offering the channel as a subscription service and placing it smack, dab in the middle of my home screen. Why is this so bad? Simple! Amazon Prime is offering a separate (completely separate) subscription to Cinemax. So, what this means to anyone who subscribes to Cinemax through our cable companies, like I do, now have two choice:1. Pay for Cinemax twice; Once to your cable company AND then to Amazon as a premium add-on to Prime Video2. Not have access to Cinemax on your Firestick at all, BUT you have to now be spammed by the advertisement FOR A CHANNEL YOU ARE ALREADY PAYING FOR but can't watch unless you pay again every month!While you cannot download a nonexistent app, and I certainly do not blame Amazon or its Prime Video division for that, Amazon NEEDS to embargo this channel until Cinemax creates an app for the Firestick platform. So I am knocking off TWO full stars from my review until such time as Amazon fixes this. If they do, I will adjust my review to 4.5 Stars, rounded up to 5.Either they need to find a way to entice Cinemax to make the app compatible with Firestick or they need to completely embargo all Cinemax content from the Firestick. At the very least, they need to STOP advertising that channel on the Home screen so that they aren't encouraging this Cinemax-developer laziness. I say laziness, because MaxGo is available as an app for the PC, iOS, and Android devices.Why Amazon (and Apple, and Roku) tolerate this is completely beyond me. But Amazon is a hugely powerful international company. They have the power, along with Apple and Roku, to exert incredible pressure on unscrupulous (or just lazy) channel companies like Cinemax to do the right thing. They may not be able to force these devs (and I am sure Cinemax is not the only one) to make their apps compatible, but they can certainly embargo the content until they do.So, as I said, my review will remain a firm 3 stars until Amazon either demands Cinemax fix this OR Amazon does the right thing with a embargo on the channel until they do. Although, I am aware that some people who DO NOT subscribe to Cinemax through their cable/satellite providers will object to this because they are NOT being forced to pay twice.So my compromise is this: There should at least be a way for people like me to REMOVE all advertisements for Cinemax from my Home screen completely, even without an absolute embargo. I should NOT have to endure advertisements to PAY TWICE for a channel, but I am also well aware that it would be unfair to completely embargo the channel for people not subscribing to all the channels and who choose to pay Amazon Prime instead. They should be able to do so.

title: A worthy upgrade ... with one huge, and terrible, glitch for multi-channel receiver/speaker setups
body: If you're upgrading from the non-4K 2nd-gen ("basic") Fire Stick, with the original remote, the first thing you'll notice is that the new remote can control the power and volume on your TV and/or AV receiver -- a massive upgrade. The second thing you'll notice is how much smoother and quicker the on-screen controls respond to the remote's commands; really, the difference is not insignificant. The 4K Fire Stick is a pleasure to use compared to the often-sluggish basic version, in almost every way.Since I'm still using a standard 1080p HDTV and not 4K I haven't yet noticed a difference in picture quality, but the big deal here is the audio -- a double-edged sword if there ever was one, if you have a multi-channel receiver and speaker setup and use a streaming app for live TV.The 4K Fire Stick has different audio settings than the basic Fire Stick on which, under "Audio," the sub-setting "Dolby Digital Output" can be set to (1) "Dolby Digital Plus Automatic," (2) "Dolby Digital Plus OFF," (3) "Dolby Digital Plus over HDMI" or (4) "Dolby Digital over HDMI." On the 4K Fire Stick, the sub-setting under "Audio" is "Surround Sound" which also has four settings: (1) "Best Available," (2) "Stereo," (3) "Always Dolby Digital Plus" and (4) "Always Dolby Digital." The following paragraph describes what happens with the 4K Fire Stick set to "Best Available," compared to the basic Fire Stick set to "Dolby Digital Plus Automatic," each of which is the default setting for the respective devices:The 4K Fire Stick provides Dolby Digital Plus ("DD+") audio to my new Denon A/V receiver on all of the major streaming apps that I use, including those that *don't* support DD+ on the basic Fire Stick such as Disney+ (another good reason to upgrade). But here's the double edge, and it really cuts: If you have a DD+ receiver with a multi-speaker surround setup, and use a live TV streaming app like YouTube TV which supports stereo only, you won't be able to use any of the surround-sound processing modes on your receiver (such as Dolby ProLogic II) because unlike the basic Fire Stick which outputs 2-channel PCM, this one sends a multi-channel signal to the receiver even though only 2 of the channels have any audio. You'll hear the left and right front speakers only; all the other channels will be dead.The *only* way around this is to set the 4K Fire Stick to "Always Dolby Digital." At that setting, stereo sources will output in stereo as they should and thereby enable ProLogic and other processing modes, but DD+ from streaming apps that support it will be downgraded to Dolby Digital ("DD"), sacrificing the higher bitrate and resolution of DD+. In other words, you can either (1) give up surround sound processing for live TV streaming and settle for 2-channel stereo; (2) give up DD+ on Netflix, Prime, Disney+, &c. and settle for DD; or (3) go to the menu and change the aforementioned setting every time you switch between stereo and multi-channel content, to get the best audio possible from whatever you're about to watch.Of course, if you don't use an A/V receiver with a multi-speaker surround setup (and/or don't use streaming for live TV or other stereo-only content) this won't affect you; if your receiver and speaker setup is 5.1 and not 7.1 or more, it won't affect you as much and you may not even be able to tell the difference between DD and DD+. (I've never used a sound bar, so I can't speak to those users.) But it seems there's always something with the audio output from these Fire TV devices; as noted above the basic Fire Stick doesn't support DD+ from some apps which the 4K Fire Stick does, but it also doesn't have this problem with stereo sources when set to "Dolby Digital Plus Automatic." Having just recently bought a new receiver to replace a 17-year-old non-HDMI DD/DTS receiver that couldn't decode DD from some apps streaming DD+ and, now, upgraded the basic Fire Stick to this device, I thought my audio problems were over but alas, they're not. Upgrading just meant trading one set of audio glitches/incompatibilities/shortcomings for another, which is quite frustrating.This has been a known issue for at least two years according to the product forum, so obviously it's never going to be addressed let alone fixed. Again, if you don't use a multi-channel receiver and speaker setup this won't affect you, but if you do, and use a streaming app for live TV, just be wary of this issue before you buy.UPDATE:There's a separate, related issue with the Netflix app, and how it delivers stereo (non-5.1) content to the receiver from the 4K Fire Stick. No matter what "Surround Sound" is set to, the Netflix app will still send a DD+/DD 5.1 signal to the receiver for stereo content; the sound will come out of the front left and right speakers only and surround-sound processing will be unavailable. Worse yet, if 5.1 content is played after stereo content is played, without exiting Netflix or restarting the device, the 5.1 content will come through in stereo, as described. The only way around *this* is to use a different device, such as a Chromecast or smart-TV app, to watch stereo content on Netflix.UPDATE 2: Sad to report, although altogether unsurprised, that the new UI update did *not* fix this problem.

title: Good for many things but disappointing & intermittent CEC equipment control
body: I have the fire stick 4k, new in August 2018 - so for two months now. It does most things well, but as many other reviewers have noted, the remote equipment control was only partially implemented and what does work is also intermittent. Amazon's oversights in this regard, and their support stubbornness make the experience very limited.If you're familiar with CEC equipment control, you know it's supposed to allow any device that supports CEC to receive and send remote control commands over the HDMI cable. In this case, both my fire stick 4K and my Samsung 55" HD TV support CEC and have it enabled.I guess we could debate which of the following problems are the most serious :-)1. The first problem is with the fire stick's inability to properly RECEIVE commands over CEC. One of the KEY advantages of CEC is that I should be able to use all the applicable buttons on my Samsung remote to control the fire stick. I know the problem is not with my TV, because when I set the input to my media center, functions work perfectly, including play and pause keys, up/down/right/left arrow keys, FF/REW keys, enter/back/exit keys, etc. But when the TV is set to the fire stick hdmi input, most of those functions don't work, and sometimes what does work stops working until I restart my fire stick. I spent 90 minutes with multiple support folks in chat session - making matters worse, those folks claim that receiving CEC is not supported AT ALL on the fire stick. That's just whacky, it would mean none of my other remotes are usable (eg my TV IR remote, my STB universal IR remote, etc) when using the stick. At least give me play and pause! In reality, what does work in receive mode - sometimes - is the enter key which alternately pauses and plays whatever media is running on the stick. FF and rewind also work, and that is all. As noted, even those functions go away from time to time, requiring restart of the stick. 2. As many reviewers have noted, the TV volume and power controls on the fire stick remote only work in IR mode, when you point the fire remote at the TV. This is a huge miss in my opinion. Since the fire stick remote is bluetooth connected to the stick, there is no reason the stick cannot use CEC so that I don't have to point at the TV. And, as other reviewers noted, scenarios with monitors and other connected equipment become very limited and frustrating.3. Moving on to the small part of CEC control that support claims is indeed supported, that too is limited and intermittent. When the fire stick settings are correct (correct model TV, control set to automatic, CEC mode, HDMI port of fire stick set, etc) and then when you tell the stick to "play I love lucy", it should power on the TV, set the proper HDMI input port of the TV, and bring up whatever program it thinks you meant by "I love Lucy". Well, it does this - sometimes. And sometimes it does most of it, without powering on the TV, and then when I power on the TV and set the input, I find I love lucy playing. If the TV was already on, it usually changes the input and starts the program properly. Finally, about once per week, all this stops working and I have to restart the fire stick (from settings / my fire stick).Other:It seems to me one of the reasons for some of the limitations may stem from the simplified (oversimplified?) stick setup for TV control. I only get one working choice for Samsung TV's when running the equipment setup for TV. If, during guided setup of the TV control, I say "No" that power control did not work, it gives me 5 more tries, and none of those actually work, so I'm stuck with the one working Samsung setup, the first one. And I'm not sure that is appropriate for CEC for all of Samsung.Given the above limitations, an alternative is the fire tv app for android phones and tablets, which seems to be a decent alternative to control the stick playback so at least you don't have to run out and buy extra fire stick remotes for more than one user to have control of a movie. But there's no substitute for using that armchair handheld TV IR remote (or the pile of universals next to it), and that should work, even though they say it should not, and even though it does work, but only partially without play or pause, and even that only works sometimes.Again all this CEC functionality works perfectly and reliably with my other CEC enabled HDMI connected media players and the same Samsung TV. One of the reasons I dumped my old Roku players was because Roku does not (or didn't used to) support CEC at all. I'm not sure if Roku is considering CEC or not, but I bought the stick because it advertises CEC support. Sad.For Amazon's abysmal implementation, testing and support of CEC, we can only say "Cmon Man".

title: Overall it’s decent but majorly flawed
body: The device comes with 5gb of available storage and a few unnecessary apps already installed. After installing a few 5-6 apps depending on the size you will be having to delete some files due to the limited space. Some apps also download adds to the stick so it really can be filled easily.The only available remedy for this is to use an otg cable to add a useable USB port for expansion via a thumb drive and use third party software. Either the cable should come included with purchase or the device should be redesigned to include a micro sd slot and proper software support integrated to move apps and data.By default the stick doesn’t work with any of my game controllers besides a ps5 controller and using that the button mappings are out of wack and a few essential buttons are just not usable, the main being rb and the O button are entirely non functional and can’t be mapped using any software even third party. Steam controllers and switch controllers do not work with the stick, you can pair but they’re not useable. And this is across all fire tv devices.I wish they released a new stick with 6gb of ram, 32gb of internal storage and an expansion micro sd slot or to include the otg cable rather than the max they just released which is a minimal upgrade from this one. If anyone from Amazon actually reads these reviews please know that I would be willing to spend up to 75 dollars for a stick with the above included as it would remedy most of my issues with the device, if they could do both the slot and include the otg cable it would be the best of both worlds for any normal user who doesn’t want to mess around with third party software.Currently I use this stick in a Christie lwu510i projector and it’s been working great minus the above issues. The stick can be powered by the projectors onboard usb rather than using an external source as long as the micro usb cable that Is used to power the stick is under 6 inches in length, then enough power comes out to fully use the stick and you will get no warning messages. I have the audio coming from the sticks Bluetooth into a echo dot that has its aux cord plugged into an old avr for full 7.1 support, it has some delay issues intermittently but it can be resolved by disconnecting and reconnecting or messing around with the av sync options but the sync options seem to be hit or miss and sometimes doesn’t even apply changes made in it.Using the ps5 controller and GeForce now I have been able to play a few games thanks to being able to remap some buttons but it’s still not a optimal experience, maybe a cheap controller would work better but unfortunately if you have a bunch of controllers already from other consoles they probably will have issues, I would recommend either the Luna controller or a cheap android based one as this would most likely solve the mapping issues since it doesn’t seem like Amazon will be doing anything to help make the other controllers function.And now finally the reason I say the stick needs more ram, while using the web browser to watch anything if the browser page has an abundance of adds it will cause the stick to overflow and the video your watching will start to stutter until you refresh the page to clear the cache only for it to occur again once enough ads have loaded, if they included both the onboard storage extensions and more ram this will remedy this issue. It would also make some browser games playable which would be a huge bonus going forward since game streaming seems to be the future focus.Also if it’s possible I would suggest someone to include the ability to talk to any other Alexa device and have it still able to use the controllers ir blaster to turn devices on and off, as it stands now if I talk to my phone or my echo devices it can’t turn the projector or my avr on or off while only the remote can, I would love if you could have it still use the ir from the remote to control devices while talking to those other echo devices or using the app on your phone.

title: Stopped working 1 day after "return window closed" Requires ALL apps to be deleted!!!
body: [EDIT #2 6/12/22] My remote died. Odd thing - it still had the original batteries, LOL. Amazing they outlasted the hardware. I put in two brand new (tested) batteries and... nada. Can't figure out how to pair another remote with this (I have 3 of these things). A new "remote only" is $26 a new remote AND Firestick is only $25. A new 4K firestick is $10 more. My TV, as nice as it is, isn't 4k so... I'm kinda on the fence. I may just start using an extra computer I have around the house.[EDIT 5/23/2021] Device began working again, apparently normally a couple of days after last update.However.. I still LOST ALL of my previous settings and am not able to reinstall all of the apps I had deleted. I've upgraded to a 3 star since it does currently "basically" work. But I still don't recommend it.There is something seriously wrong with this device.[EDIT 2/2/2021] After deleting every single app that could be deleted (not IMDb for some inexplicalbe reason, nor, of course, any of the "Amazon" apps) EXCEPT the 50MB Disney+ (the whole reason for having this piece of garbage really) the completely wrong and infuriatingly incorrect "delete apps" error now only pops up every 5 minutes. Without fail. No matter what it's doing - even if in the middle of actually deleting apps it interrupts even that process thereby disallowing me to do anything until I close it.It is absolutely maddening, irritating, galling and exasperating to have to click out of it 12 times each hour or about 16-20 times just to watch a movie.I'm going to look for something else that I and my family can use to Watch our favorite apps and channels and forget about watching any of the "Prime" crappola.And.. I'm just out not only the $40+ bucks spent on it, but also the $20 spent on a power adaptor and the $15 spent on the Ethernet adaptor.Now, I REALLY don't recommend purchasing this lightweight device that only works for 2 weeks.Unless, of course, you have an extra $75 to spend on something that only works for 2 weeks OR.. like, want or NEED to watch something on Prime so badly that you are willing to put up with the constant interruptions.==========Today is Feb 1st, 2021 as of yesterday the "return window" for this conveniently closed.I purchased it on Dec 17th, 2020 as a Christmas gift for my spouse. I set it up (installed) it only 2-3 weeks ago. AFTER the first week of January. I installed (only) Disney+ (59MB), Hulu (59 MB) and ESPN+ 41 (MB). For those last couple of weeks it seemed to work fine.But now, no matter what I try, every 10 seconds I get a pop-up (see pix) that demands I "remove apps to free up at least 500 MB on your device" . I have less than 1GB of apps and all but the 3 above CAME PRELOADED WITH THE DEVICE! Moreover, even after deleting some of the bloatware that came with it (Crackle, TMC, ec.) I STILL receive this unyielding popup.Worst of all (see pix) I HAVE OVER 1 GB OF STORAGE SPACE LEFT!!! So why it's asking for 500MB makes NO sense since it already has TWICE what it says it requires!I cannot do ANYTHING with this! each time I try to go anywhere the popup shows and doesn't allow me to do anything but either delete apps or go to the menu for 10 seconds.The ONLY way to get ANOTHER 500 MB of storage would be to delete pretty much ALL of the apps I have on it. Including: FireTV Player (73MB), IMDbTV (101MB), Pluto TV (41MB), Tubi (31MB), etc (they all get smaller from here)I would LOVE to also delete Amazon FreeTime (55MB), Amazon Music (54MB) and Amazon Photos (39MB).BUT I CAN'T!!! I don't use these, don't need them and don't want them and if I have to delete apps these should go also. BUT I can only "clear data" or "clear cache". Both of which are either @ ZERO MB or in the Kilobytes (1,000 times SMALLER than a MB!).Not sure what's wrong with it but I currently..- WILL NOT USE THIS DEVICE (actually I *CAN'T!!!)- Do NOT recommend you purchase it.

title: Probably the best of limited choices
body: The good the bad and the ugly.First things first, the installation is buggy. It had to “update” the software several times. Alexa refused to work and it kept dropping the internet connection and wanting the network password again. (It is 24” from the WiFi hub, I didn’t upgrade to the wired connection) I spent 15 minutes working on voice recognition with no luck and it worked fine the next morning. After 24 hours the Wifi connection works fine. For most software, you can cut the programers some slack. There are an infinite number of hardware combinations they they have to account for. In this case they control both the hardware and software and still can't get it right. The pre-registration feature doesn't seem to make any difference in the set up.Content: with an Amazon prime subscription, there is more than you could watch for free. However, finding it is like looking for a needle in a haystack. Youtube search is much better. Even if you use the “included with Prime” app, which I love, you end up being directed to non-free content. But you don’t realize you are in non free content until you decide to watch something.There doesn’t seem to be any organization to the content offerings. Youtube learns. Firestick doesn’t seem to. This makes the amount of content a mixed blessing. You have to dig through 100 apps that you have no interest in to find some that you do. I haven’t found a way to do this with Firestick yet. What I do is get on my phone and do a Google search for: “Best Apps on Firestick for _______” (Don’t start me on how bad Google has become.)You can’t tell from the top menu if something is: free, free with a subscription, pay per view, free with a cable provider, other………. You have to click to the buy screen to see what it does or doesn’t cost.This is VERY problematic because if you get to an old free Bogart movie you like, it will suggest a wealth of similar offerings, some free and most not.Often, I could make the decision from the top menu if they would provide the pay information. This format causes the view to go down a lot of blind alleys.Overall I am pleased but only because the older I get the lower my expectations become for modern IT.The remote is heads above every other remote. They just give you all the buttons you need and no more. what a major engineering breakthrough. (sarcasm)I looked at the Fire Box but all the reviews said it wasn't as good as this.I got the 4k model for the extra memory. Once installed the base package chews up about half the memory but new apps only use up an additional 1 or 2 %.. I have had it a month but have not yet identified very many apps that I want to add.I purchased it because they got me addicted to Grand Tour and Bosch and then refused to directly support my 5 year old smart TV. Furthar, Panasonic seemed more interested in capturing and selling my data than making the top of the line TV they sold me user friendly.I am so old that if I ever have to talk to anyone in an Apple store again I may burst a blood vessel and die on the spot, so Apple TV was out and it seems like Firestick is the only other game in town.So in conclusion Firestick reminds me of the old toast: “here’s to you as good as you are and to me as bad as I am, and as good as you are and as bad as I am, I’m still as good as you are as bad as I am.”Don't fall for the advertisements for "Jailbroken Firesticks" with pre loaded apps. You can "jailbrake" your own firestick with the click of a button. Beyond that, you can do most of what you can do legally out of the box. If you want to steal copywriter material you still can do that without paying extra. (I do NOT recommend that. Good way to let Russian Hackers into your life)It is good but could be great!

title: SOLVED: it works okay with recast now, thanks Amazon tech support
body: I bought this stick with the fire recast for my new living room TV with all the bells and whistles. The stick is about 5 feet from my wireless router in direct line of sight and yet it keeps buffering and the picture gets very pixelated. So much for HDR, 4K etc... I have a fire TV 4k and a regular fire stick in two different bedrooms, farther away from the router and no L.O.S. and they work flawlessly. I had to force my router to a single 20MHz carrier on channel 165 in the 5GHz band where there's no interference from neighbors so that the stick is useable, but it still buffers and pixelates though not as bad. I read an article online where it says it's a known bug but no expected timeframe for a solution. Well Amazon what are you waiting for to fix it? You're selling the recast with this device in a bundle knowing that it works like s%$t?!?! I'm seriously thinking about returning it and getting another fire TV 4k (not the stick) but then I'd be stuck with the old remote. I just can't win I guess. Please let us poor consumers know when the fix is coming. I'll update my review once it's fixed unless I just end up returning it.EDIT: shortly after I wrote this review I was contacted by Amazon by phone to provide technical support and try and solve my issues. I find that very nice customer support however they were unable to solve anything at all. Disturbingly, they claimed Amazon was not aware of any bug. After a few more days with the stick 4K I've noticed that my issues are mostly using it with my fire recast. That's where I'm noticing the buffering and the low quality picture. I think I'm getting 480p at most when HD channels are supposed to be re-encoded to 720p. But very often quality drops well below that, so bad that you can see big square pixels everywhere. And again my other two fire devices work flawlessly, and the difference in picture quality is night and day. I'm no longer sure the issue has anything to do with WiFi performance at all. If you're using fire recast or plan on getting one, DO NOT BUY the fire stick 4K to go with it. There's something going on between the two of them and they just do not work well together at all. I can't believe Amazon is bundling them together, really?! Keeping one star and repeat DO NOT BUY IF YOU'RE PLANNING ON GETTING A RECAST.EDIT 2: after more experimenting I found out that if I restart both the stick and the recast everything works well for a few hours then the buffering and pixelation start again. That tells me it's definitely a software issue so I'm hopeful that it will get fixed soon. I just wish Amazon would acknowledge there's an issue and give a timeline for a patch. They're supposed to get back to me, because I have an open ticket. I hope at least they come up with a workaround while waiting for a software update. Keeping the 1-star rating for now until I hear back from Amazon.EDIT 3: Amazon tech support finally came up with a workaround that fixed the problem I was having. There's a special combination of key presses on the remote that will bring up a hidden menu and from there I was instructed to disable a feature called "network congestion optimization". It's been a few days since that operation was done and I haven't experienced the constant buffering and pixelation that had been so annoying when using recast on this stick. So kudos to Amazon for taking the time to investigate and following up with me. I updated my rating to 3 stars. I wish I could give it 5 but I can't because in my opinion the product shouldn't have been released with a buggy "optimization" feature that causes more problems than it solves. That, and 2 months of aggravation. I'm just glad it's over. Thank you Amazon tech support team and boo to Amazon product development team for releasing the stick when it wasn't ready.

title: Great Functionality, but Poor Picture Quality
body: I'll preface my review in saying I bought the Fire Stick 4K specifically due to it being necessary to manage the Fire Recast (over-the-air channel tuner / DVR) I had also bought. It's functionality in managing the Recast is why I gave it 3 stars, as it does this part very well. (If you're buying it for the same reason, I touch on that part at the end of my review).However, if I'm judging the Fire Stick 4K as a media player (it's main function), unfortunately I would have to give it only 1 star. I'll start by saying I have a Samsung 4K TV with the necessary HDCP 2.2 HDMI input. I also have a quality WiFi router positioned very close to the Fire Stick with a solid 5GHz connection. For all other inputs (DIRECTV, Xbox One, Apple TV, etc.) I use roughly the same video settings with great results. However, the Fire Stick 4K had an instantly noticeable dark, soft image (no matter what the video source) and washed / flat colors. I worked for quite a while on tweaking the settings between the Fire Stick and my TV to achieve the best picture possible, but it's still far from good. The biggest impact I achieved was from turning the Fire Stick's default HDR setting from "Always HDR" to "Adaptive HDR" and simultaneously turning my Samsung TV to the "special viewing mode" of "HDR+", which is odd because it seems that reigning in the HDR on the Fire Stick while simultaneously enhancing HDR on my TV would be counter-productive, but for whatever reason it gave me the best results. Still though, this only fixed the picture enough to make the signal from my new HD antenna / Fire Recast combo tolerable enough to live with and avoid a cable / satellite bill, but far from ideal. I learned quickly that I definitely won't be using the Fire Stick to stream Netflix, Prime, Hulu, UHD movies I own digitally, or basically any streaming video beyond the OTA channels I need it for. Which is a real shame as this is, after all, intended as a 4K media streamer first and foremost. (I'll be using my Apple TV and Samsung SmartTV apps for those, as I did before.) Sad, because it actually has extremely smooth set-up, it functions excellently in terms of usability, and I love the remote. Everything about it is great except the one most important function: picture quality.On the positive side (and again the reason I ratcheted it up to 3 stars from 1), the main reason I needed the Fire Stick was to manage my new Fire Recast / HD antenna set-up and I'm happy to say it manages that function excellently. I love the aesthetics and functionality of the channel guide, and I was blown away that not only did it manage the OTA channels in the guide but it surprisingly added in my Philo live channels to the guide as well. This basically combines the major network channels I get from the antenna through the Recast (ABC, NBC, CBS, FOX, PBS) with the "cable" networks I get from Philo into one guide that feels very much (actually even better than) my channel guide experience from DIRECTV. And it just simply worked, no sweat and no troubleshooting needed. It just really is too bad the picture quality is so bad, otherwise this would be a perfect product and -- dare I say -- I would not even have needed my beloved Apple TV anymore. But as it stands, it's not even close to killing off my other media streamers.Bottom line: If you need the Fire Stick 4K paired with the Recast in order to cut the cord, and don't mind compromising video quality of those channels for the benefit of free TV w/ DVR from the Recast, then you'll get that from the Stick. But if you're looking for a media player to stream great quality video, this ain't it.

title: Nice, but major drawbacks. Covered in ads. Reboots needed often.
body: The only reason this has 3 stars is because I'm unaware of an inexpensive, dead-easy-to-use device that is far superior. Unless you want to custom build it, this or Roku is what you'll want.Good:- 4k content looks great- Play controls work great, I love the 30 sec ffwd and rewind functions for Prime videos, even better than Netflix etc- Simple remote, no line of sight required- Wireless integration with the Fire Recast DVR and channel guide works wellBad:- ADS ALL OVER THE PLACE. Half of my home screen is ads, you can't stop it, you have to navigate past it BEFORE you can start looking through your stuff, they mix in ad content with everything that I own/have access to. It's insulting and ridiculous. I pay for Prime, I paid for this device, I want to scroll through my stuff and watch it. Instead, it forces me to scroll past whatever they're advertising, and past their dedicated "sponsored content" feeds to get to more of it.- Locked/paid content is mixed in with my unlocked/free stuff. It's very annoying and often I can't tell whether I can watch something for free until I click it.- Seems like it needs a reboot to fix random problems (like stuttering) at least once a week- Reboot menu is buried in the settings menu under "My Fire TV"- Cannot reboot by using voice commands. Why??- LIke most devices, it fails to "unify" your viewing experience. I don't want to have to remember where a random movie is available to me. I want to talk to the remote and say "Toy Story 2" and have it bring up the watching menu. Half of the time it won't work - sometimes it can't return results from anything that's not Hulu, Netflix, or Prime. Sometimes it can't even find stuff in Prime. Sometimes it acts like it has never heard of such a movie and has no idea what you're talking about, even though it understood your words. So you have to start looking around between your stuff and their ads.- Voice search in general works terribly, much worse than my "dumber" XFinity cable remote. You'd think searching for the "NFL game" currently being streamed live on PrimeTV should show up in search results, especially since they're heavily advertising it. Nope! Gotta go wade through the ads on my home screen to find it.- Random wifi disconnects- You're left with about ~5GB of usable space I think? It goes fast.- The Prime Photos app, to use for screenshots, seems like it will eat up available space and let the performance of the firestick suffer and make reboots necessary. I'd really like Prime Photos as a nice screensaver but good luck if you've got 200 photos or more- Default browser is borderline worthless, Firefox doesn't work great on it either, it's difficult to go online to stream direct from sites.Long story short: I do still use this device because I can stream MOST of my content through it, and it integrates great with the Recast DVR. If I want something better, I'd have to spend time and money and effort building a little HTPC. But there are huge caveats.... a frustrating, messy experience with ads constantly getting in your way, poor voice search, and annoying reliability issues and bugs. This could all be fixed by Amazon, but it won't. And that's why I'm annoyed. They chose advertising to you over a good customer experience.

title: Nice 4K picture; some issues with streaming; one hard freeze for the whole stick so far.
body: Bought this because I could not load most streaming apps onto my LG OLED 4K TV using LG WebOS3. This Fire TV 4K Stick was easy to install onto an open HDMI slot on the back of my TV. Read the startup instructions BEFORE powering up. Sign-in and Registration was easy except it DID NOT automatically Register like it was supposed to as a direct Amazon purchase. Still had to login using my Amazon password, but minor departure from the instructions claims. Loaded ESPN+ without a problem and logged in without a problem. Loaded HBO MAX app which is unavailable for download on both my TVs at home. After going through the long log-in steps for HBO MAX (not the fault of Fire TV), I started watching available content...the main reason for purchase.Initially, the streaming ability is very good. Note you'll need more than 25mb per sec for 4K streaming. My WiFi is very high speed (up to 158mbps tested), more than enough for all my electronics. But, I have buffering issues with HBO MAX. After one or two episodes of an on demand TV show, it starts to freeze and buffer. This might be a function of the HBO MAX servers and not the Fire TV stick because I DON"T have the freezes and buffering on most apps. I note that Disney+ will buffer/freeze on my LG WebOS3...again, likely a function of the app server.Fire TV Stick 4K had a hard freeze during a start up yesterday. The remote would not command anything. Picture was frozen on opening Home page. I tested the batteries in the remote and they were good. Only way to Un-freeze was to disconnect the usb power cable from the Fire TV Stick, wait a few seconds and re-connect. Then the Stick rebooted to the Home page menu. All apps were still there and I didn't have to do any re-logins. Why did the Stick freeze on startup? Did it overheat? Was it always on and failed as a result?There doesn't seem to be anyway to confirm that you've turned off the Fire TV Stick. When I turn the power off from the remote, it turns off my TV. When I turn TV back on, Fire TV Stick is visible because I'm on that HDMI input. When I change my HDMI input to my primary HDMI ARC for cable viewing, I'm assuming the Fire TV Stick is still on. If I hit power off on the Fire TV Stick while on another input, my TV still turns off. I've found no way to go into the menu of Fire TV Stick or my LG OLED TV to make the Stick remote NOT control my TV power.Finally, I'm not thrilled with that fact that all my apps downloads on this Fire TV Stick are recorded as purchased on Amazon, even if the app is downloaded for free. I get a purchase order notification from Amazon that I've downloaded the app for $0. I've checked all privacy settings and set them for minimal or no info gathering by third parties. But since Amazon is the primary party, I'm guessing that they get to see everything, even if I've not set up Alexa voice. That's right, I don't use Alex voice function, so I can't comment on it.Overall, the Fire TV Stick 4K fills my original need to download and use streaming apps that my TV was incapable of performing. But, the Stick comes with some annoying flaws regarding power-off, hard freeze, and info gathering at Amazon.

title: Good Streaming, Remote Build Quality Almost Unacceptable
body: This is my third or fourth Fire Stick - I seem to purchase every version they release. Being the latest, this one obviously has more functionality, primarily in relation to the remote. I can't speak to the 4K ability of the Fire Stick itself as I'm not sure if anything I stream is actually in 4K, but video quality looks good to me and it works fairly smoothly for a portable streaming device. I would say it's slightly faster than the previous generation; not by a huge amount, but it's noticeable. The main reason I use Fire Stick devices is because I travel for work and often need to access hotel Wi-Fi networks. The Fire Stick has the ability to log in to these networks, but unfortunately with my previous Fire Sticks, it only works about 50% of the time. I'm hoping it's better with this latest model.The main reason I upgraded, however, was because of the new Volume/Power buttons on the remote. They do what they're supposed to do and seamlessly synced up with my 2015ish Sony Bravia TV. I now reach for the TV remote far less than I did previously (only when I need to change Input) and that was my primarily goal with upgrading. Where the remote falls short, and by quite a bit, is build quality. Being one of Amazon's more well-known products and at a price point of $50, I'm shocked at how bad it is. I initially thought I just received a bad unit (which I would be more forgiving of), but after reading other reviews, it seems that this is widespread issue with how the remote was manufactured. It does not feel nearly as well built as previous versions. There is noticeable flashing (or maybe imprecise molding) that makes the remote uncomfortable to handle, as well as noticeable gaps between the seams. It's not painful or anything, but when you hold a new piece of tech in your hand, you expect it to feel great. This remote does not. To add insult to injury, the battery door slides off ridiculously easily. I feel like this was an over-correction from previous generations as they were a pain to open the battery door. So yea, all-in-all, this feels like a remote I would expect to buy direct from China for $4.98.The reason I'm not returning the device is because, as mentioned above, it runs a little faster than previous versions and I'm hoping they have improved the network login functionality. I was also able to mostly address the shortcomings of the remote. For the uncomfortable feel, I used a leather edge beveler to remove a very, very small amount of plastic from the edges, which made them smoother to the touch. There's still a gap between the seams, but I would no longer call the remote uncomfortable (still not as nice as it should be). For the battery door sliding off, I stretched a small piece of electrical tape (pictured) at the top of the battery compartment. This makes the battery door a tighter fit and it no longer moves when holding the remote.I'm happier now that I've mostly mitigated what I disliked about the Fire Stick (remote), but this is a new product and I shouldn't have had to do those things. I think 3 stars is a fair rating, if not a touch generous. I might update this review after I've had a chance to put the network login functionality to the test.

title: Message for Amazon Fire Team: Support VUDU !!!!!!!!!!!!!!!!
body: I don't want to praise all the achievement of Fire TV 4K (you can find plenty of them here) and want to go straight to the issues that I'm experiencing. So here are the main cons of Fire TV 4K:1) Doesn't support Vudu. AND IT IS A BIG ISSUE!!!! There are tons of reviews here about the absence of Vudu, but still ZERO reaction from Amazon team that is responsible for Fire TVs. I don't even know how many more reviews should be posted pointing this very problem before they open their gates. Please Amazon comment how many more reviews should be posted before you seriously start thinking about it? And why on earth Vudu? After all, Vudu is supported by every competitor: Chromecast, Roku and even Apple TV! C'mon Amazon, don't be evil and greedy and add that Vudu app to your list of supported services. I KNOW that Vudu can be sideloaded (there are plenty tutorials on YouTube) BUT we need something that works out of the box. What is the point of having thousands of "mostly useless" supported apps when you do NOT support the app that is used by millions (According to Variety, there are 25 million Vudu users here in US).Yeah Amazon did great job by joining Movies Anywhere BUT it does NOT solve all the problems. My Vudu library has nearly 800 movies and my Movies Anywhere library only has about 500 movies (titles from Paramount, MGM, Lionsgate, HBO and some other small studios do not port to Movies Anywhere). Besides my Vudu library has a lot of TV shows (about more than 100) and none of them could be watched on Movies Anywhere or Amazon library (because there are no ways to synchronize shows across providers). The only way to watch TV shows on Vudu is to have the app of Fire TV.2) Google Play Movies is NOT supported. I know that I can watch my Google Play purchases on YouTube app. But the organization of titles is very neat and logical on Google Movies, please support this app.3) Xfinity is NOT supported. My internet/TV provider is Xfinity and every other streaming device supports it except Amazon! So sad.4) The organization of movies/TV shows on my Amazon Library is a JOKE! I always ignore and skip that tab on my Fire TV. I bet even Amazon employees prefer the interface of other 3rd party apps, like Movies Anywhere or Apple TV+, where movies/shows are presented in more pleasant and user friendly way (with all sorting options based on purchase date, alphabetically, etc.). I don't understand why the interface and presentation of titles in Amazon library is such a mess. My recommendation is: Have self respect Amazon for your own child and spent some time and organize that mess.5) A lot of useless ads/information/promotion on the landing home page. Please copy the interface of Roku. I just want to see my installed apps and channels ONLY. I don't want someone to push the content. Please have an option to disable showing recommended media/apps and other BS.I know Amazon will probably ignore this review BUT if they don't I will adjust my rating and will update my review if I see any improvements.

title: Good, but might not do what you think it should
body: ||| UPDATE ||| This might not work for youWhereas the Fire TV Stick is still as described below, I recently discovered a serious flaw that might influence whether or not you buy one. I have a spare computer monitor that I'd like to put in my kitchen, plug in the Fire TV Stick, and watch gourmet cooking shows while I burn the toast. Seems simple, right? Connecting the Stick to the monitor is no problem at all, and it works just fine...EXCEPT......the latest Fire TV software appears to implement copy protection (HDCP) the moment you turn on the Fire Stick; formerly, it waited until you were watching protected content in HD before it did so. Because my monitor doesn't support HDCP (it's a few years old) the Fire Stick thinks it's an old TV that I watch while illegally copying "Mission Impossible 5: This Time He's Taller" and promptly blacks-out the screen. I can watch nothing on the monitor setup - it makes no difference whether or not it's copy-protected content. This seems like a serious issue, especially as it also will impact anyone with an older (but still perfectly good) TV.So to summarize: it's technically quite easy to connect your Amazon Fire TV Stick to a computer monitor or an older TV - but if you do so, Amazon won't let you watch ANYTHING. Nothing at all. If I may craft a tortured analogy here: It's rather like if you bought a brand new rug for your living room, but the rug manufacturer won't let you walk on it EVER because your shoes MIGHT be dirty. The fact that you never wear shoes matters not one little bit.||| original review |||I upgraded to this from my older, more basic Fire TV Stick. The Alexa voice control is very good, and gets it right almost all the time (which, frankly, is on par with Cortana and Siri). Having a volume control and mute button on the Fire TV Stick remote control is very useful indeed; I seldom now use the TV remote, if at all. I don't need the 4K capability (and I suspect streaming in 4K would eat my network's monthly data allowance within hours!) but I guess the larger storage capacity and faster processors are a plus.However, this Fire TV Stick crashes more frequently than the old one. Most often the sound just stops working, and the only fix is to re-boot the Stick by unplugging then re-plugging its power supply. I'd guess this happens about three times a week, sometimes more. Not a big issue, but somewhat annoying. A quick scan of the interweb shows that this is a common problem for people, so hopefully Amazon will be able to fix this with a firmware update.So all-in-all this is better than the old Fire TV Stick, and I'd definitely buy another one. I think this updated version actually cost me less than the old one, so that's progress, right? As more and more of us ditch the overpriced, customer-hating behemoth cable companies, devices such as this will become more and more essential, and then (I hope) we'll see some truly amazing advances.

title: Very cluttered, locked down, and bullish device.
body: Summary:Pros:Plays 4K video without stutter.Fast menu navigation.Good remote with TV/Receiver/Device control.Volume leveler.Supports Web Browsers.Cons:Cluttered Menu with Ads.Sometimes remote is unresponsive.Stick format might cause interference with TV.Doesn't have a LAN port.Alexa sometimes doesn't understand certain search queries.No NTFS support if attaching USB hard drives via USB adapters.Forced credit card info if you want to download free apps.Can't disable screensaver, resulting in resume issue.Review:Let me first say that as a media streaming device, it performs nicely. It can play 4K videos video without issues. The menu and speed navigation is responsive most of the time. I appreciate the "volume leveler" setting for loud volume changes when watching content. I like that you can use Firefox browser, with built-in mouse control, to surf the internet with ease. The Firefox browser works well with the remote, including videos. The remote can control an old Pioneer stereo receiver from 1992, and probably many more devices besides just a TV set.This bring me to the negatives of this device. The menu is too cluttered. It doesn't seem suitable for someone who is older and wants things to be simple to use. The remote sometimes does not respond, ranging from 5 to 20 seconds wait time. The stick itself caused WiFi interference to my internal TV speakers from being too close to the back of the set. I had to by a 6 foot extended HDMI cable to fix the issue. This unit should have been a box unit and should have come with a six foot HDMI cable, including a LAN port. Sorry, but there is a fine line between cutting costs and making stupid decisions that limits its' usage. The voice control sometime decides it doesn't want to listen and gives an error to try later. It doesn't always understand what I'm asking when searching for specific content. The device requires you to register a valid account to use the device. It is "required" to have a registered address AND credit card info to download the free apps. Yes, you read that correctly. You HAVE to have a credit card registered with Amazon to download free apps in the Amazon store. The screen saver cannot be turned off. If you pause a video, go way for 10 minutes, come back, hit any button on the remote, It will take you back to the home screen instead of where you left off.I personally would not buy this again unless it is $20.00 or less. It is too locked down and too much of a forced ecosystem to trap you into spending more money. I remember installing a Roku a few years back, and it was much nicer/cleaner navigation.I would personally would have preferred a unit with USB ports, Ethernet, with NTFS support for hard drives. I might look into an Android TV box in the future. One that isn't locked down.

title: Disappointed with it.
body: I was using a Tivo Bolt with their apps on a Samsung 7series 1080p HDTV. (before 4k) The Netflix, hulu, prime, youtube apps all worked quite quickly although the youtube app would always be slightly zoomed in. Could never get that fixed. I'm dropping Tivo service so that means I can't even use the apps on the device. Bought the Firestick to replace all the streaming apps.Everything installed quickly and at first I thought it was a good upgrade. Really hated all of the sponsored content that takes up the screen. Found a way to disable that but it still shows a few slides at the top. Just give me a menu of my apps please. Speaking of which I'll go through the apps:Netflix, works just fine. No issues, layout is the same as the Tivo.Hulu, better than Tivo, allows switching profiles but it does have a different layout and sometimes hard to find what you want to play or continue playing.Prime, actually worse that Tivo. Not sure how they could screw up their own app on their own device but I have a hard time finding content now. Way too much sponsored stuff. Plus I noticed stuttering when I was watching The Grand Tour. After some digging I found the HDR setting. It was set to adaptive. My tv doesn't have HDR and there is no "Turn HDR off" setting. Switching to HDR active setting did fix the problem. Looks like it will only activate if your tv is HDR capable. Either way, poor user design on that one.Youtube, horrible. Forces you to go through a browser which means when you click the youtube icon in the menu it launches the browser, then you need to click youtube again, then it brings up the profile selector every time. Then you can finally view videos, all of which were zoomed in. Found a display setting to manually adjust the zoom by 2% so the videos are proper full screen. Something I couldn't fix on the Tivo.DLNA server. I have videos loaded on my NAS box (synology) and downloaded the DS Video app. It finds the videos but is super slow and has a very limited supply of codecs. I have a lot of .avi files and none would play. Switch over to my other samsung 4k tv with smart apps and it plays everything from my NAS box just fine and the browsing is much quicker. I'll try a Plex server next but right now it is a big thumbs down.Reliability. I've had it for 2 days and I am having to reboot the device a couple times a day. It just locks up or the screen is black. I haven't unplugged my chromecast in 2 years for a comparison. Doesn't bold well.Alexa, works well with my already established alexa powered home. I can use the remote to control my Hue lights and I like being able to fast forward a specified amount by voice. Launching apps is hit or miss. Sometimes I ask for a tv title and it suddenly starts playing a song with the same name.Just glad I got this on sale for $35. Not sure if I'll keep it.

title: I would like it a lot if it were not for the bugs
body: I got one of these in March 2019. I mainly use it to link to my streaming service Sony PlayStation Vue.I previously had older versions of the Fire Stick. Those were pretty much ok but were kind of slow.One problem I ran into with the Fire Stick 4k was that when I am running PlayStation Vue, the TV guide has a faulty "clock". Programs showed up on the guide as being 7 hours later than they actually are. For example to view a program during the noon hour, which it is now, I would have to scroll the TV guide to 7pm.Then the other day I was troubleshooting my audio/video setup and reran the initialization (setup?) procedure on the Fire Stick 4k. And it sat there for hours with the three cycling dots and the background music. So I did a chat with amazon tech support and they weren't able to solve it so they sent me a new Fire Stick 4k. And I just plugged it in and ran the setup, and it's working.And lo and behold the TV guide in PlayStation Vue now shows up correct! Hallalujah, everything now appears to be working perfectly.I also have a Roku which I used while waiting for the new Fire Stick, but given a choice between the two, I find the Fire Stick more convenient to use than the Roku. For one thing, I have my video/audio eqt behind me, since I use a projector. On the Roku that means I have to turn the remote to face backwards in order for it to work. But the Fire Stick remotes work without having to point them at the Fire Stick. How do they do that? I don't know, but in a case like mine it is a lot more convenient.Another thing I like about the Fire Stick is the TV guide in PlayStation Vue. It has the programming in each channel in a column rather than a row. On Roku each channel's programming is in a row.The format on the guide I get with the Fire Stick I find more intuitive and easier to use. Also with the Fire Stick the Vue TV guide has a line showing the current time, whereas the Roku does not do that.This is worth noting, that not only do different streaming devices have different user interfaces, they even can have different user interfaces within streaming applications.Update 14Sep2019:I have two chronic problems with my fire stick 4k. First of all, it crashes on average about 2-3 times a day. I can get it working again by doing a restart.Second problem is when I am running Playstation Vue on the fire stick, when I am watching hour long programs they freeze up at the 55 minute mark and I need to then go to the tv guide, click on the program, and click on "resume".By analogy with the previous problem I had regarding the "clock" on the Playstation Vue tv schedule, which I believe the evidence clearly showed was caused by the Fire Stick, I believe the "55 minute" problem is almost certainly also a bug with the Fire Stick.

title: Good value. Works great except BritBox.
body: My first experience with FireTV was an early iteration that I picked up for a very good price on a Prime Day a few years back. Its performance was always very sluggish, its picture quality was lacking, and its user interface was clumsy. I never really used it much.I had heard from several friends that they really liked their new 4K FireTV sticks, so I thought it might be worth giving FireTV another try. Things had to have improved over the years, hadn’t they?I must say that the 4K FireTV Stick is a great value. At the sale price of $24.99 it’s definitely the least expensive way to enter the 4K streaming market. I wanted to appreciate its quality as well as its value, but I encountered a stumbling block in that regard — BritBox.When I first fired up the unit (pun intended), everything worked perfectly. I was stunned at the picture quality and the ease of setup. The device had been pre-registered to my Prime account and detected my WiFi automatically. It updated itself to the latest software version and within minutes I was on my way. I did have to sign in to all my apps again (Netflix, Acorn TV, BritBox, Disney+, etc), but once that was accomplished, I was streaming away with great results. At that point I thought I had a 5-star device...The next day I tried watching BritBox again. This time the app hung when I tried to start it up. I switched to my Roku 4K box and had no problems watching BritBox. I went back to the 4K FireTV and BritBox hung again.I tried removing and reinstalling the app. Then I tried forcing the app closed and was eventually able to get into BritBox again, but now the video display was very choppy and unnatural. I checked all the other apps and the picture on everything else was fine, but BritBox was unwatchable.I connected with the support team for FireTV and after trying several troubleshooting steps, their conclusion was that my FireTV was too far from my router. But why would this only affect BritBox? They didn’t have a good answer for that.My next step was to add an Ethernet adapter to my FireTV in order to alleviate any WiFi signal problems. The first Ethernet adapter was DOA.
