The second Ethernet adapter worked properly, but still did not resolve the BritBox video choppiness.Now I’ve wasted lots of time and an additional $15 for the Ethernet adapter and still can’t watch BritBox on my FireTV.If you don’t have BritBox, I guess the 4K FireTV stick might be a good product for you. For me, I’ll use my Roku 4K device and forget about the FireTV. Besides, Roku also supports the PeacockTV app which FireTV does not. And the Roku Ultra has built-in Ethernet.Sorry FireTV, I really wanted to like you this time...

title: Lots of Annoyances, But It Functions Fine I Guess
body: I've never owned one of these myself, so this was a first experience for me. I bought this as a gift for a non-tech-savvy person. They've got prime, but never had a streaming device to make use of prime video. I was hoping for something to get them easy access to the content that comes with their prime membership. Some of the issues I had related to the overall experience, while others related to individual apps. Since this whole thing is a package deal, I see no reason to separate the two. This is the 'Amazon FireTV stick' experience, after all. Some of the disappointing stuff that I ran into while trying to set it up over Christmas...- Most of the content that is being dangled in front of you is behind paywalls for the various channels. Seems like there are a lot, and they all have their own subscriptions. That's a lot to keep up with.- There was a 'firefox' app, but no youtube app, which I thought was a pretty big oversight as far as free content goes. I may have missed it though.- In the 'amazon prime video app', mixed in with the 'prime' channels are 'fire tv' channels, which are not included with prime. They are paid. The font is so small that it's hard to see what's included and what isn't without clicking on it first.- On most videos, they have a 'customers also watched' section. Most of the videos under there are not included with prime and are suggested to rent/buy.- The search function is well hidden. You have to back out to the main page and pick a mostly-hidden magnifying glass icon at the top left to search. Most search results are paid content.- There's no easy way to filter out the free (as in, included with prime) vs paid content in general. This is very irritating to me, as I don't like constantly being asked for money.- Before every episode, every movie, they play an ad for some junk amazon show. They don't announce that it's an ad, so it just seems like you clicked on the wrong thing for a while.It's basically set up to disappoint and frustrate the user. I have a pretty good hunch this device won't get used much. I use the XBone for my streaming device, and it is much easier to navigate. I was considering buying one for myself if the experience was good, but now that I've set one up for someone else, I'd have to pass on this.The product itself, I mean it works. Out of the box, it just connects to your wifi and 'goes', so if you don't mind the never-ending paywalls, subscriptions, and rent/buy microtransaction experience, go for it. I think (hope, really) that there are better streaming devices out there though.

title: UPDATED --- Great streaming option! (Best?)
body: UPDATE after 1.5 years of use:Well after a year and a half of reliable use, my fire stick has stopped working. Not sure what the problem is - it just stopped responding, and now won't display on the tv at all. Tried 2 tvs and several different HDMI ports, no picture no nothing.Still giving it 3 stars as it worked reliably for over a year, and the price is right - but not very durable. Rather than replace it with another fire stick, I've ordered an Apple TV 4K. Yes it costs more - but most people I know have been using the same Apple TV device for years without issue - and as someone already invested in the apple ecosystem, it just makes sense.PLUS: a couple major omissions from the fire stick I've noticed over time made the Apple TV a more enticing option. Namely, Amazon/Fire TV doesn't have HBO MAX or the Spectrum TV app, two big omissions. And of course Airplay is not native to Amazon, another big star in Apple TV's corner.I don't regret the purchase; it is a good product for its price point but it has its limitations.-----I've been holding out on getting a dedicated streamer for years because I had been using my ps4 for that purpose. But my ps4 is getting long in the tooth and is breathing very heavy these days, so I figured it was time to get a 2nd device for streaming to hopefully extend the ps4's lifespan until the ps5 comes out! Would hate to have to replace it right before the new one is coming...So I've also been an Apple fanboy for years - iMac, iPhone, iPad, Apple Watch, etc...logically my first choice in this dept was the Apple TV. Now, normally I don't mind paying for Apple products, but in this category, after doing some research, I couldn't justify spending an EXTRA $150 when AppleTV didn't really offer anything other than an Apple-driven experience...ALL of the features on the Apple TV are available on the Fire Stick, except native Airplay. Except I can buy 4 Fire Sticks for the price of one Apple TV.For $40 (sale!) I got a 4k capable streamer that has all the channels and services you could wants, smooth OS, can use my Apple Music as the default music app, and can mirror my iOS devices (paid $3 for an app and it works flawlessly so far). The remote is great too, better than the Apple one from what I gather; Alexa works very well, and the fact that I can use the remote to control my tv and soundbar is just sweet. Couldn't go and spend $180 on the Apple version after doing some research and realizing they're almost the same feature-wise.Should've bought one sooner!

title: decent buy for the money but the remote and stability issues keep it from greatness
body: This is fine - it does what it does relatively well. I can stream most stuff at 4k on my tv, which is what I got it for. However there are times when things just either lock up in the middle of a show (rare) or there will be some sort of error in an individual app. Sometimes the only way to fix this is to hard reset the fire tv by unplugging power and plugging it back in. Not a huge deal, but kind of annoying especially in the middle of a show. Hopefully stability improves in further iterations.The included remote is pretty chintzy. It has this overlarge battery cover that will absolutely slide off if you grab the thing wrong. Also the remote is a smol hidey boi so you're going to lose it in your couch pretty much every day. This is not the fault of the manufacturer. However something that is their fault is a key thing missing from the remote: a TV INPUT BUTTON. You need to allow us to change television inputs with it so that we can switch to gaming consoles or whatever else might be plugged in to the tv. Add this to the fact that the remote is about as cheaply made as you can get, and I get the feeling I'll be needing to replace it soon when it dies or inevitably gets lost in the shadow realm. Thankfully replacements are cheap and relatively plentiful.The experience itself is pretty decent. They do a good job of aggregating the different streaming services as well as utilizing some of the bigger streamers' API to provide nice pages of "next up on $service" content. This is nice but you still need to dive into the individual apps to find the good stuff. I get that Amazon is trying to do a "one UX to rule them all" kind of experiment here and the results are passable, so I'm not going to be too harsh on it. As far as streaming service aggregators out there I still consider these early days. Yes, the UI does seem to give priority to Prime Video content, but I expected as much and to expect otherwise I don't think is realistic. That said, I don't think I need to add here that if you do not have prime video, do not bother with this as there are other aggregators available. For that matter, use a console.Overall it's fine and for $50 or whatever it made my "not very smart" vizio into a bit of a better set. That said, my next television might very well have the integrated amazon stuff so I can skip the middleman here. I am not a huge fan of how the thing hangs off the back of the set like a dongle - you're probably going to need to do some light cable management here if you've got your tv mounted on the wall.

title: Fascist app environment, but useful otherwise
body: Let me tell you, I was super excited for the launch of the new FireTV Stick with its leading edge technology and processing power. Well, it’s got that but there’s a couple bug, game breaking problems I have with this thing.1) The Amazon platform is basically retooled Android, which is a crap interface. It’s cluttered, redundant, and Amazon being Amazon wants you to use all their services over everyone else’s so not only does their menu get in the way but the top third of the home screen is promotions for whatever they want to push.2) Also because it’s Amazon they want you to use THEIR streaming services to rent movies so much so that there is no Vudu or Google Play app available to install. If Prime Video had a decent selection of rentals (it doesn’t) or a halfway usable interface (it doesn’t) I would have less of a problem.3) Our FireTV Stick has issues with flickering and sound dropout. Tech support immediately supplied a series of less than helpful troubleshooting tips. “Did you try a different TV? Did you try a different HDMI port?” Despite my Roku working just fine. I elected to return this since I was already fed up with the closed interface and terrible menus.Slightly less of an issue but still annoying is the inability to completely turn off HDR. Its either “always on” or “adaptive” meaning the content dictates HDR which is a problem especially with Netflix (which is calibrated too dark on some TVs)For now I’m happier with my Roku Stick despite less robust HDR support.Edit: updated my review for the following:- Amazon reached out immediately after my review to get additional feedback and to offer more support. That actually means something these days. I’ll continue to monitor for problems- I upgraded to a new TV and this makes the HDR issue better since it actually looks good on this TV. Problem still stands of not being able to disable HDR entirely if needed- The smart controls feature is nice. I can use Alexa to change TV inputs now.- Less flickering on new tv but still have sound dropoutsHowever my issue with not having a way to watch VUDU/UV movies is a huge issue. On top is that, MoviesAnywhere doesn’t tell you which movies were transferred over in 4K. So while the titles may sync, in cases of certain films (eg Deadpool 2) you only get the HD version and not the UHD/HDR one you paid for. Until this is fixed I’ll leave my review at 3 stars.

title: Needs fix for Dolby Vision/Atmos content support and more built in memory for storage and RAM...
body: Performance and features and especially the remote of the Fire TV Stick 4k is great and a huge improvement over previous versions but, I really wish it has more than 8 GB's (only 5.34 GB's are usable!) memory or even allow you to adopt external storage so you can expand capacity. Yes I am aware you can do workarounds by using an OTG cable and manually moving files around but that doesn't really work because you can't change the save location of apps. I only have a few tv viewing apps installed and my storage is already full.Review update as of December 2019 (dropped to 1 star).I purchased a new LG SM9000PUA 2019 TV (updated to newest software version) with Dolby Vision and Atmos support. I was hoping to experience Dolby Vision/Atmos content on the Fire TV 4k but was disappointed to find out the Fire TV 4k is "only" outputting UHD.I tried connecting the Fire TV 4k directly to the TV and also to the soundbar (also has Dolby Vision/Atmos support). Both connections produced the same results. I have read in the Amazon device forums other TV brands having the same issue so I don't think it is isolated to a compatibility issue with the TV or LG.I did specifically upgrade my Fire TV sticks to the newer version for Dolby support as an advertised "feature" of the new Fire TV 4K.The settings on my Fire TV 4k are:Resolution: Auto (up to 4k) - I have read that Dolby Vision works on 1080p but what's the point at that resolution?Color Depth: Up to 12 bits (I've also tried the default at 8 bits)Color Format: AutoDynamic Range: Adaptive (tried always HDR)As a side note, my Apple TV pushes full Dolby Vision and Atmos content simultaneously through my TV and soundbar.I really hate to give a product, especially this Fire TV 4K stick, a 1 star but, I don't feel it is living up to its advertised feature set. I hope Amazon provides an update to resolve this issue and I will update the review accordingly. Sorry Amazon for the 1 star, I really do still like your products and recommend it, but for me and my use case it just isn't working.[Update 12/2019]I found a movie "Suspiria" that outputs both Dolby Vision and Atmos. Simultaneous Dolby Vision and Atmos does work so it is only the lack of content and app support that is the issue. As a test, I installed Disney+ and found Dolby Vision present but not Atmos. Adjusted my rating to 3 stars.

title: Better than the one it replaced.  With a caveat.
body: This unit replaces a 3-year-old Fire TV box, the old style one. It has a noticeably faster processor and a slightly better picture, and is unseen behind the TV and therefore less obtrusive. It also has more apps, some that the other unit doesn't offer. (I contacted Amazon and was told some of the newer apps are simply not compatible with the older electronics in the ancient-by-comparison Box unit.) And having "4K" (actually a misnomer, what companies call 4K is not QUITE 4K) capability will be a plus in the near future when I replace the TV which this stick now services.One nice feature is that the power button on the remote can turn on the TV; I can even turn on the TV by telling Alexa to do it! And it's nice to have volume control on the remote, especially since the native volume of the Fire TV does not match that of my settop box (neither did the old Fire TV Box, it was way louder and not itself adjustable).My only reservation: While it has more built-in memory than the old Box, the Box has a slot for a card to augment the memory, while I don't see one on this unit. After downloading all of my TV-related apps AND all of the game apps that my grandsons like to play when they come over, there really isn't all that much storage space left. Fortunately, I don't see any need for more TV apps in the foreseeable future, and if my grandsons want more games they will just have to delete the ones they no longer use.NOTE: I originally rated this unit as 4 stars. Unfortunately, recent Amazon events have forced me to downgrade it to 3. Amazon Fire TV always used to have a screen saver consisting of beautiful pictures being scrolled. A short while ago, some ads started creeping in between the pictures. I inquired and Amazon said there was nothing I could do to remove them, essentially that I was stuck with them and had to watch them if I used the screen saver. Very recently, however, ALL the pictures have been replaced by ads on the screen saver, and so using it forces us to watch nothing but ads. This is a totally obnoxious act on the part of Amazon. We pay our money for a product and then Amazon apparently takes control of it and alters its operation to fit its needs. I refuse to ever use the screen saver again; if I need such a feature, I will simply switch to another (unused) input on my TV. Better to watch "snow" or a blank screen than to be subjected to this.

title: Great--good color/contrast with flat-panel TV. Poor contrast with 1080p projector. Unwatchable.
body: I appreciate the video streaming features of this device with my 1080p flat-panel TVs--I have chosen not to purchase UHD-TV, since the differences are barely noticeable at normal viewing distances. I bought this in the effort to future-proof our viewing, meaning I don't know how long reasonable-quality 1080p TVs will remain available and affordable now that UHD is becoming popular.Having made comparisons between the two (HDTV and UHD-TV) I decided my existing HDTVs are fine for the present--as a hobbyist artist/painter I do appreciate the fine texture of UHD displays, but find that without getting closer than most of us actually do when viewing TV, the UHD doesn't offer significantly more in the overall TV-watching experience than my current HDTVs. The advantage might come when viewing in projecting a 12-foot diagonal (or larger) image on a screen, as in a theater. Pixels can be seen if viewing from two or three feet away when viewing the 1080p projected image. (Incidentally, the digital projectors at the multi-screen cinema in our town are all 1080p, and no one complains at the quality!) As most TV and projection experts who have compared discernible image quality differences in the two resolutions have said, the biggest factor in the perception of image quality is related to image CONTRAST.My year-old Benq 1080p projector has very good contrast with our home-theater PC, and satellite TV, but quite poor contrast with this device. I have yet to feel comfortable with the price/features/quality of 4K/UHD projectors, so I can't predict how the 4K Fire Stick will perform in UHD projection. I can only say that with my otherwise excellent Benq HT2050A projector the contrast is unacceptable, displaying an overall gray "fog" with terrible black levels. Yet when I switch our Yamaha HTPC receiver to our PC's HDMI output, the contrast on Amazon Prime, Netflix, PBS, etc. is excellent, and DirecTV's satellite content is fine also.However contrast is fine as long as you connect the 4K Fire Stick with flat-panel TVs.My feelings about the 4K Fire Stick: as long as you use a flat-panel TV, you'll probably be happy with it. But if you use a projector, borrow someone's 4K Fire Stick and try it before purchasing.I gave up on the device for projection use, and put it on the TV in our kitchen. It's nice for catching up on TV and news content while cooking or sitting at the dinner table.

title: TRUST ISSUE: Storage space advertised is not Storage space delivered
body: I’ve purchased the Newer “2nd Gen. Fire TV stick 4k” after reviewing specs which claimed 8 Gigs of Storage space (SSPC) on your own web site; and only received one with 5.28 Gigs, with only 3.68 gigs available. I’m no math wiz but doing simple math says that I am cheated out of better than 50% of what was advertised and what I thought I was under good faith purchasing. This is a trust issue to join several I’ve had with you since I’ve been a member and purchasing customer of Amazon; which in fact has been better than 2 years. I would like for you to either send me the 8 gigabytes Fire TV stick 4k or return $10.00 of the $42 + change I spent on it and have you put the true specs on your site. Had I known I would get less than what I expected and paid for, I would have opted to seek another service and TV to purchase as the more apps you add to the thing, the harder it is for it to run, i.e. it runs very slow, freezes and a lot of times changes from a selected program to an altogether unrelated one shortly after a freeze. Please correct this. I think this is fair. Now I’m very Leary of purchasing a TV from you; had Laptops and TV’s in mind; but if I can’t trust they’ll be legitimately operative, efficiently and effectively; I’ll have to stick with Sony, Sharp and Vizio products, and not purchase them through you. Please let me know soon; and that customer rep I spoke with over the phone, and that supervisor should know that if there were actually 8 gigs on the thing, it wouldn’t say there are “3.68 GB of 5.28 GB available” it would show 8 minus the used GB’s; so condescendingly talking to a customer with unrealistic math equations doesn’t negate the actual mathematics facts. But they were polite and that’s something I suppose; but give me what you advertised and I thought I was paying for…..now; as I’d like to purchase a newer model TV now. I’ll need to know if I am ending our relationship in this forum (TV/internet Entertainment); oh. and I understand mistakes are made. I’ve made a few myself; which is why I’m so thorough at paying attention to details. By-the-way, I record all correspondences from everywhere I spend my money; just a legal disclaimer in case anyone wants to challenge me legally where I'm being the victim of fraud. Thank You Amazon The 3 Rating is because My last one worked well for two years; until storage space was almost all used up; that's why the upgrade.

title: A lot of bang for the buck with one major fault
body: I will start by saying that indeed the new stick offers a lot of bang for the buck. It was nice that volume controls, mute and Power were added to the remote. The 4K and HDR abilities are awesome for a device that cost so little. My Fire TV box has been awesome from day one. Plenty fast, great picture, easy to navigate and well worth the cost. This new stick however, while offering so much takes a major turn when it comes to picture quality. This might sound unusual to some people but I actually purchased the device to use with my Roku 4K HDR TV. The TV itself has a beautiful picture but when it comes to the Roku part and my streaming TV service provider, DirecTV now, less attention apparently has been paid to getting the DirecTV Now app working as well for a Roku TV as it does for most people with a stand-alone Roku. My Fire TV box is Flawless with my TV service. The inability to change channels quickly by simply holding down the scroll button it's where the Roku Falls flat for people who need to change channels with in particular apps like so many streaming apps today. The Roku is also slow to pull up the program guide and change channels. This is where the new Fire TV stick comes in. It indeed shines brightly when it comes to changing channels fast and the program guides pulling up immediately. It does take longer for the picture to stabilize at a higher quality with the stick compared to my TV box. The quality is very off though. there are quite a few options available to fine-tune things within the new Fire TV stick and I've played around with all of the settings but none will allow the picture quality to shine the way it does using the TV normally or on my TV which uses the Fire TV box. I have an old Fire TV stick in another room which handles the menus, channel changes and picture quality so much better. Most people might be able to live with their picture looking more like it is a grainy 720 resolution. There is such a drastic difference though between the regular TV picture quality through the built-in Roku app vs this new Fire TV stick. If someone has found a workaround or knows if there is an issue that is being addressed, I would love to hear about it. Outside of that one major complaint, this is definitely a great device for the money. If Amazon and Google ever come to terms with YouTube, it will be even better having the regular YouTube app back again.

title: Good video and audio quality, but suboptimal interface and durability
body: We have had two Fire TV Stick 4K for 7-8 months. We like the video and audio quality, we enjoy X-ray, and the ability to control associated devices (TV, sound bar) with a single remote. TV control (Sony and Panasonic) has been flawless, but the control of a Panasonic Sound bar is hit or miss in terms of its ability to turn on/off (we often end up having to use the sound bar remote to turn it on). Volume control of the sound bar is fine. Tweaking the settings has not improved the reliability of the on/off feature.The user interface is OK. It is however far less intuitive than the Apple TV interface. It requires more clicks to access the same content and it is a lot less clean. Also, the existence of multiple ways to access the same services is confusing. For example you can subscribe to Showtime's streaming service through Prime Video Channels or through the Showtime streaming app, but they are not linked to each other. If you subscribe to Showtime through Prime Video Channels you have to access the shows though the Amazon Prime Video app. There may be a way to use the Prime Video Channel subscription to watch showtime on the showtime app, but I have not found it. On Apple TV this is more or less seamless. You can subscribe to Showtime through iTunes, but then you use the Showtime app for streaming, as if you had subscribed on the showtime site. It is not a huge difference, but just less convenient watching and tracking your subscriptions on the Fire TV Stick 4K.Finally, hardware reliability has been disappointing. The remote on one of the Fire TV Stick 4K stopped working 7 months after purchase. Unfortunately, we have to pay for a new remove almost as much as we paid for the device (stick + remote) in the first place (the Fire TV Stick 4K was bought during black friday). The remote on the second Fire TV Stick 4K is still working, but that unit is used a lot less. Of course a sample of 2 is very small, and maybe we were just unlucky. On the other hand, I have many other remotes for electronic devices that have lasted or years without failure (including an Apple TV remote).Overall we are debating whether to stay with Amazon devices or switch back to Apple. The main draw to stay with Amazon is the price, especially if you want to have multiple devices. If the price for the devices was similar we would probably switch to Apple.

title: Great for under fifty dollars... If you don't really care about 4k HDR and Atmos content.
body: I waited to start season 3 of Man in the High Castle JUST so I could watch this season in 4k HDR with Atmos. And the only way to get that trifecta is with this device. I'm sad to report that so far the only thing that seems to stream in 4k HDR with Atmos is Jack Ryan. The ONLY thing. Even that has been hit and miss. Sometimes its' got all three, sometimes it's just 1080p HDR with Atmos. Sometimes it's 1080p no hdr and no atmos. The Netflix app doesn't seem to support HDR or Atmos at all. No matter what title.The diagnostic shows my signal is 'Very Good' over my 5Ghz home wifi. I'm getting stellar 4k HDR Atmos content on my Apple TV 4k. It sit's right next to my Fire TV Stick 4k. I've rebooted, updated, restarted, etc. I've worked with two nice associates over the phone about the issue. So far the results are still the same. The last rep I spoke with said that they had forwarded my issue to 'the developers'. I was told I should expect a call back today. We'll see how that goes.Other than that, the interface seems pretty quick and intuitive. The remote is a god send compared to the Apple TV 4K's excuse for a remote... AND it's. just. $50.As of now though, I'm still waiting to watch season 3 of Man in the High Castle... And as of now just two stars, until this device can deliver the 4k HDR Atmos content it promised.UPDATE:I spoke with two more associates. One confirmed Man in the High Castle is at least in UHD. I've since given up on the notion. On my Apple TV 4K, I get Jack Ryan in UHD, HDR and Atmos. Man in the High Castle, still just HD. I'm upgrading my rating to 3 stars.Again, the interface is pretty intuitive, and it's responsive enough. Integration with Alexa is kinda neat. Only three stars though due to the lack of UHD HDR Atmos support in the Netflix app, as well as massive mess in trying to find 4k content in amazon prime's offerings... Let alone HDR and Atmos.I see a lot of 5 star reviews here. None that I've read seem to associate that with the higher end formats of 4k HDR and Atmos. So I suspect that the bulk of these users either don't have the tech to get these features, or just don't care. If this is you, then you'll likely love this device at this price point. If you do want these higher end formats, you're probably better off with a different device.

title: It's quick and yet it fails on one important part- no bass output!
body: Well designed and downloads apps very quickly. Advances in fast forward much better than my Roku Premiere +.I'm pleased.-This is an update: The Fire TV Stick has great features, yet I realized that I could not get it to talk to my sub woofers.I have two subwoofer one is a Sunfire which was overhauled and the other is a passive VMPS subwoofer connected to an outside DAYTON AUDIO subwoofer amplifier. My second 4K Fire Stick is in the bedroom and (there I don't care about extra bass).The audio signal which is optical from my main SAMSUNG TV is sent to a Digital to Audio Converter. There the signal connects to the two Subwoofers.Here is the following-- I'm on an antenna which is used frequently and I use Directv for my main programs.The Fire TV Stick will not send any subwoofer information to the optical output on the TV.So I have a great bass on over the air antenna and Directv ( the Directv was soon to be replaced with streaming).I've also reconnected an older Apple Air Play which has limited selections and no 4K... but yet it gives the bass I want.So I finally contacted AMAZON support and asked about my issue. The lady took all my information down and located my name. Then she asked for a few minutes of time to talk to her superior. About 5 minutes later she reiterated the questions and asked for more time. At that time I asked whether a new unit would solve my issue-- for it was only $24.95 Prime day price. So I was placed on hold again. After about 5-6 minutes she was about to come back and suddenly I heard that loud disconnecting dial tone. She had my email and phone number and she never attempted to contact me again.The whole point is that streaming through the 4K Fire TV stick was to be my new way of watching TV but without a complete frequency output it's a waste for me.I know I'm probably over reacting here, but if that's the way they treat people I'm now ready to walk away from AMAZON----completely. No ALEXA, nothing! I've been a member since 2006 and their treatment is just rotten. I'm just unimportant!Another Update- After I received ''no'' support from AMAZON, I kept on adjusting the settings on the Fire Stick and removed the Dolby to just STEREO and now I finally have the bass I wanted, All is well-- yet AMAZON is not interested. So I give it 3 stars.

title: Glaring Oversight If Used With Fire Tv Recast and/or 2nd Homes
body: If you plan to use this to access your Fire TV Recast content from a location outside of your home, you must know that it will NOT access your Over-The-Air Content (OTA) from an HD Antenna. This is such a glaring oversight I can't even believe it. If i had known this i would not have purchased a Fire TV Stick, let alone 2 to go along with a Fire TV Recast..Let me explain...I recently cut the cord and installed an outdoor HD Antenna and wired it through my existing coaxial cable in my house. That is amazing and now i get about 35 FREE HD channels. The challenge was figuring out how to set up a DVR to record OTA content and stream content as well. I did a lot of research and ended up buying a Fire TV Recast along with 2 Fire TV Sticks... 1 Fire TV Stick for use with the Fire TV Recast. This has been great, the connection is good and it has the apps i need (Netflix, Prime, Hulu), and the menu and integration with the HD Antenna also works well. I also downloaded the Fire TV App on my iphone and ipad. This app is amazing as it lets you access your Fire TV Recast content (from anywhere) including your LIVE TV and DVR'd content. Okay so far so good.The 2nd Fire TV Stick i purchased for my 2nd home i own that is 2.5 hours away. I figured since i can use the Fire TV App to access my Fire TV Recast content from anywhere, of course i will be able to access the same content using a Fire TV Stick at my other house......well i was wrong!For some reason an Amazon Fire TV Stick does not allow you to access the same content that the Fire TV App does. It says that you must be on your "home" network aka the network that you Fire TV Recast is on to access the LIVE TV & DVR content. So the Fire TV Stick doesn't allow you to access your Fire TV Recast, but literally any other device with either a wifi or cellular connection does. So my fix unfortunately will be to install another app to cast my iphone to the TV screen at my 2nd home, or i will have to get a physical adapter. I just don't get why or how Amazon could have let this one slip. If i had known this i would have purchased the Tivo Bolt OTA and paid the $6.99/m subscription, but then again I'm not even 100% sure that would have allowed what i am trying to do anywhere.Hopefully Amazon fixes this problem soon.

title: Updated interface pushes you to Amazon content and tech issues
body: Context - We own no less than four 4k Fire sticks. Have been a Fire stick customer for about 3 years. Have also been a smart TV user for 10 years. IT career for over 25 years.Tech Issues:Up to the last update we had twxhnical glitches and issues periodically with our equipment. Devices restarting randomly, even with replaced power bricks ans USB cables the devices still randomly reboot periodically.Software and Interface Issues and Downgrades:Amazon is trying to push everyone to their ecosystem. This means with ever update they are pushing their content more and more to the top and the customers preferred content to the bottom.The latest update is a prime example. Recently used Apps is now buried several rows down from the top of the interface and on a very large 4k TV I cannot see recently used apps like Netflix, Hulu, and YouTube from the main screen anymore. The top third of the screen now has a live preview of Amazon content forced upon you. Then you have to go past multiple rows of settings and other Amazon content just to get to Recently Used Apps let alone any other non Amazon apps.Also, some streaming apps do not seem to be working very well with this new update. YouTube and Hulu are both freezing a lot and not loading certain content on the first click. You need to click multiple times going out and back in to launch the stream. Its possible that will be resolved once updates and tweaks and fixes for this new version come out.Bottom line they do not want you to use other apps. This is a huge issue for me. Amazon is already getting my money for the device and I pay for multiple services including Prime and have been an Amazon prime customer from THE beginning of the service. I should be able to modify my Fire stick to display the Apps and content I want and I should be able to remove their ads for their content if I don't want it.I will likely be going to Roku as it seems to have everything I need plus doesn't force things down your throat.Amazon is getting too big for their britches and should not be allowed to force customers to their content or should at least give paying customers the ability to chose what content they want.Signed,Not a fan anymore

title: Power supply connector is Achilles heel
body: We replaced our first Firestick that served us well for years. It pretty much worked flawlessly EXCEPT when we were forced to power reset the Firestick when the Firestick refused to play (usually the first suggestion ISP remote tech’s make after suggesting you first power-reset your modem). On average, we had to do a few times a week. Doing this required disconnecting the power cord that feeds the Firestick, disconnecting the Firestrick from the monitor’s HDMI jack, wait 30 seconds, then re-assemble everything before the Firestick would begin working. Unfortunately, the power connector used in our first Firestick was a cheap, super-delicate connector set, definitely NOT designed for high cycle use (i.e., it wasn’t designed to be connected and dis-connected thousands or tens-of-thousands of times). As a result Firestick power connector began to fail prematurely, supplying power intermittently early in life. The more time passed, the more intermittent it became until it was impractical to “baby” an adequate power connection by applying just the right amount of flex and torque on the connector pair using cable ties wrapped around the Firestick!) So we bought the 4K replacement Firestick. And while it has a few more bells and whistles, guess what? Visually it appears to use the same cheap, light-weight, super-delicate, (and I’d assume low cycle life power connector as our original Firestick had. So that’s a big negative more me.Regarding Alexa Integration, we do not have, nor ever will have (for privacy issues) Alexa, so I cannot comment on that.Regarding Voice recognition, It’s my opinion that our first Firestick had better voice recognition. I’m an old aviation buff and one of my Youtube channels is by Ward Carroll. Voice recognition on the new Firestick NEVER recognizes this channel. I have to spell that out in the search bar (hen-peck style) because if I don’t, the voice recognition usually produces anything BUT Ward Carroll.I do enjoy the on-remote control volume control that my old unit did not have. And the feature that alerts you (via a screen prompt message) that the remote control is out of range, that can be priceless at saving you cumulative hours upon hours finding the remote you accidentally left (and forgot about) in the utility room!

title: Nice 4K device but with some issues
body: In our techy family we have a Roku Ultra, Apple TV 4K, PS4 Pro, and now the new Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K...We have yet to come across the perfect device and are still looking as the Stick 4K is really nice but still not quite perfect.We have tested Hulu OnDemand, HuluTV, AmazonPrimeVideo, SlingTV, and Netflix on each device.Hulu works great on the Stick 4K except the on demand shows have no 5.1 sound (which the PS4 does), and the HuluTV has the older guide only showing what’s playing currently but nothing into the future (which the Apple TV let’s you do)...Netflix also works okay except has some HDR issues (most noticeable on Marvel shows where it’s 12 bit down converted to 10 bit) and looks dull/washed out (yet can be seen in perfect 4K HDR on the PS4).It’s best streaming service, which can be expected, is Amazon Prime Video. It plays up to full 4K HDR and looks great! Unfortunately they still have it scattered all throughout the home screen cluttering it up like in the past (the Apple TV and Roku have way better streamlined easy to navigate layouts).Also, if you use SlingTV or DirecTV Now, they work well on the Fire Stick 4K (except Sling has 5.1 audio only on Apple TV and DirecTV has some 5.1 audio only on the Fire TV/Stick 4K...)I like all the video options on the Fire TV 4K. You can set the resolution, color bit depth (8/10/12 bit), and wether or not to force HDR (can’t turn it off though!, only forced on or set by content).And as far as sound, it does have nice sound capabilities but are crippled by forcing everything in your selected format. If you choose stereo then EVERYTHING will be in stereo. And if you choose Dolby Digital/Dolby Digital Plus, then EVERYTHING is output in Dolby (even all shows in stereo ruining the Dolby Plus surround and forcing everything into Dolby 2.1 stereo). Also beware, it does not come with a ethernet adapter so needs to be purchased separately to be able to hook up to your router (for the best internet connection).Basically, every device has its quirks and issues and while unfortunately none are perfect, the Fire TV Stick 4K is a great choice and should be perfect for most people.

title: Still has problems with freezing
body: I had high hopes for the Amazon Firesticks and I have to say I own four of them including the latest 4K version. The problem I have had with them is that when watching PlayStation Vue the screen will freeze on a regular basis for between 5 and 15 seconds. It seemed worse on live sporting events and it seemed to be at the most inopportune times, like bases loaded 3-2 count and pitcher just about to deliver the ball. For the longest time I had assumed this was either due to my internet service of PlayStation Vue. On Black Friday I came across a great sale on the Roku Ultra device and decided to try it. To my pleasant surprise I have been watching for over a week and have not had a single freeze with the Roku Ultra.So to be fair the Roku Ultra is not a stick but a small box but if you want to watch tv over the internet with quality similar to cable the difference is pretty meaningless. That is not to say there were not things about the Fire Stick that I did not like better than the Roku.First the Alexa voice control works amazingly well. So far I have not gotten the voice control on the Roku to do anything.Fast forwarding works much better on the Firestick than the Roku. On the Firestick when you fast forward through a DVR program the video keeps up pretty well allowing you to easily fast forward past commercials. Fast forwarding on the Roku you need to watch the time and guess when the commercial might be ending.Access to the guide and the previous channel is much better on the Firestick. One of the most ;useful buttons on my remote was the previous channel button. On the Firestick holding down on the control button on the remote brought up a menu with the previous channel and the guide as well as a means to go to DVR shows. On the Roku holding down the OK button brings up program information and the ability to select the guide and previously viewed shows (note shows and not channel so if the show is over there is no previous channel capability).So the Firestick 4K might be the best streaming stick but if you want to use it to replace cable and don't want to be frustrated by your screen freezing then you might want to purchase something else.

title: Hard to get Prime Video to work
body: Set up was fairly easy. The stick is somewhat wide/fat and would not fit in my HDMI port without interference with other HDMI connectors, so I had to use the included "dongle". Basic set up and channels worked fine.It took several weeks (and reading other online comments) to get Prime Video to work even though I already have a Prime account. When I first tried it and logged in, it had me "sign up" which apparently created a new account. I discovered this when I was billed $12.99 for the first month a few weeks later. I had to call Amazon to have this cancelled and credited. When I talked to the representative they were not sure why it did not automatically recognize my existing account. I tried to de-register and re-register the stick several times but this did not help.I have a household Prime account. In order to get Prime video to work, you have to log into your prime account on a computer or other device and go to manage your household. Then you have to share your payment method with the other household member. When you do this it asks you to verify the account which brings you back to a login screen. At this point you need the other household member to login at this screen (if you login again yourself it does nothing). I guess this is because there is still some content you may need to pay for, but not sure why the free content included with a Prime membership would not work without this step.It is now working, but I had to find information on how to fix this by reading online posts. The instructions on the Amazon site are very poor and even after reading what to do it was difficult to find the right page/section of the membership site to make these setting changes. It seems for a Tech company they would have an FAQ or simple step by step instructions to set this up properly. Based on my online search this is a frequent issue for users.As many others have commented, the other issue with the Firestick compared to ROKU is the Firestick promotes Amazon content. It takes a little more work to arrange your home screen to put your preferred apps at the top. This is not a huge deal overall.

title: Buy a Roku for TV stream, buy an Echo, Echo Dot, Echo Show, Echo Spot or Fire Tablet for Alexa.
body: I have several Roku devices and a Fire TV stick and a Fire TV Stick 4 K. I got the 4K Stick in Feb 2019 per my purchase history. After spending approx an hour with tech support, (Amazon tech support is great, Fire Sticks are not) I finally got my problem resolved by doing a complete factory reset, which means I had to go through everything I went through to set it up when I first took it out of the box. The issue was with the Alexa part of the Fire Sticks. I had the same issue with both the Fire Stick and 4K Fire Stick. While I was playing Jeopardy or when Alexa was speaking for more that a few seconds, the Alexa voice would just stop. Like I said the other features worked just fine. However, the Roku device seems more user friendly to me. It offers more channel options, and I have never had to do a factory reset even on Roku devices I have had for several years. Don't get me wrong. I love Alexa, I have a 7 and 8 inch Fire Tablet (which is connected to the docking station to make it work like an Echo Show), I have 2 Echo Dots and an Echo spot, and I think they are all great. I have Alexa set on my Android phone as my default voice assistant. I have many smart home, car, etc devices connected to Alexa. So many in fact, that I have issues if my internet is out. If you want TV streaming device buy a Roku. If you want an Alexa device, get an Echo Spot, Dot, a Fire tablet 8 inch or bigger I would suggest, and though I don't have them an Echo or an Echo show, would probably be great too. (Along the same note, Amazon is not permitting reviews on the new Echo Auto yet. I got one as soon as I got my invitation. I have the same issues with the Echo Auto that I have with the Fire sticks. Alexa's voice stopping, also have issues with my Flash Briefings not working, and it is worthless when using it for directions due to the voice issues. When I am able to review it, it will get 1 star or maybe two. And Amazon has a list of vehicles that Echo does not work with. My truck is not on that list, so it should work fine.) Again, I am an Amazon and Alexa junkie, but I will not buy another Fire Stick or another Echo Auto.

title: How to change single sign in info
body: UPDATEUpdated to 3 stars since I found a solution to the single sign on issue but am mad at Amazon for ignoring the problem. I spent hours on the phone with Verizon and Amazon and they just kept blaming each other. None of my single sign on apps were working and kept asking me to purchase a cable package if when I tried to sign in. After reading a reddit post I tried signing into the AMC app, signing out, and signing back in again. For some reason this app brings you to what looks like a Web page log in screen where you enter your cable credentials and then it updates it for all your other single sign on apps! It's amazing. I don't know why this app does that but it's the trick to updating your single sign in credentials and fixing everything!---------Thought I was being real clever getting a great deal on Verizon Fios internet and then using the Firestick to sign into apps to watch TV using my parents cable info. Did the same with my Roku beforehand and it was great! Essentially free cable and HBOGo, whole package. Now I switched to the Firestick (whose platform I like better) and due to the "convenient" single sign in feature it automatically signs into my Verizon Fios account without me providing it with any information. So every time I download an app and try to sign in it says I have to buy a Verizon cable package. I've done a ton of research online and people are having the same problem with no solution provided by Amazon, cable, or internet providers.Verizon keeps giving me the run around possibly because they're blocking other cable providers on their network now that net neutrality has been repealed. And Firestick has messed up in the fact that there is no way to turn this feature off. They also messed up in the sense that ALL they had to do was at any point ask "Who is your cable provider?". I would have put in the info and moved on. Now I have to go back to the Roku so I can save $100 a month on cable and keep my relationship in tact so we don't kill each other over this dumb situation and the fact that he can't watch football anymore. You've been warned.

title: Not terrible but Roku is better
body: We have been using a Roku on a spare TV for a couple of years so we wanted to try the Fire TV Stick.The main issue:The Fire TV's home screen interface is cluttered. You have a top horizontal menu. Then you have to click down twice to get to your apps. But don't make the mistake of clicking down from the wrong menu item on the horizontal menu up top - you won't get to your apps that way. I dinged a star for that.Now on to the Vudu issue:You can't download Vudu without going around the back door and using a developer app. I dinged another star for that. After downloading the hacked version of Vudu, it works, but it's not its full self - you can access your content but the interface on the contraband Vudu is missing some graphics. This isn't a problem with Roku - you get the full Vudu in all its glory. Hey, I hate that Vudu is owned by who they are owned by, but we have movies we watch repetitively as white noise, and since we already owned the discs, we were able to put them up on Vudu for very little money with their Movies Everywhere functionality. We all know why Amazon won't let us have Vudu without hacking the stick - Amazon's primary job is to sell and rent movies - but I already owned the movies! Roku gives you full access to Vudu. Come on, Amazon - it's 2020 and you are the most powerful company in the world. Do better.The remote is pretty slick, though. Thinner and just cooler looking than Roku's, but with all the same basic functions as the Roku remote, nothing more. We didn't integrate the Fire TV with our Echo because we knew we were going to return the devices so I can't comment on that functionality.So the two Fire TV devices we ordered went back and Rokus were ordered as their replacements. Neither of these issues discussed above were necessarily a dealbreaker, but as we already knew how streamlined Roku was, it was an easy decision for us since the devices are the same price. If you haven't used Roku and you don't mind Amazon's interface, you probably won't be missing anything unless you use Vudu heavily.

title: Not a noticeable improvement over the previous version
body: Update as of 05/2022: purchased this on 10/2019 to replace the previous version that had started acting up after a year of use. 2 days ago this unit started acting oddly. Remote randomly started disconnecting and it's sporadically dropping internet connection and freezing. This was the exact same thing that my previous Firestick started doing and why I replaced it with this unit. We have VERY good internet service here due to so many of us in this household working from home, the batteries in the remote were changed less than a month ago and I'm not streaming 4K media (mostly YouTube with a few movies on Shudder a week). 2.5 years is probably an acceptable time frame for a device like this, but compared to my 6 year old Chrome Cast that's still going strong it's kind of sad. I guess I'll be buying a new one soon.Bought this as an upgrade for an older HD model that started getting spotty wi-fi reception after a year of use. The spotty wi-fi reception seems to have been caused by a system update, n9t a failing device. This kne worked fine for the first hour after installing until it decided to update, after which I started getting notifications that my signal was weak and had to restart the device (same fix I used on the older model).The voice recognition works well... when it decides to work that is. Voice recognition goes in and out at random, I'll get the 'ding' in response to hitting the button, but it will randomly refuse to acknowledge that any verbal commands were given. The voice recognition on the older model still works perfectly. My roommates have this same model Firestick and are having the same problems with the voice recognition, so this is not an isolated problem.I have a 5 year old Chromecast that still works perfectly that I'm strongly considering switching back to at the moment.On top of everything the shipment arrived a day late and I had to go through 2 different departments to get anything cleared up.Overall a so-so purchase that arrived later than stated and gives me continual problems every time I use it

title: Still no real support for ultraviolet/vudu libraries
body: Still requires anyone with a significant UV/Vudu library to maintain a Roku/ATV, etc if you have a lot of movies that are UV only (Paramount, etc.). (I'm aware of the sideloading option and hoped it would address this problem but a) currently locks up all playback on the FireTV if I try to play a UHD movie via sideloaded Vudu b) support still stinks because those UV only movies don't show up in FireTV search (and do on Roku)). Maybe someday the remaining UV members will join MoviesAnywhere. Or maybe Amazon will get a clue and get a deal in place so Vudu is supported (or join what's left of UV) (Note to Amazon - I'd be more likely to buy movies from Amazon if I didn't have to leave your device to see what I own in Vudu on a different device, because I then just buy there.) 5 stars if Amazon ever understands enlightened self-interest and either deals with Ultraviolet or Vudu.On the plus side, when connected to a TV that won't properly pass Dolby Digital plus to a receiver or sound bar, this device allows you to select DD only so that the Fire TV transcodes DD+ for output over ARC or optical. Currently shipping Rokus don't do this (not an issue if your TV does the transcoding or if the FireTV is connected through a receiver with 4k support).Also on the plus side, Amazon finally noticed that people like to adjust volume without yelling at Alexa and so added power/volume controls for the TV. If you don't have another set top box and don't use apps in your TV, you might be able to get away with using only the Fire TV remote.Video quality is good once TV settings for the input are adjusted for the device (might take some tinkering for HDR).Summary: If you don't own any UV only movies via Vudu or other former UV members, it's five stars. If you like to keep your movie collection in one place for easy search and that place is currently Vudu, Fire TV loses to AppleTv (which handles 5.1 audio via ARC correctly also) or Roku (if DD+ via TV->receiver is not an issue for you)

title: Meh
body: At first I thought "Great!" But then the menu button ceased to function and I couldn't find a way to control closed captioning on my apps such as Acorn TV and Brit Box. It also wouldn't even run Acorn TV properly. Now I have CC back but don't know how I did it and am afraid to mess with it any more for fear I'll lose it again.As suggested, I deleted the data for Acorn TV and reloaded it. It now SEEMS to be working properly, but I have no faith it will continue to do so. If I'm running an Amazon video everything is fine, but I was expecting more functionality than that, thus the 3 star rating. Why could I find no ratings on Amazon before I bought it?I thought about returning the stick, but I do like that the remote controls my TV as well as the stick (on/off, volume) and I'm still working on learning what it can and can't do. Alexa was also a selling point, but it can't do anything with CC....she told me more than once "I can't do that" when I asked her to turn it on. I've tried lots of searches to try to resolve this and problems with my apps such as Acorn. I might add I have never had any difficulty with my ROKU products, so the Stick has been especially frustrating.This may turn out to be a complete waste of $50, but I'm going to keep it. Yes, I'm a glutton for punishment. I will update this review in a few weeks. Maybe it's all down to a learning curve and my wife is right...."It's all your fault." 😊UPDATE 7/16/19----Well, took more than a few weeks, but...3 stars is still about right for me. I tired of my apps showing up in different places on the home screen and not completely displaying their content. This was a particular problem for non-Amazon apps such as Acorn and Brit Box. If you just stick to Amazon media it should be fine. However I just bought a ROKU Streaming Stick + and couldn't be happier. As it is agnostic, it doesn't care where your apps come from and the interface is much easier to understand and use. To each his own, I guess.

title: Twitch and Youtube apps lack, not favorite device
body: My wife purchased the 4K stick through QVC a year or so ago. It's definitely not our ideal streaming device. We mostly got it only because Roku no longer had a Twitch app (otherwise we would have stuck to Roku)Even though Amazon now owns Twitch, the Twitch App is severely lacking and buggy. The emotes pane in Twitch doesn't show all global emotes, and glitches out every now and again showing chopped up emotes that don't look right until you reset the fire TV. Further more, it never gets updates to work with new Twitch features, it's just barebones for viewing single streams (it has chat, but it's only good for reading chat since it's so much easier to use the mobile app to actually type in chat). No polls, no squad stream, no hype train or any plugins. it's just barebones stream and chat.It took forever for them to get a Youtube app but I'm glad they have one now. It works great for a while. Unfortunately, after a certain amount of time (I haven't figured out how much time yet) the audio and video go out of sync and is incredibly noticeable when watching people speaking in their videos. I don't know why this happens, and resetting the fire stick is the only way I know to fix this (sometimes). Occasionally, even resetting the fire stick doesn't fix the audio video sync issue and it's incredibly frustrating. It seems to only happen to Youtube, but it's one of my most used apps.Netflix is fine, though there's NO WAY TO TURN OFF AUTOPLAY. leave your cursor on a movie or tv show for a second and it will play a trailer. Go into the movie or tv show to read the description and it will either play audio or start playing the first episode instead of just letting you READ THE DESCRIPTION. It's annoying but that's the only issue.Prime Video works fine, but I wish it had a better catalogue for browsing. It's fine for searching for titles you already know you want to watch.I use Sling and the Sling app is perfect. I've never had any issues.

title: First off, I am a big Amazon fan...
body: I love shopping on Amazon - I use Alexa and actually own a Fire Stick for travel. In looking to outfit my numerous older TVs with a streaming player, I naturally had to look at the Fire Sticks as well as Roku Streaming Sticks. Fire Stick Pros: 1) Easy to set up, 2) Nice looking and easy to use remote, 3) Has a lot of the channels I would want in my streaming device (still need HBO Max and Peacock IMO). Then I tried the Roku Streaming Stick+ - comments: 1) Easy to set up, 2) I like the Alexa remote better - its sleeker, more high tech - but its still easy to use, 3) It also has a lot of the channels I would want in my streaming device (also needs HBO Max and Peacock IMO). So why did I pick the Roku? I wanted to keep things simple and Roku offers me a simple display of my channels without too many placed ads and "features" littered throughout, so it is easier to navigate through what I would like to watch. I really do not think I need Alexa on my remote or on my TV, and I do not want to see everything that Prime Video offers while scrolling through the Fire Stick (I also subscribe to Prime Video, but I don't want to see it displayed everywhere). Finally, although I like the Fire Stick, I deducted two stars. The first star deducted is due to the fact that I am being charged almost $8 to return the Fire Stick unless I return it directly to an Amazon Locker! I've purchased hundreds of items through Amazon and I believe this is the first time I am being charged to return anything. The second star deducted is that I have to completely package up my return in a box. I have returned dozens of items to Amazon, and I would say 90% of the time I can just drop it off at the UPS store without having to package it up. Not with this purchase! Is this Amazon's way of getting back at me for not keeping the Fire Stick? If so, I will be much more careful purchasing Amazon products in the future - they don't play well in the sandbox with other competition...

title: lags during movies, remote is maybe the best part of this
body: I had high hopes for this 4K Fire stick. I've used their older fire stick and had issues in which movies would pause in the middle and you'd have to unplug the fire stick to correct the issue. Then I switched to Roku and that has been working like a charm for a couple of years. However, the lure of watching movies in 4K was too tempting and when I saw this go on sale, I decided I'd see how it works.After three weeks, I am disappointed. First, the 4K movies that are available are very few, and none that are very good. If you are buying this to watch 4K movies, the selection is only on Amazon Prime and there are only a handful to choose from. Note, I have a cable provider so I didn't buy this as a television or cable replacement - the main reason I bought this was faster processing speed, 4K movies, and the Alexa feature on the remote.Unfortunately, the movies still lag and I get that infamous pause in which the systems slowly goes from 0% to 99% before resuming. 90 minute movies turn into two hours with the amount of times it lags or pauses in the middle trying to catch up at times. I'd blame my own internet, but the issue only seems to be on the Fire stick. Not on my Fire HD 10" Tablet, and it never happens on my Roku either.However, the remote is awesome. It controls the volume and I love the alexa feature to rewind, fast forward, or to jump to a new movie you'd like to watch. If it weren't for the remote, I'd probably give it a one star.Update: I am upgrading to three stars. There are a lot of movies that you can watch for free with some of the apps that are included with the 4K Fire Stick - where I'd be paying to rent with Roku. Apps like Pluto, Sony Crackle, and Shudder are great add-on's. I think I've saved over $20 on movies I'd otherwise have rented when I was using my Roku. Unfortunately, still getting the pause/lag issues where it freezes, plays for a few minutes, and freezes again.

title: Don't have a 4K comp TV? Don't buy this!
body: I bought this prior to purchasing an upgraded TV, then decided against the TV upgrade, so the 4K Fire Stick did not come in handy as much as it could/should have. That being said, there were still several options that we made great use of: 1. The load times were lightning fast in comparison to its predecessor. 2. The TV power button on the Fire Stick remote was very useful, as well as the volume adjustments. 3. When the Voice Recognition feature worked, it was excellent....HOWEVER...it rarely ever worked. I'm confused as to how asking for "ROM COMS" would load shows like "The Unexplained with William Shatner". (Not a random or singular experience...not by a hundred-or-so).Additionally, be prepared to have to restart your Fire Stick religiously...We've gone a few days with no issues at all, then had some days where we had to reset close to 5 or 6 times. Btw, we are a tech-savvy household and my and my husband's jobs (and son's gaming sanity) all depend on our internet connection...so we have the fastest speeds possible and farthest range WiFi routers....so that's not the issue.So....unless you have a 4K compatible TV, it's REALLY not worth it unless having an off switch and volume control in one remote is of the utmost importance to you. Honestly, we really were happy enough with it for the past year and a half (purchased in mid-November 2019), restarting and Voice Recognition aside (I've got Google...I'll be ok 🤣), but we can't really deal with the latest, worst, root-planting issue: it now refuses to pick up our WiFi signal and register it anything above fair, then drops it, and lists our address as "connected with problems". Funnily enough, every other device in the house works just fine. In fact, I unplugged the 4K, and replaced it with the OLD model we kicked out of bed for that one, and it works just fine. Thanks, Amazon. Don't make 'em like they used to, huh? :/

title: Works, but Interface is busy
body: I've been using Windows 7 Media Center for my home DVR (don't laugh, it works great). But when the PC running it started to crap out I looked for alternatives. I got a good deal on this on Prime Day, along with a Fire TV Recast it replaces my recording setup for less than what a PC replacement would. This setup does have the advantage of watching recordings at all my TVs, providing I get a Fire TV Stick for each one (no, I don't thing moving the stick from TV to TV is practical)The interface is a bit lacking. Granted I'm coming from a very TV focused DVR setup to this, which is more streaming focused. However this interface is mostly an advertisement for Prime content, with a sprinkling of links to other streaming services. When you add in the Fire Recast you do get local channels, both live and recorded, but you can tell that part is definitely an afterthought. You are mostly encouraged to watch streaming, and just getting to the live TV channel guide is sometimes difficult. Most of the time I find myself just hitting the mic button and telling it to show the guide. It's nice that there are voice controls, but in some ways you are forced into using them unless you want to spend a lot of time looking.Video quality seems to be the same for other streaming services, for Prime videos it seems to be better than my LG's Prime App. I'm not completely convinced the 5.1 sound really gets output, but that's hard to tell if it's a problem with the Fire Stick or the content broadcast.I do connect to the Internet via it's built in WiFi. I had no problems with buffering, but the WiFi router and the Fire Stick are very close, so I wouldn't expect any.All in all I'm glad I bought it (especially at the $25 Prime day price), but I'm looking forward to them updating the UI and fixing some minor bugs in the software.

title: Can’t play local 4K
body: Overall disappointed.I own the regular current generation fire tv. I also have a Roku 4K stick, Apple TV (not 4K) and early chromecast.I was hoping the 4K direct could be my go to device for everything. The fire tv either 4K or regular, the only device that can display a website. But I discovered limitations.My biggest issue is that I have not found a way to play 4K video from my local server with decent quality. I tried using VLC. It’s usually a great app on most platforms. But here in fire tv or android, it falls short. It has the following issues:- in a file I tried to play, aspect ratio was wrong and the correct ratio wasn’t one of the possible choices.- colors blown out and washed out.- no way to have audio passthru of Dolby or DTS 5.1.On all three counts, the Roku 4K stick with the Roku Meg is player worked very well.Other sources of 4K that I tried included amazon prime video and YouTube. Both of these worked fine, but they also work fine on the Roku.Bottom line for playing 4K content, the Roku is superior with no drawbacks other than inability to view a web site. I can stay with my regular fire tv if I need that. The 4K firetv is noticeably faster, but that’s not enough for me.I tried other video players. Could not find a player that was better than VLC. Some were expensive and still didn’t perform well.The initial setup process was extremely difficult. Confusion over two factor authentication caused the system to not let me use the device. I called support. They were not helpful. Ultimately I had to disable two factor authentication, add the device and reenable it. It was my issue for the way two factor authentication was set up originally, but the message on the fire tv did not give a clear indication of the issue.

title: Setup could be a lot better
body: Last year on Prime day, I got a half price deal on the older diamond shaped model. It worked well, and with this year's sale, I decided to get two more. It's great to have when traveling and I wanted one on each TV.I was disappointed when I installed it and the colors were washed out. They were fine on the older Fire, and when using apps for the same content on a smart TV. The specs on the new one are better across the board, so that made no sense.I played around with the settings, and found that setting the Dynamic Range setting to Adaptive, instead of Always HDR fixed the problem. I also changed some other video settings, but that was the major one that helped. I read some reviews outside of Amazon to see if I could find out why, and I found several reviews from major tech publications that suggested making that change if you have a newer TV. It's worth reading articles with titles such as "top ten things you need to do with your new Fire" to see if anything else will help you.The big problem I have with this isn't that there was a drop in quality, but that this should be part of the setup process. As it walks you though the setup, it should ask you which of the two settings looks better on your TV, after showing you various content. But instead, they bury it. Users should not have to explore ways of getting things to work properly, and if I hadn't had a Fire in the past, and known that it should have been capable of showing a much better picture, I might have had a dull washed out picture. Users shouldn't even have to have technical knowledge. They should simply know that if the picture looks washed out and the device gives a better choice, or if things look oversaturated and the device gives a better choice, then the user could select it at setup time and not have to worry about it again.

title: MORE CAPABLE THAN STD FIRESTICK BUT NEEDS MORE MEMORY, HAS OTHER ISSUES
body: REVIEW UPDATE OCT '21: The 4K Fire TV Stick we use the most (we have 4) steadily got more prone to hanging up and other video issues like pixelation, so we replaced it. The new one needs to be restarted every 2-4 days to keep it happy. The screensaver screens are beautiful but take up far too much memory. Amazon should either double the memory or provide an option to limit (or turn off) screensaver screens. (Selecting Screensaver "Force Stop" does not do that, nor does it inhibit the download of numerous large image files the app uses.) The YouTubeTV app still doesn't work as well with Fire TV Stick as it should, even the 4K model. And now there's a Fire TV Stick 4K MAX, apparently to address such shortcomings.BOTTOM LINE: When it's happy the 4K Fire TV Stick works fine but it doesn't stay that way for long, even with rock solid 200+ Mbps internet and an awesome Netgear wireless router. Rating reduced to 3 stars.ORIGINAL RATING:We had standard Fire TV Sticks and subsequently signed up for YouTubeTV service. Unfortunately, they would not stream it in 1080p HD, even though we have very robust 200 Mbps broadband that consistently tests 230+ Mbps. After working with YouTubeTV tech support at length, we decided to try a Fire TV Stick 4K. We found it *would* stream YouTubeTV in HD and appears to have a better receiver because it consistently tests over 150 Mbps down. (Our wireless router is AC2600-class.) Things have gone well since then except the Fire TV Stick 4K periodically gets cranky and we find it's about out of memory. To fix it we just clear the cache on all apps and manually restart. Bottom Line: It's a very good streaming device, with a great selection of apps, but really needs 16 GB of memory instead of 8 GB, unless your app collection is skinny.

title: It's ok was hoping for better. Probably best to avoid it
body: The fire stick is ok at best. Weird minor issues with it on the regular.Sometimes there are quick audio cut outs. That is in all apps. This really kills the vibe of movies and music.The streaming can be hit or miss. I am on a 800 down 20 up connection and still titles will sometimes get blurry for a few seconds before becoming clear again. I use the wifi but they should make a wired adapter that supports gigabit connection speeds rather than the one they currently make which tops at 100mbps.There is no screen saver. If you have an OLED make sure to set a sleep timer ALWAYS. This thing is a piece of crap. It will show a slide show for about 15 minutes then the screen goes black. This is "standby" but it is still sending video signal but just a black screen. Your TV won't activate its screen saver or automatic shut off because it is receiving a video signal still. AND YOU CANNOT CHANGE THIS WITHOUT VOIDING THE WARRANTY AND SELF HACKING IT ON YOUR PC!the remote constantly loses connection and sometimes requires unplugging the fire stick power to reset it. That is a lot of fun on a wall mounted TV. Also the remote's battery door is so flimsy that half the time it slides off grabbing the remote off the night stand.For a product that has been out for so long and so many generations of it. This is very subpar. The Playstation is a better streaming platform and it's designed to play games. This is literally designed to stream only and does a worse job. I just can't fit or afford another Playstation for this roomSadly it seems this review translates to the same issues that roku has as well. Seems like there isn't much for streaming options. Maybe Apple TV but I don't like Apple and I hear it has its own problems as well.

title: Alexa Fire TV Stick 4K
body: I got the Fire TV Stick 4K during the thanksgiving deal for a discount. So, I do think it is well worth the price. I also have a Roku, so it was easy to make a comparitive review.1. BulkyThe first thing you notice is that the fire TV stick 4K is bulky! Unlike the Roku stick which is like a usb pen drive, Fire TV Stick 4K is a bulky stick that did not fit in the provided slot for my TV. I tried using the provided hdmi extender to make it bend so it would fit in the hdmi slot. But, after multiple attempts it was not recognized by my JVC tv. I was lucky that I had a 3rd hdmi slot on the side of the TV edge. This saved my day.2. Needs constant power connection.Unlike the roku stick that is powered just by plugging the stick into the hdmi slot, the Fire TV Stick 4K needs to be not only plugged into the hdmi slot but it also has to be plugged into the power outlet using the power adapter provided. This wire for power just kinda hangs on the side of the TV and is ugly.3. Streaming is slowThe first thing we tried was youtube streaming. Unlike the roku which works perfectly with streaming, for slower connections Fire TV Stick 4K compensated the buffering by a blurry continuous stream. This almost made me feel like my eyes were blurry. My eyes are becoming blurry, but my wife confirmed that the video was indeed blurry and its not just me seeing double images. This cant be good for my eyes. I dont remember Roku ever doing that. But, we have a slow AT&T internet connect, but if you have a fast internet connection, this may not be an issue at all.4. Free channels are not really free. The Fire TV Stick 4K is advertised as accompanying many free channels like NBC and many other channels. Unfortunately none of them worked for me. When you try to access these supposedly 'free' channels you are asked for your provider - to select from cable company or internet company. Unfortunately, when I selected ATT, it goes through my ATT login and I got a message that I dont have a token to play these channels. Only the basic free channels like Crackle, some news channels work. This was a bummer, but not something I wanted from Fire TV Stick 4K. So, its misleading that Amazon has to advertise it that way.5. You have to link it to an Amazon account. Unlike Roku, where it is just plug an play, without really logging in, it is an inconvenience that there is no default option to play Fire TV Stick 4K. You are forced to either use your amazon account or create an amazon account to use Fire TV Stick 4K . This is pretty bad. The downside for us was that some of the Amazon apps are registered on my name and some on my wife's name. I decided to go with my account so I could access my amazon music on TV. But amazon prime is on my wife's account and it doesnt let me access amazon prime without completely replacing my account with my wife's. On roku, we didnt have this issue. Also, our kid's paid videos on Amazon are on my wife's account. So, with my account on Alexa, it wants me to subscribe again to access the kids paid video account. It is just a big hassle. So, we now go back to our Roku to access Amazon prime but for most else we stick with Fire TV Stick 4K. I really dont like the switching.Now going into the good things about Fire TV Stick 4K1. The remote is fast! atleast compared to Roku, it better be since the Fire TV Stick 4K is contantly powered by an AC adapter plugged into the power outlet.2. The voice remote option on the remote is awesome! This is the biggest difference I noticed compared to Roku. Alexa is sweet. It is almost like the voice assistant on the phone (in my case android), but it is just more visual because it is connected to the TV. I dont have echo, but it works for me although I dont get the full experience of Alexa, but most things are easy to get to using the voice remote than using the navigation buttons on the remote, plus its fun to chat with Alexa, atleast until the fancy of newness lasts.3. Integration of Alexa Fire TV Stick 4K with the Alexa app on the phone is poorVery often when using the Fire TV Stick 4K voice remote, you will find it referring to the alexa app on the phone. So, you will be forced to download the app on the phone. Then you find out that it was a waste of time because the phone and the remote are not integrated well.Overall, I do not regret transitioning from Roku to Fire TV Stick 4K . I am still enjoying the Alexa experience.

title: Almost 5 Star (4.95) Hits most of the marks
body: I'm always looking for the perfect streaming device. Something that is fast and responsive. The 4K FS hits most of the marks.I was running the previous generation Fire Sticks with a "SideClick" (very cool add-on for Fire Stick owners that want to eliminate extra remote controls). This setup gave me all the functions I needed with my streaming service and TV/Soundbar controls/input switching etc..The older Fire Sticks were sluggish when scrolling through apps and it would often lag and I'd be inadvertently skipping through the app I wanted to select. I'd also find myself unplugging the Fire Stick to eliminate the lag and sluggish behavior.The 4K has a much better processor and is very fast and responsive (actually has the fastest processor of all the Fire TV devices). Downloading and installing apps is lightening quick compared to the order Fire Stick. So 5 Stars for thatThe 4K remote now has an On/Off button to turn on the TV set and also a volume control for either the TV set speakers or a soundbar. Integration and set up on my TV was simple. 5 Stars for this too (eliminates the need for a "Sideclick"- actually kinda sorry to see it go)Here's the rub! I signed up for Peacock TV trial (three months of the prime (all content with commercials) and it worked great with the older Fire Stick.
