The new 4K FS will play most of the content but hangs up on some of the movies (Specifically The BIg Lebowski, Moonrise Kingdom and Lost in Translation to name a few). Other movies and TV shows play fine. While this isn't a deal breaker, it is an annoyance since in all other areas the 4K stick is a huge improvement over the older one.I realize there isn't an "authorized" Peacock app available for the Fire Stick, but there are Peacock apps available that work fine (at least on the older Fire Sticks). As mentioned they work perfectly with the older Fire Stick and the Fire Dongle. Can't completely blame the 4K stick for the problem as it may be something in the app that is causing this to occur (some of the movies on Peacock show 45-120 seconds of commercials at the beginning of the movie and then w/o interruption going forward - this could be the problem with the app not working on those titles). Just letting everyone know that if you use the Peacock streaming service, you may encounter a problem with some of the content w/ the 4K stick and the current app available.I have an Nvida Shield Pro (and it is fantastic). A lot more expensive that the 4K stick too. All applications work excellently with it. Strangely, If is start to watch "The Big Lebowski" (which is one title that won't play from the start on the 4K Firestick) on the Nvidia Shield Pro, and stop it, then go to my 4K Firestick, the movie will play fine from the point I left off viewing. This could be due to the way the app is handling the title starting w/ an Ad or something else.FWIW, anyone with a Vudu account and movies in the cloud, the Vudu app for the Firestick (you'll have to look for this search this one out too as Amazon does not support Vudu), the available app will allow you to access and watch movies you have purchased but does not allow access nor viewing of any of the free content Vudu offers. Nvidia Shield does support the android Vudu app and I have access to all my purchased content and all of the free content offered by Vudu.Take away on the 4K Firestick? Run and buy one NOW!. It is nearly as fast as the Nvidia Shield Pro and so easy to set up and use. $49 is a great price ($34.95 on sale was a steal). With the exception of Peacock TV and Vudu, everything else works great. Saving a few buck$ on the older generation isn't smart. You be happier with the speedy performance and future proofing yourself if you ever get a 4K set (providing you IP gives you the speeds necessary).Hope this review helps..............................

title: FireTV is great. Remote control (opening battery compartment) is PITA, needs improvement.
body: I'm a Prime member but rarely used the video&music portion of my membership. Previously I was accessing the video thru my DVD player which had a clunky user interface (which is probably why I didn't use it much). Amazon ended support of the Sony products. I got a $25 discount code and thought...why not. I have an older 1080 HDTV but opted for the 4K FireTV to be prepared for a future TV upgrade, plus cashed in some of my Amazon Rewards and had this for free.Biggest complaint is the remote, specifically, the back is a real PITA to get open. I have literally opened thousands of battery compartments. Nearly every one in intuitive. I expected this to be the same, but NOOO, I struggled with it for a good 15min before finally heading to google for some assistance. It seems that this is by far and away the biggest complaint. Do yourself a favor, don't struggle with the remote like I did, head over to google or youtube and watch a video or two and you will get it open fairly quickly. Amazon needs to change the design of the remote battery cover and/or get the quick start/manual updated, because it is clearly not as simple as "open the remote and install the batteries"...but in all honesty, opening the battery compartment is something that will be done infrequently and you really don't want to cover popping open every time the remote drops on the floor (which already happened a few times for me).Other than the hassle with the remote battery cover, I had this up and connected to my wifi with 10min. After the connection was established the FireTV automatically updated and rebooted. This took several more minutes but in no time I was clicking thru the many video and music choices. There are quite a few settings in various menus. One thing I don't like is that the whole Alexia. Maybe I'm a bit of a foil-hat but I have not bought into the whole auto-everything home. I also have a programmable remote control that I used for my entertainment center (older LogicTech 650) that works well. I don't need or want the FireTV controlling all my devices, indeed it can't because the FireTV remote does not have options for amp, tuner, DVD, CD, turntable, cable/DVR. One final irk, there is no sleep/power button for the FireTV. The power button on the remote is only for controlling external devices (i.e. TV) and my LogicTech manages all my prerprogrammed settings just fine. In order to put the FireTV to sleep, you need to press and hold the Home button to access a mini-menu where Sleep is an option. I did find the menu for Controllers and Bluetooth Devices and was able to pair an old keyboard that I used with my KindleFire.The video, music and photo services are great. Was watching video and listening to music in not time (after the initial setup, update, reboot). There are pluses and minuses to the Amazon video and music services, as I use them more I'm sure I will get them customized to my liking. For now I'll still use Pandora for music as I am more familiar with it and already have many "stations" created to suit my various moods and situational settings. Previously, with my DVD player access, I was not able to take advantage of the photos options. I uploaded some recent photos and wow, quite impressed with how they look on the TV, wife loved it. As a Prime member I have unlimited photos so this is looking like a great option and an easy way to share and view all my photos (of course it won't be my only backup, because to put all your eggs in one basket just not very smart). The keyboard made it handy to use the web apps such as Firefox and YouTube. My keyboard was a bit klunky and I'm going to pick up one with a glide pad for easier mouse/pointing.Overall very happy. The user interface is far better than with my Sony DVD and as such FireTV will get used frequently in this house.

title: My First Firestick
body: So as some of my devices I used to open streaming services with started to become outdated and no longer supported (such as old Sony Blu-ray players) apps like Hulu would disappear, and apps like YouTube would sometimes begin to crash. Assuming this is because the devices are running really old versions of the software and there's been so many front-end/back-end/ad delivery changes that these old apps just don't work anymoreSo I needed to get something that would stay up to date, and figured a popular item like the FireStick would do just that (and it did, so far!)It's better than using my TV's smart features simply because it has a far better internal NIC (or in simple terms, better WiFi signal). I like the remote it comes with, easy to use and they make sure of the play/forward/menu buttons on the virtual keyboard as well which helps make typing easier in most of the applications.One downside is that this device is so cheap because when you turn it on, it's going to feed you some advertisements around the main menus, they come in the form of tiles which can appear pretty much on any screen. They're not super annoying because you can just long hold the menu button and select apps to strictly see your installed apps but they do kind of bother me just a tad.It seems to stream in great quality, has lots of video/sound options (make sure to go through the options/preference/privacy menus) and change things to your liking. For example you can disable auto play back, adjust to a specific resolution, force HDR for HDR supported televisions, and even match frame rate for amazon prime video app (apparently only very few apps support frame rate matching - it's an ok feature)The HDMI extender provided will be necessary to use for a lot of people who have samsung televisions because otherwise it will block the smart connect HDMI ports for your other HDMI devices. Thankfully it comes included, looks clunky but it works.Pretty much any streaming services you can think of (TV ones included) is supported on the fire stick and should be supported for a long time. This works best if you have an amazon prime account too because amazon prime video is the 'main' feature, and I can only assume amazon prime video will get bigger over time since I've been seeing them offer renting of full theatrical releases which I don't mind at all because personally I prefer to watch movies at home, compared to a theater where I hear twizzler wrappers and popcorn bags being ripped open the entire timeAfter making this purchase (which I don't entirely regret) it seems like the ROKU may have been an even better buy. But alas.. that's what reviews are for. This one gets 4/5 for me, the UI being force fed with ADs and me not being able to customize my icon tiles the exact way I want (because forced ads move their way into your app tiles) it loses a star. Also I'm not sure if the device ever 'turns off' there probably is a button combination or hidden menu settings somewhere to do a full shutdown but I've only seen restart and sleep - so it might always be on doing a small energy drain/network drain to receive app/software/firmware updates OTA while it's not in useOh and one last thing, when you use the voice recognition outside of an app and tell it to open the app and do something, sometimes it'll show a blank screen - it does the action but the device goes to sleep for some reason. For example: In the main menu talk to it and go "Look up flowers on youtube", it'll open youtube and search for flowers but make the screen blank (device goes to sleep?) if you turn it back on then it'll be on youtube with flowers in the search bar.. just a weird bug I guess. Not sure where to even report bugs/issues for amazon products

title: Love the upgraded remote
body: I ordered this product to replace my 2018 Fire Stick. I have been having increasing issues with the old Fire Stick especially with no sound on certain apps, video and audio being out of sync and some app functions not working. While some of these issues may have been within the apps themselves, I found that the quickest resolution was to restart the Fire Stick. However, the issues would always return sometimes weeks later or the next day and occasionally I would have to reset my old stick to Factory Default. After multiple attempts to troubleshoot the problem, I was left with either live with it or buy a new Fire Stick. I got a really good deal as a Prime member so I opted to buy a new one, at least for the TV I use most often.Firstly, the stick is slightly larger than the old stick. Both length and width but less than an inch. Something to keep in mind if the space behind your TV is tight.As for the new stick, I think the video quality is slightly better, although it is hard to tell without a side by side comparison. We have had the new stick for a couple of weeks now and although it didn't resolve all the issues with functions within the apps, I did notice that my inability to access a show's episode list on CBS All Access did finally resolve the other day so maybe it is getting better updates. It has seemed to fix the issues with no sound when I open an app or the video and audio being out of sync. At least, I haven't had those problems since I got the new one. However, it does have a new issue that the old one didn't have. I've noticed that, occasionally, when I pause a video, especially for an extended period of time (like to make lunch not just run to the bathroom), the video "stutters" when I resume playing. It's not that the video and audio are out of sync or that the video is buffering, more like the playback is "jerky". It doesn't skip or pause, per say, it's more like if you were watching a VCR tape (remember those?) and the tape got stuck for just a split second. It is super annoying and makes the video hard to watch, almost giving me a headache. Exiting out of the app and then going back into the video doesn't help. However, it is easily fixed by restarting the Fire Stick (Settings>My Fire TV>Restart) and as I said, it only seems to happen when I pause a video for an extended period of time, more than ~5 minutes. This is why I only gave it 4 stars.I didn't rate the Voice Recognition or the Alexa Compatibility because I haven't used those functions yet. I tend to forget I can use my voice since I grew up in an era where my dad's version of remote control was telling me to get up and change the channel. Although, I plan to give it a try and I will update this review when I do so.Speaking of remotes, my favorite thing about the new Fire Stick is the remote. The old stick's remote had voice recognition but couldn't control functions on my TV like Power and Volume. So I had to use my TV's remote to turn it on and off, adjust the volume, and mute. The new Fire Stick remote has all those functions and even better it immediately recognized my TV (I didn't have to spend 1/2 hour trying different codes to program it like you used to). BTW, I have a Sony LED TV but it is not a Smart TV. I love having only one remote to control the TV again! I am anxious to try to connect it to Alexa and see if I can use my Echo Dot to control the TV. Although, I'll have to remember I can do that instead of just grabbing the remote.Overall, I recommend the 4K Fire Stick over other Fire Sticks if for no other reason than the remote's functionality.

title: Very good for the price! Only 1 small con
body: I purchased 3 of these altogether after buying 1 and trying it out and liking it for the most part. Mostly pro's here but a few cons. I never purchased a fire stick in the past due to their limited hardware and I never was a stream freak but for years I streamed music and movies locally from pc to pc using windows smb and kodi media center for htpc client in the living room. This year I remodelled the living room completely and parted ways with the laptop, 3dtv, and 5.1 surround sound. With new walls, flooring, and new furniture I decided to get a new tv and hanging shelf and do away with anything clunky and un-used. I purchased a Samsung QLED Q9F 65" and wall mounted it above a hanging shelf and around that time I noticed as I was doing some shopping on Amazon that this new 4k fire stick had just released and it could run kodi with not much effort and handle 4k streams. My tv already had lots of online streaming apps but afaik not capable of running kodi or if possible it could I still didn't want to use a 3rd party app to download and install it on the tv memory so I chose the fire stick. All the online streaming apps run great and so does Kodi. The only con is that it doesn't have a good enough gpu and/or cpu to handle 4k remux or complete 4k bluray iso files without skipping. I had to encode to crf 17 and I think at that resolution the highest bitrate that works is 30MB/s or less. It bothers me that it is sooo close to being perfect but for the price it is totally worth it. It will play full 1080p remux and bluray img files flawlessly it's just that it wont quite do lossless 4k. It does however handle atmos 7.1 with no issues as far as I can tell and HDR as well. It also pairs well with my tv for the fact that it is almost the same remote in size and by design and for most functions it will even serve as the tv remote for almost everything. If you are bothered by the volume control on the Q9F remote then it helps to use the fire stick remote to turn the volume up or down instead of using bixby voice to do it in increments that you set. I havn't tested the wifi due to purchasing the ethernet jack but I do run a 5ghz wifi network and it should perform well due to being close to the access point but with local streaming I've always preferred wired. I'm amazed at what it does handle due to being small in size and fairly cheap in price but a future 4k fire box is in need of for anybody serious about lossless streaming. I'd say 8gb of ram/quad core intel cpu/gpu capable of rendering 4k res at full bitrate/and built in gigabit or 10gig ethernet and it should be able to monster everything around. In the meantime if you are running kodi from a pc for 3d and/or 1080p and you are looking to do 4k, then stick to pc with a 1080ti or better and lossless 4k aint a problem, but otherwise encoded 4k movies using profile main 10 with a crf of 17 will run perfect throughout the entire movie on this 4k fire stick. It is easy to setup hardware wise and during the on screen setup it's very straight forward. I'm only giving it 4 stars cause I think it should've had more ram, more memory for apps and storage considering solid state and thumb drives store so much in a small form factor these days, and gigabit should've been fit to the device directly and/or at least offered in addon adaptor. Even with a slightly higher price these perks could've put it at the top of the streaming tech race but if you don't have a fire stick at all and you have amazon prime, netflix, 4k movies of your own and time to encode them then don't hesitate to buy. It's totally worth it at it's current price.

title: Went to Roku and came back due to better Plex App player support
body: First off, I had the previous iteration of this device, the Fire TV Pendent. It worked pretty well at the time, but with this upgrade, a major selling point for me was the remote and the faster CPU. Once I plugged the device in and fired it up and got it configured, I could definitely see a major speed increase over the Pendent (which wasn't that much of slouch to begin with).Set up was easy, but initial will take some time as it also updates the device to the latest software. Once that is done, it will walk you through steps to integrate the remote with your TV. If it works, you'll have a remote for the Fire TV as well as your TV where you can power on and off your tv, control the volume of the TV, and if compatible with your TV, you can use the voice control to switch from one HDMI port to another as well as to your cable port if you have cable or a digital antenna, which is what I have as we dumped cable years ago.My surround set up is a bit different. I have a DTS decoder hooked up to a set of 5.1 surround sound speakers but it only has an optical input so I have to attach the TV optical out to the decoder's optical input port. But it works. But, I have no way to control the decoder unfortunately, so I still have to have 2 remotes: 1 for the Fire TV and the other for the decoder (for volume control, etc). But, that's better than when I had to have 3 remotes. However, another draw-back is there is no way to switch channels on your TV that I have found without the use of the actual TV remote, so you'll still want to keep that handy.But, overall, this device is a great upgrade if you have the previous incantation of the Fire TV. Picture quality is excellent and surround sound is excellent. The reason I chose this over the Roku Streaming Stick+ (which we have in our living room TV) is I found that the Plex Player app on the Fire TV device handles DTS whereas on the Roku, it doesn't. No sound whatsoever. It won't down-mix or anything even though my decoder handles DTS. This is with Direct Play turned on.With the Fire TV device, it works just fine. Also, with the Fire TV Plex Player app, it is much more configurable. You can have it handle either optical or HDMI pass-through. Roku doesn't give you this option. And since my TV only has an optical out, that is my only option to get it hooked up to pass the DTS signal through to the decoder.My only other gripe (and I've done all I can to fix it), is that WiFi streaming will stop after an hour or so and will require a device restart to fix it. No other devices are affected by it and the router is a Netgear Nighthawk R7000 (AC1900). It handles the correct 5ghz WiFi protocol for the Fire TV 4K stick and yet, unless I have the Fire TV stick hooked up to the LAN adapter dongle, I'll get issues where the network connection over WiFi (2.4ghz as well as 5ghz) will go out after about an hour or so. The Roku does NOT experience this issue nor any other device hooked up to the router. But, since I do a lot of watching my Blu-Ray collection on my Plex media server, I find that the Fire TV device handles every one of my movies without issue (whether it's AC3 or DTS audio). The Roku, not so much. Some movies it refuses to even stream or others no sound (whether it's through the TV or through my DTS decoder). Definitely Roku's software seems to be behind the times as it's hardware might be as well.

title: Super value at the sale price. I prefer my Roku Ultra, but only incrementally.
body: I bought this to upgrade an old Vizio (semi-)smart set because the Vizio performance was glacial at best. When I saw the Fire TV Stick on a half price sale it was a simple impulse buy. Two days later, I plugged it in, logged into WiFi, Amazon, MLB, YouTube and YouTube TV and I was up and running in mere minutes. Its performance is on par with my Roku. Although it would only work on the 2.4 band because of the distance to the router, the streaming performance is excellent.I really like that I can access all of my commonly used functions on the Stick's remote control, including power toggle and set volume. This means that the Vizio's remote can now live in a drawer somewhere until the batteries explode. Remote functionality is also on par with my Roku. I love the fact that the Stick's remote doesn't have dedicated streaming service buttons like the Roku which gives it a cleaner look. I also don't subscribe to any of the Roku's four dedicated buttons' services, but I digress. The Roku remote does have a headphone jack that I have occasionally found helpful but I don't miss having it with the Stick. I do, however, love the fact that the Stick's remote has a mute button. Roku has volume but no mute. I have not enabled Alexa and do not intend to so I cannot comment on that functionality.The Stick's user interface is Amazon product-centric which is understandable since it IS an Amazon product, so no demerits for that. I found it reasonably intuitive and easy to use but compared to Roku's UI it looks cluttered. It responds quickly to the remote's commands and it's easy to get to the desired service quickly. I was less than pleased the number of services that were pre-configured in "My Channels" in much the same way that PCs come with a bunch of pre-installed applications. But removing them was simple.Where I prefer the Fire TV Stick over the Roku Ultra is the fact that it's a stick and requires no real estate which is helpful where space is limited and especially for wall-mounted televisions. I honestly don't see the need today for a streaming "box". Sure my Roku has a USB port which is never used because I can stream my devices directly to both the Roku and Stick. So, for me, the stick form factor is preferable as it's functionality is equivalent to Roku, but hides completely out of sight.The Fire TV Stick does everything I need in a streaming device. It's quick performance, broad range of available streaming services, image quality and form factor all get high marks and I highly recommend it, especially at a price of about US$25 on sale. While there are some peripheral things that I prefer with the Roku, if I needed to buy another streaming device tomorrow I would get another Fire TV Stick without a second thought. Highly recommended. Buy it. Get two. They're small.

title: Way better than previous generations
body: Have a first gen firetv box, first gen tv stick and 2 4k firesticks. The 4k sticks are very fast, millisecond lag on loading, no buffering and excellent picture. The 4k sticks have a better picture and quicker load times than the old firetv box, also so far neither 4k stick has frozen up like the old firetv box occasionally does. 4k Firestick way faster than first gen stick 6 second load times and blu ray player 20 sec+ load times. Using tp link c9 router with 100 meg service. All devices within 60 feet of router.Initial setup was a mixed bag. 4k stick automatically detected my network and prime account (nice) but would not proceed without trying to detect tv type. Could not skip this part (4k stick directly into HT receiver therefore no sound from tv). Seems like Amazon designers assume you want to give full control to firestick over your HT system. Had to go through many settings to turn off automatic control of other equipment. IMO too much tweaking for a simple addition of a streaming device to a HT system. I just want it to display video content.Also a problem was that on my recent purchase, the first stick that was shipped was defective. The remote would not turn on the firestick after being off a few hours. Tried directly into 2 other tvs besides HT with same result. To get a replacement was a complete hassle. Could not accomplish online, input information and progressed steps and submitted only to get back to the start of replacement request. Had to request a call from a rep and spend like 20 minutes explaining the problem, answering technical questions and giving account information. Rep was doing his job and cordial but crappy situation. Almost an hour spent to replace a defective firestick?Alexa integration? She has trouble answering many questions, would not trust to integrate at this point.Handy tool for basic, direct questions is useful but limited.I don't use voice recognition.Conclusion? 4k firestick is a very fast loading device, it's tiny dimensions hide behind everything, with a great stutter free picture. Curiously, 4k labelled content when paused displays 1080p on left above time progression bar. A non issue for me because my 4k tv picture is stunning no matter what the 4kfirestick is playing.The 4k firestick on an older 1080p has a great picture as well despite lower resolution.Be prepared to spend time setting up and going through the menu settings. A lot of default on settings I turned to off, like data collection. Big advantage if you are already a prime member with a firetv device. Could be smoother, more streamlined.Quality control or product quality could be an issue forcing a similar customer service experience that was not smooth for me.Basically a great product that could be excellent.

title: Almost perfect but have found a couple quirks that are off putting.
body: Ok. This latest Firestick hands down has better streaming quality than our Roku device for the same money and having the Alexa integration is a bonus as well as having TV volume and power control once the unit is synced to our Samsung Smart that is a few years old. The only reason I did not give it 5 stars was that the home button, in my opinion, doesn't really do anything when you tap it. You have to use the back button repeatedly to actually get back to the home screen that appears when you initially start the unit. The only thing the home button is good for is when holding it down for a few seconds, a munu if all your downloaded apps appears. Ok, I'm not a software engineer, but home should mean "home" like the Roku is. And this is something minor but noteworthy, my wireless keyboard that is paired with my TV will not work with the Firestick. It does with the Roku and with other functions of the Samsung TV such as it's built in browser etc. I can't stand searching or inputting usernames and passwords with the on screen keyboard and toggle with the remote. Though it has Alexa voice recognition, there are times when an actual keyboard is more efficient and preferred.Lastly, it would be great if, when using the Firestick remote to turn on the TV, that it defaults to the Firestick as the source to use upon powering on. However, the TV will turn on and go to the source menu as it would if I was using the factory remote. So, I must then grab that factory remote to select the source for the Firestick and launch. It's just and extra added step that could be eliminated. If it can't be automatically selected by using the Firestick remote to power on, then at least put a source button on the Firestick remote so I don't have to fumble with two of them. Oh, First World problems.Btw, the Alexa voice remote does not functiin like your Echo devices only in the sense that the remote does not have a wake word nor does it play nice with your Echo devices, I have found. Meaning, that the a remote button for Alexa to wake and listen must be held down in or for her to do anything with the Firestick. You cannot just simply say "Alexa" to wake her, nor do the Echo devices control the Firestick. Unless I am missing something, the Firestick acts as its own encapsulated Amazon device separate from the others.Other than those improvements needed, the picture quality and endless variety of content available to stream make it a hit even even seems to improve the picture quality of my non 4K HD TV much like a Blu-ray player improves the quality of a standard DVD. I'm still very happy with the purchase but wish there were some refinements.

title: Fire TV Stick 4 K Review
body: The Fire TV Stick 4K works great. The most common problems people experience are:1. HDMI Extender (Included) - Use this because it is also an antenna and helps with Wi-Fi reception. Wi-Fi 2G is stronger and 5G is faster. If you are having Wi-Fi problems use 2G.2. Power Cord (Included) - The Fire TV Stick is designed to work with 110 wall outlet. If you try to power it from the TV's HDMI port and USB port it is like a power failure and reboots every time the TV is turned on. On a 110 wall outlet it goes to sleep and wakes up.3. Go to Settings, Display and change to Original Frame Rate this decreases artifacts and HDR to Adaptive or the display is wrong sometimesAll HDMI ports are not the same. For example my Vizio TV has five (5) HDMI ports but they are different.1. 4K at 30 FPS (Frames Per Second)2. 4K at 30 FPS3. 4K at 30 FPS4. No HDCP (High Bandwidth Content Protection)5. 4K at 60 FPS HDCP 2.2Only HDMI port 5 has 4K at 60 FPS and HDCP 2.2I see a lot of people are unable to change the HDMI input. Press the microphone button and and say "Change to HDMI 1, 2, 3, 4, and 5". This works on some TVs.I have some concerns and problems with the design.PlugI have four TVs and two of the TVs are wall mounts. When the house was built there is a recessed outlet with two plugs inside the wall mount behind the TV. The Fire TV plug is to wide to fit in the outlet and the height is to tall and blocks the other outlet. Maybe its time to go to a box design and incorporate the antenna, power supply and the stick inside a box and use a regular HDMI cable and plug. The plug and stick are to big.RemoteThe ring on the remote to move top, bottom, left and right. Place an X on it to identify there are four areas to press. There should be lines in the ring at 1:30, 4:30, 7:30, and 10:30 on a clock face. This would make it easier to use.The circle inside the ring should have Press OK or OK on it. This would make it easier to use.StickThe new stick is bigger and heavier. If you use the HDMI Extender (antenna) it adds more weight. On my Vizio TVs (4) the HDMI Ports are on the back and the side. On the back of the TV the Stick is to big and long to fit behind a wall mount. I have to use the HDMI Extender. My HDMIs point down. The weight of the HDMI extender, Stick, and gravity creates a loose connection. In some instances the weight of the TV is on the stick because the TV is at an angle. There is a big space at the top and a small space at the bottom. On the side of the TV the Stick extends beyond the TV.

title: How well it works compared to others
body: Ok, so if your like me you wonder what exactly does Fire Stick do? Well 1st. saves you a lot of money Compared to having say, a Genie with 5 tv's hooked up. 2nd. gives you the option of more, lots more to watch, may even be overwhelming in choices. 3rd. Less HDMI cables to purchase. So lets get to what it really does, gives you apps like Prime video, Netflix, Hulu, and too many channels to list here. That's I think why they don't. Get every channel including many local channels that you can think of, like TNT,SYFY, PBS and so on. HBO, Starz, and those channels like Netflix are at a cost but add it up and you will probably, more then likely save a lot over cable or satellite. Sports are a little different as far as some places have, local sports channels you can get included in your cable package. This you will have to pay for unless your watching live on local channels. Now what's different between Fire tv and Roku Ultra besides the price? No VUDU on Fire tv, which if you are like me I have movies on there for yrs. Anyway no HDMI cable is needed for Fire tv it plugs straight in to your TV, Receiver or equipment like an OPPO Blue-ray player and so on. The two are the same except those four things. VUDU, HDMI cables, and price. each have quad-core processors, 4k at 60fps. and Voice control. Fire stick seems to preform very well if you hook it up correctly. So in the short of it go with FireTV stick, if you want to spend far less depending on how many TV's you have, and you like ALexa voice control. If you don't mind paying more and want things like VUDU go with the other. You could do like I did because we have 4 people watching TV's to get both. Each give you two people at a time watching other channels or devices. I have two FIRETV Sticks and two ROKU's. 2 are straight into TV's for full 4k at 60fps, 1 straight through my receiver and another through my OPPO 105D which give me 4k at 30fps. True 4k at 60fps is very rare out there yet so most equipment that's just a few yrs. older will still give you 4k but at 30fps. I personally don't see a difference but it's hard to find a show that is recorded at a true 4k 60fps. as of NOV. 2018. Oh I missed something important to me but not others as much which is ROKU Ultra has a net cable connector and wi-fi which FIRETV does not. I like the fact that there is never a problem with connection or speed with a net cable versus wireless, plus you don't have to type in your security code with a net cable connection to setup.

title: Seems to need a reboot to speed functionality up, once in awhile...
body: Let me begin by stating that I have been an Amazon Fire TV user for many years since the launch of the original Fire TV device. The device has always served its purpose well and offered incredibly easy functionality for all ages...When I started to have speed issued with the Fire Stick generation prior to the 4K version, I decided to look into purchasing a new Fire Stick better suited for my needs. I began browsing Amazon and located this product. I immediately purchased it when I saw that it offered 4K quality on qualifying movies and shows on account of owning a 4K television.When I received the device, the packaging was practical and hassle free. The set-up process was very quick and streamlined much like the previous Fire Stick generation.One of my favorite features of the new generation Fire Stick is the support of 5GHZ networks. This allows for quicker streaming and less connectivity issues experiences sometimes when connected to 2GHZ networks.I have owned this new Fire Stick for approximately 4 months now and I have only one complaint. The device has begun to run a lot slower than it was out of the box. Now I know that some slowing is expected the older the device gets and the more that is downloaded on the device. However, I have not downloaded anything more than Netflix, Hulu, NFL Sunday Ticket, NHL, NBA, Youtube, and Youtube TV. I have no personal videos or pictures on the device so there's nothing other than just the apps...To detail what I mean, there seems to be an increased wait time when navigating from app to app and when pressing the home button. Sometimes when I press the home button when coming out of an app, I will be staring at a blank blue screen as the Fire Stick populates all of my apps and the other items usually seen on the main home screen. This issue tends to be fixed by a simple restart of the Fire Stick but I really wish I didn't have to do that due to the time it takes.Other than that issue from time to time, it works great and offers endless hours of streaming for all types of households. I also love how Amazon keeps adding more functionality and useful features to the Fire Stick.I would recommend this product to anyone with a wireless internet connection and 4K television. If you don't have a 4K television, I would stick with the older generation.

title: The remote controls your TV on this one.
body: Update: I've had the stick for 4 months now. There is 1 big problem. The remote turns itself off after not using it for a few minutes. It saves batteries. Previous models didn't do this & you'd have to change batteries often. Because the remotes are working off Bluetooth instead of infrared like most remotes. The problem with it disconnecting is I sit here every single time hitting buttons. Nothing happens. Takes like 20 or 30 seconds to reconnect. So if I want to pause, I can't when I want to. If I want to play the next episode as the 1st is counting down, I can't. It's great that I am on the same set of batteries, but I had already got rechargeable batteries for my other fire sticks. I'm used to changing batteries every few weeks. Now back to my original review..I have five fire sticks. That's right. You heard me, I have 5. I've retired some. But I have one in each room. I got two of these 4K sticks. I got them on Black Friday. I was under the impression that these particular sticks are faster then the previous model. I was under the impression they were an upgrade and maybe had more features. I think it's fair to say this is not true. In fact like most things these days this newer model is missing features. If you want to screen mirror from your phone, you can no longer do that. It is not any faster. I see no new features. Alexa works the same as it always did. You can still sideload apps obviously. The only major difference is during the setup process, the stick knows what kind of TV you have. It asks you to turn the volume button up & miraculously you can control your TV with it. I can't speak for every TV but I have Sony TVs. The remote will turn the TV on and work the volume. If you are only using the fire TV stick and don't need to switch HDMI ports, than this remote is all you need. That alone makes the stick worth it. You don't need a 4K TV but if you do have one then you can get the 4K. However, if you want the 4K then you have to pay dearly for it. You will have to upgrade your Netflix. Netflix will charge you out the butt. You might see a difference. Reality is, you probably won't. But you'll feel better about it cuz you paid a lot of money and it's telling you you're getting 4K. Personally, I dropped my Netflix down to SD. I only get 480p & it looks just as good as it did when it was 1080. But what do I know? Lol.

title: Why stop on 3rd ? You clearly had a home run !
body: Had a Roku 3 and thought it was great until i tried to side load apps. Roku doesn't want you doing that. Well dammit........ok, I have ro buy another streaming device. Not wanting to spend big bucks I opted for the firestick and let's make it a 4k....hahaha, rite (sarcasm). Well, to my surprise the firestick blew the Roku away. I could side load 3rd party apps, use the internet, stream video without buffering in 1080p and in the limited 4k content. Wow, the 4k looks really good too. Did I mention the tiny footprint? You can't even see the firestick, it takes up 0 space since it connects on the back side of the tv, completely hidden from view. All is good, better than good, but wait a minute. I don't think I have many apps loaded on the firestick but it is full. What the &%$@!!, this thing should have 16 gigs min. not 8 gigs. 32 gigs would be even better. How can they have made this blunder! I don't know if there is a way to remove the bloatware but that would give 2 or 3 gigs that the firestick desperately needs. Seriously, that is the only flaw I found and it's a big un. My review wouldn't be complete unless I mentioned that the remote is very well thought out for a minimal / simple device. You can get away with no keyboard most of the time by using the voice to text feature. The firestick does a decent job dictating. The batteries last a long time and you don't have to point the remote directly at the tv to get it to work like my tv remote. The price has dropped over the last year and is a bargain that will satisfy your average user.Update: I decided to drop the rating from 4 stars to 3 stars due to Amazon removing the cast feature for Windows PC. Amazon supported casting from Windows devices in the past and for some unknown reason they pulled the plug. The only work around for Windows 10 devices is to use the "connect " feature available through the notification panel (lower right corner} of the window 10 desktop. The default setting is to duplicate the screens, however you can change this to extend display, which allows you to keep working on the pc screen while watching a movie on the tv screen. Basically you have 2 monitors. This is the only work around. since no 3rd party apps will work. Booo Amazon, Yaaaa Windows.Update: Back to 4 stars. You can cast !! yaaa

title: Still some room for improvement of the 4K Stick
body: I purchased 2 AFTV 4K Sticks as I use them with other pieces of TV gear such as a MythTV DVR connected to 2 HDHomeRun Extend Over The Air Tuners which allows me to record or watch up to 4 OTA Channels at once from our local market. Some of these channels still are sending 1080i and 480i Interlaced video, and most devices will not provide any deinterlacing in the actual hardware device, but the AFTV 4K Sticks are suppose to now provide that function. Overall, the deinterlacing function works pretty well. I was using Kodi as my front end software with the Mythtv Addon to watch live and recorded local channels on the AFTV Devices and performing the deinterlacing within the Kodi software which did a fair job as well. It's not perfect as I can still see some interlacing and jitter in the picture, but it's a little better than the software deinterlacing I was using. The AFTV 4K Stick does seem much more responsive than my prior AFTV Gen 2 Stick and surprisingly, even a little snappier than my AFTV Gen 2 BOX which was a real surprise. I also subscribe to Playstation Vue Access level which provides several "Cable" channels that seem to work well. I did notice however that when watching Playstation Vue on channels like Fox News Channel, Fox Business Channel, etc where there is a streaming "Ticker Tape" at the top of bottom of the screen, the right to left motion of the ticker is extremely jittery and jumpy as is the associated video. At first I thought it was my eyes, but when I put either of my original Gen 2 devices back into service and compared the video, the AFTV 4K Stick had a very pronounced Jitter which is very odd because I believe Playstation Vue ONLY outputs a 720P video stream, so it should be smooth as butter all the time, and it IS on the old Gen 2 AFTV devices, but rather jumpy on the new AFTV 4K Stick. Hopefully either Amazon or Playstation can address this issue soon because it is a bit hard to watch. I also tried watching the Fox News Channel and Fox Business Channel AFTV Applications, and the video and scrolling tickers are always smooth as butter on all of the AFTV Devices, so I'm not sure what is going on with that. Anyway, I got them on sale on Prime Day, so If I get tired of them, I can always sell them used and get my money back and move on to something else.

title: Too annoying to be used - updated 3 to 4
body: I was fully prepared to love the 4K Fire Stick when it arrived but sadly I can’t say I do. Certainly it has some great aspects such as excellent wi-fi range and speed, ease of use, and a great remote with TV power and volume controls. But there are fatal flaws in mine, most annoying of which are audio sync problems. Upon first seeing a significant sync problem I found a solution presented online. Simply set the “audio” settings of the device to “stereo” instead of allowing it automatically select among the available Dolby settings. Limiting the features included just to make it work isn’t exactly ideal but I could live with that. However we still get less severe audio sync issues, just enough to be annoying. It comes and goes. No fix found. There is also a problem with exiting Amazon Music and going to streaming live shows. No matter how I exit Music, when I open PSVue (our streaming provider) and play a show if we pause the video the last music we were playing restarts – loudly. Start the video and the music stops. It can be stopped by going into “Apps” and doing a forced stop on “Music” but this is temporary and will return. It can also be stopped by going into “Prime Video” and playing a few seconds of any show and then going to Vue. Again temporary and way too many clicks to be acceptable.I still have an old semi-obsolete Roku3 that I hooked up to another HDMI input on the same set, using the same wi-fi. There is no audio sync problem and there is no problem with “Music” not closing. And yes, I have swapped the units between the two HDMI inputs with no change. I’m now looking at a newer Roku with an expanded remote. More money but hopefully less annoying.UPDATE 9/25. I kept playing with my setup and found that not all my HDMI inputs were created equal. Most were listed as HDMI input with HDCP, ARC but one is just listed as HDMI input with HDCP. I moved the FireStick4k to that input and I have not seen any audio sync problem in a week (audio still set to Stereo only.) This problem was apparently due to my setup and not the product. It still has the problem closing the Music app but this is not that bad and we are using this adapter full time now. Raised review from 3 stars to 4 stars.

title: better than i expected
body: i have a smart TV with many of the apps that the fire stick offers, but i bought one of these anyhow for some expanded integration and because gadgets are fun... and also, it was on sale. :)things i didnt expect:+ the fire tv app is much faster than, say, my comcast guide. its awesome.+ individual app responsiveness is significantly faster than the same app on my smart tv. (this isnt terribly surprising, but the amount of the difference really was.)+ streaming on all apps is far more efficient through this device than through my tv apps.+ by accident, i realized that when i did a voice-search for something, this device checked every available service i had. that means that i can just speak, for instance, "inception," and it will search hbo now, hulu, netflix, amazon prime, disney+, and everything else i have connected to it - and show me which services have it, then allow me to click which to play, launch that service, and go. much improved over my manual check of each service before!two cons, which is why its 4-star and not 5:- third party apps (hbo now, hulu, netflix, disney+) time out back to fire tv home screen a lot faster than they should, IMO. if i pause a show to answer a phone call or answer the door, it shouldnt kick me back out to the fire tv home screen so quickly.- some of the apps have some big improvement to do around streamlining their session maintenance -- like, some services are great at understanding youre the same tv that was halfway through watching a particular movie two hours ago, and putting that content front and center for you when you jump back into that app. other services (im looking at you, hulu) make you start over every time: select your profile, go through the main menu, and locate the same content again to resume it. i expect thats entirely the individual app developer's decision, but i would appreciate if amazon put some pressure on their major app partners to make things more delightful to their users... especially in light of the fire tv's behavior on my last point with fast app timeouts. its a bit irritating, especially when binge watching and pausing to go to the kitchen for a few minutes.

title: Supports 4k and surround sound from most apps where available
body: ProsStreaming services that provide 4k, HD, UHD, Dolby 5.1 or DTS just work.Supports HDCP, although it should require 2.2, at least in my case, older HDCP devices still work. (YMMV)Reasonably useful interface for accessing apps like Netflix, Disney+, and others.Prime video interface works well.Access to apps on road trips.ConsThe interface is a bit awkward and makes it difficult to see genres across multiple services.Seems to have ad supported suggestions?Data collection, privacy concerns."Free to me" includes apps with ads, not just my subscribed services - needs a subscribed services tab.Firestick is always on.Does not turn off signal after you stop watching and the screen saver goes dark.This is the biggest con for me, as I have ancillary equipment that I have been using the TV as master with a smart power strip that turns off all the equipment when the TV turns off. By setting the TV to turn off after 5 minutes with no signal, game consoles, Blu-ray players, and others will stop signal, which in turn allows the TV to turn off and power off all the rest of my equipment. I have two setups like this and it works great from any other source except the firestick, or if you actually power off the TV. Unfortunately, if someone falls asleep after watching the firestick, they wake up to find everything still powered on. I have exhaustively researched this and it is a limitation of the fire stick hardware with no viable solution. I suspect this was an early design decision because they anticipated the user plugging the power into the TV's USB port. That doesn't work with most setups as it's not enough power, so there is little reason for this "feature", and in those situations, the remote would power off the TV (and in turn the firestick) anyway.I still use this as my primary way to watch TV, as it allows me 4k and surround sound with all my apps with one device for a reasonable price. It's also a convenient device to watch movies in the back yard on a projector or to bring on trips, although hotel TVs are getting more and more difficult to drive from an external device...

title: Love these, but all amazon devices need a little tweaking
body: I love these devices. They are amazing and connected to the Recast and a streaming service, we more than we had with cable and TiVo all at a much lower price. Combine this with the power and volume buttons on the remote and the integrated Recast DVR (500 gig multi-room TiVo device) make this well worth the price. Using Alexa on the remote for your smart home devices is icing on the cake. Even with our SmartSmsung TV we use this for DirecTV Now (AT&T) as the Samsung app has problems. It is unfortunate that it does not work with YouTube TV yet. It is important to get this released as soon as possible.One minor complaint is more a function of Alexa than the Fire Stick. There does need to be some better identification of the target device for Alexa. If you link your Echo Dot to the Fire Stick, any request to play something defaults to the TV, even if you just wanted to play something on Echo. Alexa also has no concept of multiple locations tied to the same account. If you are lucky enough to have a second location, Alexa can't isolate the devices and commands for each location based upon where you currently are.I don't think Amazon has grasped how well these and the Echo devices have been accepted. If they rest on their laurels, they could get passed by as other companies are at their heels. One major shortcoming is not being able to rename the device. No, not in the Alexa app, but to the network (to your router). We use these and Echos throughout the house. The more you use these, the more of them you have, the harder it is to identify which one you need to work. With 4 FireSticks, Recast, and 4 Dots, which amazon-xxxxxxx device did not reconnect to the network. Every other smart device allows you to change its name and the Amazon engineers just haven't figured out how to do this. It is not the function of the router to define the name of the device; it is up to Amazon to figure out.Overall we are drinking the Amazon Kool-Aide and we are very happy. I highly recommend these and installed all of these same things in my brother's house. Amazon - please let me change the host name.

title: Remote not compatible with projectors...
body: After an hour of frustration trying to get through the remote control setup for my ViewSonic projector, trying various different IR profile numbers and codes and searching on the web and reviews for answers I finally called Amazon support through the “call me” feature on the order itself. That was great and they called me right away and connected remotely to my $25 fire tv stick 4K (half price on Amazon day) to trouble-shoot and eventually came to the conclusion that the fire stick Remote is not compatible with any projectors. In other words you can’t control the volume (and I guess a couple other things such as on/off) with it. Not the end of the world for me since I plan to hook the sound up to a sound bar or receiver eventually anyway (and I can always use the projectors remote for the projector volume/mute/on/off) but it’s more remotes to juggle and it was VERY frustrating that the fire stick setup FORCES you to configure the remote for volume for some device And projectors are not supported and there is no easy way to bypass that part of the set up. So the workaround is just to lie and say the remote works with a code that it really didn’t work for to get past it. You can come back later to the settings and change it of course.Other beef was that I also had the problem many other people had with prying the back cover of the remote off to install the batteries. I tried several small and tiny screwdrivers but that didn’t work and ended up scratching up the back of my brand new remote. I finally found a razor blade and was able to Wedge that in the crack and pry it open enough to get a little screwdriver in there to get it all the way open. Obviously a design/manufacturing flaw that’s not easy to change now for Amazon but I never had that much trouble putting batteries in hundreds of other remotes...On a positive note for $25 (including 2-day prime shipping) - and a wasted hour of frustration, I turned my LCD projector into a Smart TV, and with the latest technology. And the talk to Alexa feature on the remote is pretty cool. Not sure how much I’ll actually use it though.

title: Better value than Apple TV
body: Generally, I have Apple products. iPhone, MacBook, iMac, and an older version of the Apple TV. I also own a Roku and have used my family’s Fire TV, so I felt like I had a pretty good idea of what things I did and didn’t like about all of the aforementioned devices. Despite being an “Apple” person, I decided to go with the Fire Stickand, aside from a few hiccups, I’m generally very pleased with it for the cost.Likes:-Alexa integration. I wasn’t sure I would really use it but I actually like the voice commands and it works well, even switching from the Fire Stick to my cable TV.-Cost. Admittedly, this was a big factor in deciding between this and the Apple TV. The latter is three times the cost of the Fire Stick and, since I am not a fan of the Apple TV interface nor do I really utilize iTunes, this one was an easy sell for me.-User Interface. It sounds kind of silly, but I like the Amazon layout on the home screen and the way the menus are divided.Dislike:-HDR setup. I actually like setting up electronic things and even I found this to be a bit tedious. Our TVs and other devices are so advanced at this point that it takes a NASA scientist to get them to work well, and this was no exception. Initially, when I first hooked up the device, I thought the picture for most of the apps and in general was terrible. Everything was too dark and the colors really just didn’t pop as well as they did when I watched through the apps on my Sony Bravia TV (Netflix, etc.) Since my TV is a few years old now, perhaps it does not have some of the best technology but it is 4K. For me, the fix was turning on ”adaptive HDR.” I still had to mess with quite a few settings on my actual TV to make things look how I was accustomed to them looking, but I am now pleased with the results.Overall, I think this is a great device for the cost if you’re not a dedicated iOS user and utilize Prime Video to watch or rent movies, stream add on channels like HBO, etc. I do like Roku as well, but I’d say I probably prefer the Fire Stick if I had to choose.

title: Solid performance in streaming movies
body: My main objective in purchasing this device was to eliminate the use of the the T.V. Remote and have full control of the set with Alexa. Setup wasn’t complicated following the instructions provided and YouTube videos. I made the connection to a 54” Panasonic which did not have internet connection. Upon completing the setup I was able to control volume, turn off the set and switch streaming service such as YouTube and HBO Max with Alexa commands, but unable to turn the set on using Alexa. After trying unsuccessfully to solve the power on issue I decided to contact Amazon Tech Support. First call did not solve the problem but I was appreciative of the effort made by the technician. Afterward I received two follow up calls from Tech Support and in each call I notice that the technician had a genuine interest in solving the issue of power on. On this last call I received from Amazon the technician exalted the call to the next level which informed me that the Fire stick was not going to perform full compatibility with all sets therefore my desire to have Alexa turn on the set was unfortunately not going to happen. This was a disappointment, but considering all the effort I received from Amazon, I couldn’t be more satisfied with their service. That said I decided to dwell a littler dipper into the problem and found that Panasonic sets have a option under its setup call VIERA link which has to be set ON which then give you access to activate the Power On and Power Off link. Hope this helps others suffering the same issue.Currently I have Alexa Turing on and off the set, selecting YouTube videos with voice command, but it is important to note that you will not have full compatibility with all streaming service.For streaming movies the device delivers solid video performance with perfect sound syncs. Home menu is easily to navigate and can be configured to your liking. I am very happy with my purchase and completely satisfied with the service I received from Amazon Tech Support. My congratulations to Jeff Bezos and his team for today’s Space flight.

title: Fire Stick for Hallmark Channel & Hallmark Now
body: Update to review below:After leaving my review, I received a phone call from a gentleman by the name of Steve. He reassured me that he would pass along my frustrations to Hallmark and see if anything can be done.In addition, I would like to clarify that my frustrations are not with the fire stick, it is with Hallmark for not having a Hallmark Channel paid app and my cable provider for monopolizing what I can and cannot access.Because of Steve's call, I will most definitely be keeping the fire stick for my allowed time before returning (January 31, because of the holiday return window). I'm hoping that I will have fallen in love with the device and keep it at that point. If not, Amazon makes it easy to return if I'm not happy with the product.I have added 3 stars due to Steve's call.I will give another update after I've been able to fully use it, so stay tuned._________I thought for sure (after reading many reviews of people upset because it didn't work for them because they had cut the cord from cable TV) that I surely would be able to use the Hallmark Channel app... BECAUSE I DO STILL HAVE A CABLE PROVIDER. NOT! The Hallmark Channel app is useless unless you have the channel already from your cable provider. The response I received while trying to add is: "This network is not included in your current Xfinity TV service.To add it to your service, upgrade now."Why would I need the fire stick if I already got the channel FROM MY CABLE PROVIDER?!?!? I'm beyond frustrated because I wanted to give this as a gift for my husband whom LOVES Hallmark. Well, I can't get what I really want (which is the Christmas Countdown) because the Hallmark Now channel (different app & part of the company) doesn't play any of the same movies or programs. I'm totally willing to pay for both the Hallmark Channel AND Hallmark Now if it were possible. Beyond irritated and considering returning the fire stick because it is truly useless to me. Buyer beware if Hallmark is why you're considering the fire stick.

title: Streaming performance is GREAT!
body: I bought this to replace a Roku stick. I have this connected to my Ethernet switch using their Ethernet adapter.The performance on this stick is great. The interface is snappy. No lag. You press a button on the remote and the response is instant. The picture is actually sharper with this stick than it was using my old Roku.Setup is easy. Plug it in and it walks you through the setup process. Your account info comes already configured. All you need to do is program your WiFi info, if you use WiFi. Then, it walks you through setting up your remote. That's easy, too. Similar to Roku. Both are easy.The only thing I don't like about this device is the interface. Roku's interface is much friendlier. Roku puts all your apps in the same place, every time. So, when I turn the TV on, I know where to look for it. Amazon's interface is too cluttered with previews. I usually have to hunt for the app I am looking for. Finding new apps is also a bit hard because I have to hunt through categories, etc... I wish there was a way to simplify the interface and get rid of all that unwanted clutter. I only want a few big icons for whatever apps I use. I don't want to see a lot of small icons for things I don't care about. It's not a deal-breaker, though. But, it's gonna cost them one star, though.I have not used the voice control, Alexa integration, or WiFi. I mainly use it with HDHomeRun, Channels DVR, Philo and YouTubeTV. All of these apps run very well. If you have a good network setup, then you should have no problems streaming with this. When it goes on sale, it's definitely a bargain. I recommend the Ethernet adapter so that you won't have performance issues with wireless interference or capacity.If you are looking to cut the cord to save money, then this is the first step. Most streaming apps will work on this. YouTubeTV, Hulu with Live TV and Fubo are all there. And, if you're using an antenna, HDHomeRun, TabloTV, Channels DVR and PLEX are all supported, too!

title: Cool When It Was Working (update)
body: UPDATE 5/1/2012No warning. Black screen. It responds to the remote because responds to restart button code on remote but that's it IUPDATE: 6/26/19Having lived with the firestick for a time now I still think it is the best of the bunch especially if you have prime. What I do not like is the fact that if you can not aquire a wifi connection it is literally useless. You will only get the settings screen. No home screen or any apps just settings until you get a wifi connection. Even Ifvyou have games or in My case external storage connected that does not require wifi to function. I am in a situation that my wifi flakes out now and then and when this happens the stick becomes completely useless. I really like the firestick but this is so rediculous that you can not even get to a home screen. Extremely irritating to not be able to at least get to the home screen so I can access games and other storage. No good reason for this design. No wifi say so but let me get a home screen.I was avoiding buying a firestick because a few reviews mislead me to believe it was very limited compared to the other popular streaming stick. I bought 2 of the other before buying a firestick and kicking myself for not buying this in the first place.If you have prime this is THE choice for plug and play streaming at or away from home. I am using mine often away from home and over weak signal wifi setups and the wifi radio holds onto signals my other could not.You can also sideload apps from external sources that make the firestick even more of a value. Once you investigate a bit you will discover firestick is a much better value overall even if you do not have prime though prime adds value since the other sticks can not benefit like the firestick can.Another HUGE plus for me was my 3 year old Sony TV did not need any adapters like the other brand stick did for the HDCP crap. Just plugged this in the hdmi and it works. That alone for me was worth the price.

title: Pretty Decent. But Atmos not supported. Now includes ads
body: I think the thousands of other reviews have done a good job summarizing this product, so I won't repeat them. The FireTV 4K stick is a pretty good product. I like it, and I would probably buy it again. I'm going to list my two major complaints below, but I'm otherwise pretty happy with the product.The FireTV has RECENTLY STARTED RUNNING ADVERTISEMENTS on the screensaver. This is honestly, unacceptable. Currently, they are only for other products under the Amazon umbrella (IMDB TV), but it is an ugly direction to be moving in. This isn't supposed to be an ad supported product. People pay for the hardware. People buy content from Amazon. Stop trying to sell me more stuff, you already have all of my money. I know that there are hacks, workarounds, and third party solutions to block their advertisements. But honestly, thats a lot of extra hoops to jump through, when I shouldn't have to.My other complaint, is that although this hardware is advertised as supporting Dolby Atmos audio, it doesn't really support it, at least not in the way most users are expecting. By that, I mean that Atmos audio is NOT supported for any third party apps (like Netflix), but only Amazon's own software. This was really disappointing, as I was excited to try out Atmos, and specifically bought this product for that feature. Netflix is about the only one producing much Atmos content these days, so if you want that support, you will probably have to go with an AppleTV instead. Amazon doesn't have very much Atmos content of their own, so the feature is kind of useless. This product listing has since been updated to include an asterisk, basically to say "Has Atmos, but not really". That disclaimer wasn't there a year ago when I bought this, but I'm glad they eventually added some warning.In summary, it is a good product. But look elsewhere if you have Atmos speakers. Also, be prepared to accept the fact that they can start pushing advertising on you whenever they change their minds.

title: It keeps losing its wifi connection
body: I really like the 4k overall, but I'm having a fundamental issue with it losing track of its wifi connection, usually after a period of inactivity.This is the second firestick 4k I've had. The first one worked for about 4-5 weeks before it started dropping the wifi connection.BTW, my phone and computers do not have any wifi connection issues.Powering the 4k off and back on didn't help. I called Amazon service and they were very helpful, giving me a secret back door key combination that reset it to the factory defaults. I laboriously re-keyed in all the network info and logins to various apps. This worked for a few days, but then the 4k stopped connecting altogether. I called Amazon service again, and although the 4k was out of the 30-day return window, they allowed me to return this 4k lemon for a refund, which I did.I ordered another 4k right away, since the final season of Game of Thrones was imminent. Now, several weeks later, the new 4k often loses track of its connection overnight. Once, it dropped the connection in the middle of watching a movie. However, it can be revived by simply unplugging and replugging the power supply. I'm likely outside the 30-day warranty on this one, too, but it hasn't quit completely (yet) so I'm just watching it for the moment. I'm an unofficial beta tester, I guess.We have Xfinity wifi at home, and it's possible the wifi is rebooting itself or resetting all connections periodically, I don't know. But all my other devices are somehow able to deal with it, so why not the Firestick 4k? It seems ludicrous that this basic, well-understood function is not more fault-tolerant on the 4k. Did I get two lemons in a row? Is this a common problem with the 4k? Amazon service didn't know or wouldn't discuss it.Again, no complaints against customer service or the 4k (when it is working properly). It's a great device to add on to non-smart TVs.

title: Excellent online streaming, user interface values "glitzy" over functionality and usability
body: Easy to install and log-in, and almost as easy to install streaming apps (the Roku is a bit easier in this area). The device is reliable, has a crisp picture and works well, generally. The area that I can fault is the user interface (UI).The UI is very "glitzy" with emphasis on color, motion, sound and cramming the maximum number of choices on the screen at the same time rather than clear information on each offered program. A user wants to see several bits of information clearly and when they first browse the program: whether the content is available "free" or as a part of their Prime subscription (when looking on Prime or some other paid "channel" accessed via an app on the FireTV device), or if there will be a rental or purchase; the user also wants to know what other viewers or reviewers have thought of the content (e.g., star ratings or review ratings), its genre (crime drama, comedy, etc.), a brief synopsis of the content - hopefully with an option to "see more," the running time, and its rating (PG, PG-13, etc.). FireTV shows only some of this info in the apps I have and then it is shown only after you go down to the second level after you select a program or third level before you play the content. If you decide you don't want to play that content and "back-out" of the selection, you go back to the top-level display of all offerings but your "place" in browsing the offerings is not preserved - - you need to go look for where you were when you tried the selection that you ultimately rejected. I call this a "punishing user interface" because it causes you to do a lot of work to recover from any mistake or dead-end that the UI itself leads you into. This and other UI annoyances make the FireTV UI less than a 5-star rating. But to be fair, neither is Roku's UI something you are going to write a rave review about. Maybe Google or Apple has done better, but I don't have these to comment on.

title: Great Device
body: I have an older Smart TV that dropped support of Amazon Prime, which, to date, is the only streaming service I use. I was viewing Prime videos through an equally old Blu-Ray player. I won't bore you with the number of steps it took me just to get to Prime, or the problems I had once there, so I'll just say that I bought the stick as an easier way to get there until I upgrade my TV. Since I don't have a 4K TV or Dolby Atmos yet, I can't speak to how well that works. However, I got this stick for the day when I do have both those things.Pros:1. It was extremely easy to set up. I'm about mid-range in my technical knowledge and abilities, but I truly think someone with fewer skills than I, could set this up. I just plugged it into the back of my TV, connected it to Wifi, and it worked!2. I takes me two steps to access Prime. I have to change the input, and then select "Amazon Prime," and that's it.3. I love being able to tell Alexa to switch back to my Spectrum Cable DVR without having to change input, and it was very easy to get Alexa to recognize my device.Con:1. When I fast forward, I want to see pictures rather than just hope for the best with numbers. I really thought that the stick would allow me to do that, but it doesn't. For some, this might not be a big deal, but for me it is. I hope that, in one of its updates, Amazon will allow the stick to do this.2. I'm having trouble finding all the items I have on my watchlist, which doesn't appear complete when using this device. There's probably something I'm not doing correctly--at least I hope that's what it is. I know I can use Alexa to locate something, but sometimes I just want to scroll through my selections.Since the only reason I bought this was for Amazon Prime videos, I'm happy with it, and recommend it for at least that purpose. It'll do just fine for me until I get a newer Smart TV.

title: Will update this review as necessary
body: I've gotten very accustomed to Amazon devices & I've got to say, that I really do like them. Just opening the newest Fire TV Stick 4K and the product itself is quite compact; which is great! The Fire TV Stick 4K itself is shorter in length than the remote, which is also not huge, but quite comfortable size unless you're beyond a giant. Set up is super fast from plug in, to "programming it," 10 minutes or less..w/o a doubt!! (Not including login to your existing Netflix, etc accounts.) NOT complicated by ANY means!! It does it all by itself, just follow the on screen prompts & push whatever buttons on the remote & you're all set!! REALLY THAT SIMPLE!! If it's anything like my Fire TV Box, I think I'm going to like it A LOT!!My ONLY question..which I wasn't quite sure about when I was placing the order was..I had originally wondered if the Fire TV Stick 4K would require to be plugged into a wall outlet or not? It might seem like an obvious answer of yes...but the reason I was thinking about this is because sometimes devices like this can SOMETIMES get it's "power" from what it's plugged into..& I know that would mean that the HDMI spot on the actual TV would need to be able to "handle" that..but I wasn't sure if MAYBE Amazon would've considered this & worked out a perfect solution, so that you wouldn't need to have one more thing to plug in, behind your TV. .... Sadly they didn't "handle" it... let's be clear..the Fire TV Stick 4K does include ABSOLUTELY EVERYTHING you'll need, (except for a TV), & it's SUPER EASY to set up.. BUT one less wire would've been FANTASTICALLY PHENOMENAL!! But that's just one small detail, so far! Maybe they'll address it w/next version??I'll finish/continue updating this review once I've used it for a little bit. Because as of this moment, the voice recognition isn't working/recognizing my voice..as my OTHER (slightly older) Fire TV Box easily does!!

title: Works as advertised, but some definite annoying limitations
body: The 4k output looks good on the TV, and the unit is responsive to all commands. As expected, it has good integration with the Amazon "universe"--Prime video, music, photos, etc (with the current exception of the Amazon Ring devices, which I understand they're working on). It has a decent number of the streaming services available, certainly almost everything I use. (I do have to switch over to my TV's Android OS for some of the Korean and Japanese streaming services some in my family enjoy--I didn't mark the unit down for this. It can't support every streaming service from every country in the world.)My one continuing annoyance with this 4k device, and for ALL Amazon Fire TV units (and I own or have owned most of them), is storage. Every app, every streaming service requires memory, and there isn't a whole lot. I've purchased several of the family game packs which my family enjoys when we get together, but I can't load them on my system. I'm constantly having to go into the apps, and deleting things I use in order to load something else I want to use at the moment. I was hoping that they'd at least add some kind of microSD support. I'll happily go out and purchase a 128 or 256 GB card to supplement it. Bottom line is that it's just bad design. Their engineers are NOT paying attention to content requirements. And if I were to write this on an evening where I had to spend an inordinate amount of family time loading and unloading apps, I might knock this down to 3 stars.) This is the only real, solid negative to this device. And it's extraordinarily disappointing that they haven't addressed this even in the latest versions. (The Cube has a whole 16 GB. Nice try, Amazon, but a definitive and resounding NO! If you don't want to put 128 GB in the unit, then give us a microSD slot so that we can add it ourselves.)

title: Good Streaming Device
body: This is a great little streaming device. I use this mainly for Youtube, Netflix, Hulu, Plex and a few other apps and it plays all 4k content no problem. The UI is fairly smooth (not as smooth as the 4K Max because of the ram) and the wifi is solid. The remote is very ergonomic and has all the necessary buttons you need for a remote ( I prefer this over my Chromecast remote). It takes 2 triple A batteries which are even provided for you. There are a few cons though. One of the biggest reasons I removed a star was because they added Amazon ads back in 2021 that take up half the screen. It is annoying, but I combated this by installing Wolf Launcher (there are a few other ones I have not tried yet). For the most part the launcher has worked pretty well, but it bugs out every now and then, which brings me to the next con. It only comes with only 8gb of storage! 16gb should be the minimum and 32gb is ideal. I can only have a handful of apps installed before it runs out of storage space. At least let me delete some of the preinstalled apps I don't want/use. As for the ram, I am not sure why they only went with 1.5gb. As noted above, the UI could have been buttery smooth, if they went with 2gb of ram. I have the 2nd generation fire tv box 4k and that came with 2gb of ram. They went back to 2gb of ram with the 4k Max (which I also have), but that is extra money you have to spend which should have been present in the original 4k model. This device is listed at $50 msrp, but imo it is not worth $50 or even $40. I would not pay more than $25 for this. If you are looking to purchase this, I would wait when it goes on sale which is quite often. The 2 notable days you can get this on sale is either during Prime Day or Black Friday. Sometimes they have a trade in program which makes the deal even sweeter.

title: Probably the best value in a streaming device right now
body: I've gone through all generations of Fire TV devices. I left them for a while to go to Roku, when Amazon and Google had their little spat and YouTube TV was not available. Now that they resolved it, I eagerly came back and picked up a FireTV Stick 4k at Black Friday pricing -- but even at full price, it's a great deal.I'm glad that the Fire TV remotes now have controls for TV power and volume. (The remotes on my previous Fire TV and Roku devices did not.) What really impressed me is that they can be programmed (during the setup process) for multi-device configurations, like if you have a soundbar or receiver that takes care of the audio.
