Somehow the Roku remote wasn't able to turn ON the sound bar - but was able to change the volume if the sound bar was already ON. The Fire TV remote is also intuitive for the sound bar - if the sound bar is off, and I press the volume + button, the sound bar turns on.I also like that the remote has voice search. Staying on the Home screen and using the remote to say "Alexa, play <title>" works fine in most cases. Staying on the Search screen and just saying the name of the title (without "Alexa, play") also works fine - it shows the top search result. There were some quirks - saying "Alexa, play <title> on HBO Max" did in fact start playing the correct title - but from Episode 1 (even though I had paused midway in Episode 4). However, "Alexa, continue playing <title>" did work (it resumed where I had paused).Other Alexa searches on the Fire TV remote ("Alexa, what is the weather like today?") also worked great. It is not as natural as using my Echo Dot (have to keep the voice button on the remote pressed down while you speak), but it works well.Setting up new apps was confusing. I went to the Apps screen assuming I would see all apps I had installed, and a way to add more apps. Instead, there was an endless list of apps with no obvious way to quickly locate the app I wanted. I had to go to the Search screen, search for the App, and then add it (Using the Home screen, scrolling down to Your Apps, scrolling all the way to the right, and clicking Add more Channels brings up the same endless list)I find the Home screen very cluttered (compared to the minimalist Roku Home screen), but I don't go beyond the first two rows (Recent & Your Apps). Using it this way, it is nice to quickly get back to the App or show you were watching earlier.The Fire TV stick is very quick and responsive. The picture quality is also great (especially with shows which have Dolby Vision) - I basically don't notice any difference in terms of lag or quality watching on the Fire TV stick connected to my TV v/s using the in-built Roku function on the TV.The initial setup was also quick (the Fire TV stick did not fit directly into the HDMI port on my TV, but using the adapter that came in the box worked just fine).Overall, I think the Fire TV stick is a great value for money (especially at the discounted Black Friday price) - the remote alone is worth the price. I am using the Fire TV stick way more than I had planned to (except for Peacock and Apple TV+ which are not on Fire TV - still use Roku for that). Because of how some apps are only found on some of the streaming platforms (Roku, Fire TV, Google TV) but not all - having multiple options (like a Roku TV with a Fire TV stick) is good "insurance" for flexibility in the future.

title: A multi-tool resolution for many entertainment needs
body: I am a gamer, content creator and tech dork. My review will not apply to everyone's reason for purchasing the product.First off I'd like to say I was not into the whole Firestick TV thing. I didn't really get what the big deal was, I have multiple Xbox's and Roku devices, tablets, laptops. I really didn't pay attention to the product much.... that was UNTIL I found out it displays native 4K in most if not all applications. I was surprised at how I flicked back to my Xbox and YouTube was not in 4K HDR... you can watch 4K videos but it is not native 4K. THEN I found out you can load shadow PC onto the device and you can output display 4K gaming and HDR with dynamic lighting and RAY TRACING and I hit the floor. Now to explain to others who may not understand. I'm a gamer and I love to game, I use Shadow PC for my PCVR games as well as playing High Res PC games and running very heavy applications for video and recording editing. This is a way most people are able to get a high ended pc without shelling out thousands of dollars. Instead you download a program and run a virtual pc and it updates immediately to whatever the top pc technology that is out on the market and stays updated in the app so you always have the newest and best technology. No I am not sponsored by these guys but I know it helped me when I read what others were saying about it so maybe this may help someone else out there. So yes my firestick is now a walking portable pc in my pocket and I can take it ANYWHERE!Shadow Cloud PC - GeForce RTX 2080 (THATS 36 teraflops!!!!)VRAM: 16 GBRay Tracing ready4 cores / 8 threadsI am able to run all the next gen games at the highest output settings The Medium and Cyberpunk as well as very graphically detailed virtual reality pc games like Half-Life Alyx.Bare with me because yes I am nerding out here. I can connect any of my bluetooth devices to it easily so I can use my xbox controller and use it on my firestick or my mouse and keyboard. I can also just say "Turn on my LED lights" from my TV REMOTE!! and it controls all of my Alexa connected devices. I can get the weather by asking it or just asking general questions instead of launching the key phrase... I just push my mic on my remote and ask or say a command I did not know it could do all of this!Everything runs smoothly I never have any issues and I almost got a Apple TV4K first but I hear they were having issues with loading Shadow on it (long story). This sale randomly popped up and my aunt grabbed it for me now my xbox barely gets turned on lol. I can launch whatever I want from my firestick and it plays fine. I can go into xbox parties through shadow so I can still talk to friends and go play multi player etc. Yes I am speaking highly of the gaming aspect of this stick because this shows the versatility of the product.Side loading apps and programs as well as over all accessibility makes this a 100000% hands down easy purchase. Especially for the sale price of $24????? We got 3 OF THEM!!! lol I haven't been so happy with a purchased product in a long long time and this is just amazing. The future of gaming and entertainment in one bundle. Don't skip this one just buy it and be amazed at what you get for such a small price. I'm so happy I no longer have to travel with anything but my firestick and I have all things I need right there.

title: Cancelled cable finally!
body: My sister in law brought this over to our house for my nephew to watch his shows and YouTube. I set it up and fell in love! It's so easy to use and the free Pluto app has me calling ATT today to cancel service even with unlimited internet I will save $80 a month on the bill. That will be a savings of 1,920 over two years. I plan on going with Philo TV for 60 channels and $20 bucks a month. You can't beat that along with the Amazon prime movies/ kids shows. I have since purchased 4 more lol and each time set up has been great no buffering. No internet issues. It's been great and I hope to be able to save so much money using the amazon Firestick! If you're on the fence do it! It's awesomeEdit 8/28/2019:After playing with this and streaming TV through the app I realized it's sucking way more data than I had previously thought. Don't get me wrong the streaming service is awesome and the apps work great! Loved it but in our household it's not going to work. Unlimited data with Comcast would be $100 and unlimited by itself with direct TV will be $90. My satellite direct TV with unlimited internet not that much more expensive. I ran the device the morning for several hours and figured it's using 6.6gb an hour. I even changed the resolution to 720p and I'm shocked it is using that much data. My parents are retired and literally have the TV on for like 17 hours a day. I calculated how much how much data we would use and it would be like 3TB a month. That's insane.That being said I will keep these because I love the features and with used sparingly it won't be an issue. When storms come through and knock out the satellite it will be a great backup device as well, I still think it's worth buying even if you keep your cable service.Edit #2, So I checked our data and the boxes had not been being used and we were at 35gb for the day. I was confused because they had not been on. So the next day I unplugged the firesticks and low and behold our internet usage was cut in half. The firesticks continue to use data and STAY ON even when you think you have turned them off. So if you are concerned about Data usage simply unplug the device when not in use and when you use it plug it back in. No big deal. It powered right up for me and all my settings were saved. We may at some point down the line still cancel our cable and go with unlimited internet but I can't make up my mind. I absolutely love the firestick and use it in the kitchen and bedrooms. The Amazon prime exercise videos my mom loves. So its still a win-win. Just needed to keep them off when not in use to save on data.Edit/ Update #3-- I finally cut the cord 6 months ago. We no longer have to unplug the devices whatever was sucking data seems to have resolved itself. We just make sure to go back to the home screen (exit any applications) and then turn it off when not in use. We use Pluto (free) Philo $20 and FrndlyTV $5.99. the reason we completely cut the cord is because DirecTV decided to raise our bill to $190 a month. My internet bill alone is $70 so even after friendly I'm still saving $80 a month by cutting the cord. Our internet usage typically doesn't even get to 300gb for the month the resolution set to 720p. I have 5 firesticks now lol one in each room. I don't see a need to ever have cable again. ;)

title: Combined with echo dot
body: I recently ordered this fire TV stick in combination with a couple echo dots to dip my toes in the water, and see how I enjoyed the "Alexa experience." I'm very pleased! A little backstory--we have a smart TV, though it's not very "smart." It just doesn't offer all the apps we want, so I have always used a ROKU device with it. Sadly, I recently broke our ROKU out of sheer clumsiness. I was about to order a new one, but my kids had asked for Alexa for Christmas, so I took a moment to do some research on the array of gadgets that Amazon offers. The fire TV stick and echo dots (on special 2/$50 at the time) were a relatively low risk initial investment before possibly purchasing more devices.First off, the voice feature included on the TV remote works well and accurately. You simply press the microphone button, pause for half a second while the feature activates and then tell it what you want to watch. I took this a step further, however, and connected my fire TV stick with one of my echo dots. That way I can tell Alexa what I want completely hands-free. I just have to specify, "on fire TV," with my command.Another nice feature on the remote is the ability to control the TV volume and power. During setup, you're prompted to pair the remote with your TV to unlock that functionality. Another downside to our TV (gosh this TV is big, amazing and beautiful, but it's got a couple quirks, for sure) is that the remote sucks. Doesn't matter if it has fresh batteries, we're always struggling to get the TV and the remote to communicate properly. So I was pretty happy that the fire TV stick almost completely eliminates any need for the original TV remote. The only thing we ever need it for now is to change some TV specific settings. To just watch and enjoy, only the fire remote is necessary.As far as the offerings available on Fire TV, the list is endless. It's what you've come to expect from just about any similar device, I think. All major providers such as Netflix, Hulu, Sling TV, etc., along with all major network apps like CBS, NBC, STARZ, Cinemax, HBO and so on. I couldn't even tell you everything you can get because the list is that expansive. Because it is an Amazon product and we are Prime subscribers, it plays especially nice with all of the Amazon services.A happy discovery I made as well was that with Amazon Music (another service I subscribe to), not only do you get those handy lyrics on the phone app, but they are also on the fire TV app! Hellooooo karaoke party in my living room! If you want some better sound, the fire stick will also pair via bluetooth with an external speaker or an echo device. The smaller echo dots offer decent sound, but I do believe my next purchase will be an echo plus for something even better. Alternatively, there are also stereo configurations sold here on Amazon that play well with Alexa and fire TV.The user interface of the fire TV stick is easy enough to navigate. It's perhaps a bit more complex than the simplicity of the ROKU I was used to, but nothing one can't manage.All in all, I plan on purchasing more Alexa-enabled devices, in addition to another fire stick for my bedroom TV. We are really enjoying all the handy things Amazon and their great line of gadgets bring to our home.

title: Amazon Fire TV Stick is Good!
body: Just purchased the 4K version of the Fire Stick TV. The reason I bought this was to replace a Frontier set top box in my home office that I really no longer needed, but wanted to continue to be able to use my TV there. I did not totally "cut the cord", so I still use Frontier in the rest of my house. Not cutting the cord impacts how I was able to use my new Fire Stick TV. I chose Fire Stick over Roku, primarily because Fire Stick is cheaper.1. Initial set up was very easy through the Fire Stick menu system. I was up and running in 10 minutes.2. After initial setup, the task at hand is to start downloading "channels" that you want to see. There are lots of Channels available (ABC, NBC, FOX, FoxNews, CBS, AMC, TNT, TBS, Netflix, Amazon Prime, Hulu, Sling, Fubu, etc, etc, etc.). Again, this process is pretty easy. I already have an Amazon Prime account, so the rest of my review is based on that.3. Most of the channels that you want to download do not provide "live streaming" out of the box. But, they all include content that you can select and view. I wanted 'live streaming" on all of my channels. This was available primarily because I still have an account on Frontier. Most of the channels provide an option to view "live TV". Just open the channel application on Fire Stick, select the "live TV" option, and Fire Stick will present a menu to you containing an activation code and instructing you to go to the channel's web site to active live streaming. Activation was almost instantaneous after entering the access code into the channel's web site. Again, since I never cut the cord with my cable provider (Frontier), activating live streaming on each of my channels was easy.4. If you have or plan to "cut the cord" from your cable provider, you won't be able to activate the "live streaming" option for the majority of channels. You will only be able to see the static content available for each channel. In this case, the majority of people who cut the cord will install an HD antenna which can be used to pick up content from the TV towers in your area and run TV coax cable from the antenna to their TV's. Of course, this can be a pain.5. Overall, I'm very happy with my Fire Stick purchase. It essentially paid for itself in two months based on the monthly rental savings that I had in returning the set top box to my cable provider. I only have 20 mbps internet download speed at my house and I don't see any delays when watching any of the channels, but my Fire Stick is in the same room as my wi-fi router.6. The only downside so far is that I don't have a TV guide anymore through the Fire Stick, so I have to get the channel lineup and show start times for live streaming from a different source.7. Update: I just discovered an app called Kodi that you should download. Just go to YouTube and search on Kodi and there are lots of videos to explain how to download the Kodi app. After downloading Kodi, make sure you also download a Kodi add-on called Supremacy. Supremacy appears to allow you to live stream many of the common cable channels without going through the process outlined in step 3 above. I assume this works even if you've "cut the cord".

title: Easy Setup, Lots of Features, Takes Some Use to Get Most Out of It
body: I had a Fire Stick on my tv in the guest room rather than a cable box, and the grandkids mostly used it. Frustrated with my cable provider cost and service, I moved that Fire Stick to my main tv to try out for a couple of weeks. I was very happy with the content, ease of use, etc. so I purchased this Fire Stick for my main tv, upped my internet service speed (had a few instances of buffering and freeze on my old, very basic, very slow internet service), and I canceled my cable tv service. My monthly bill was cut in half, so in 1 month I recouped more than the device cost.As an Amazon Prime member, I get a lot great Prime original content and have access to many other shows and movies at no extra charge. I'm still learning all of the features and capabilities of this device. The voice command button, our gal pal Alexa, is extremely helpful with this and with finding shows and content. I really appreciate the remote's pause feature which provides the names of actors in the scene I'm watching, especially in older shows with actors who look familiar, but whose name I don't recall. I can pause what I'm watching at any time, go to other content, and come back to what I was watching when I want. If I miss something while watching, via the voice command button on the remote, I can ask to rewind any number of seconds, minutes or even restart the content from the beginning. I can resume watching content from where I left it even days later.I'll likely purchase a compact antenna to allow me to view local broadcast stations/news, but even without subscribing to for-pay streaming services, I'm finding plenty of content that interests me: Prime content, movies, old tv shows, national news (delayed), etc. If you're able to surf the net on a computer and are willing to take a little time to browse thru and play with the features and capabilities, I'd definitely recommend you get a Fire Stick.If you're not comfortable working on a computer or using a web browser or smart phone, you might find using the Fire Stick a little confusing. My 86 year-old mom can access FB on her Fire tablet and would really enjoy access to some of the content available via the Fire Stick, but I won't suggest that she buy one as it would likely be too confusing and end up frustrating her. My 6 and 8 year-old grandkids, however, whiz thru using the Fire Stick and access YouTube, games, tv shows, etc. have since day 1. They've been using Fire tablets since they were toddlers, so navigating its menu system is natural to them, even for one that cannot yet read. I also was able to set up parental controls on the Fire Sticks so that the kids cannot access inappropriate teen and above-rated content accidentally.I'm sure there is more for me to learn about the Fire Stick; learned a couple of days ago that I can send content that I'm streaming on my Echo Show to both Fire Sticks and view/hear it on those tvs simultaneously. There's an option that allowed me to sync the audio between the devices.I would definitely recommend this product to anyone looking to cut their cable, access all kinds of content with ease and take control of what they want to watch, when they want to watch it.

title: The best streaming stick available in 2020
body: We already had an Amazon TV Fire Stick 4K in the living room but it was time to replace the Amazon Fire TV Gen 1 stick in our bedroom as the Gen 1 stick couldn't load the Apple TV+ app, the menus had gotten slow and we were getting some glitches in the playback of content from all providers.Before just getting another Fire Stick, I evaluated the Tivo Stream, Roku and Fire Stick streaming devices to see if I wanted to make a change.The deal breaker with the Tivo was the lack of dedicated play/pause/fast forward/rewind buttons on the remote.The deal breaker for the Roku was the lack of dedicated volume buttons on the remote.The Fire Stick remote has both dedicated play/pause/fast forward/rewind buttons as well as volume control buttons yet still maintains a comfortable size.Also tipping the scales in favor of the Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K was the Alexa integration, that we already knew the user interface for the Fire TV Stick and remote, and that I had such a great experience with the initial setup of the one in the living room - especially the seamless configuration of the remote controls for the TV and soundbar.Also helping the cause for the Fire Stick was the fact that Amazon offered a an upgrade credit if our Gen 1 stick was still working and we had all the pieces (which it was and we did) and we set it to them. As a reminder, if you take the upgrade route, remember to reset the Gen 1 stick to factory settings before you box it up so all of your personal information gets wiped.I did end up having one glitch in the setup of the new Fire TV Stick which ultimately turned out not to be the fault of the Fire TV Stick. The catch was that when the setup process got to the audio I wasn't getting any sound. I went through all 36 options for the vintage JVC receiver we have but none worked. I then tried the audio setup again choosing sound bar rather than receiver but none of the six options worked. Somewhere in the back of my mind I vaguely remembered that there was an issue when I hooked up the optical audio to RCA converter I use to connect the television to the JVC receiver. Turns out the converter can not handle Dolby audio so I was able to go to the Fire TV Stick settings, then to audio settings and change the audio output from Best Available (which defaults to Dolby) to Stereo. Then the sound started working and I was then able to go into devices in settings and add a new device using receiver and JVC and got everything working.I did have to reload all of our apps for things like Netflix and set up the logins again but I would have had to do that if I had swtiched to a different brand so that was a wash.As expected we are now enjoying all of the benefits of a new Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K:Smooth playback of contentCrisp menu performanceAvailability of apps for all the services we subscribe toAbility to control the television, sound system and stick with just one remote controlAlexa integrationBottom line: This was a significant upgrade from our Gen 1 stick and we are happy with the purchase of our Amazon Fire TV Stick 4K and would do it again.

title: Does Exactly What We Need
body: There were two reasons we made the jump to Fire TV, cutting the expensive "cable" to satellite or cable tv and the voice command feature.I'll start with the voice command feature first. We have a blind individual in our household. We needed something simple to use that would get her to content without having a tremendous number of remotes or devices. The firestick fit the bill. It allows her to tune to live tv just by asking Alexa, but she can also access Sirius XM using the same remote. Alexa has excellent voice recognition which makes life much simpler for her. Works beautifully. The remote having just basic functions allows her to easily control volume and power as well - if she doesn't use Alexa directly.I found that we were streaming more and more television. With our prime membership it allowed us to watch a lot of content for free. I was already enjoying originals like Homecoming and Bosch but with the Fire Stick I could also start getting live tv through Hulu. I have a 4k tv but it is a little older so Hulu live was not directly compatible with my tv. The Fire Stick solved the issue. I have found it fun to be able to ask questions of Alexa like where a city is located if I am unfamiliar. When I'm done, Alexa continues the programming directly where I left off. I leave my tv on the Fire Stick as the default because all my services are available through it. I only go to the other inputs when I want to watch a Blu-ray or play on my Wii-U. Cutting the cord saves me $50 per month - I would imagine that others could save even more.A great plus is all the apps available. I use OANN, Netflix, Hulu + Live TV, Amazon Prime, Sirius XM, Pandora. There are many others. I can say that I am watching Amazon Prime and Hulu + Live TV much more than Netflix now. Amazon has upped its game with it's new originals.The remote does work with our Blu-ray but some of the features still need the use of the original remote but that's a minor caveat.Summing up Firestick TV:1. Allows viewing of content at a much reduced price2. Has excellent voice command capabilities3. Has an excellent selection of apps for various capabilities, including shopping if you want to4. Has the ability to benefit disabled persons, especially the blind5. Simple remote that has lots of compatibility6. 4K ability allows 4K televisions to stream 4K content at no extra cost7. Setup requires plugging into the power source and a HDMI input. Our tv immediately paired and did not require any further setup. Our Blu-ray was also incredibly easy to setup so no complaints there.The only con is that some TVs may still need the use of the original remote for some functions - like settings. The same is true of other devices used with the tv.I highly recommend this device to anyone looking to save money or that has a physical limitation. It works as advertised and actually has surpassed our expectations.

title: No more auto scrolling glitch (so far)!
body: First, I am glad I read the reviews before purchasing. I had recently bought an HDR 4K television, and after purchasing the Fire Stick 4K I found that the video was much softer than my Fire TV pendant had been, almost like it had a shade over it, and was not nearly as sharp and clear as my Fire TV. Then I remembered reading in some of the reviews about the HDR setting, and I found the following in an online article:If you have an HDR capable television and one of the newer Fire TV models, you probably want to change the Fire TV’s Dynamic Range setting from “Always HDR” to “Adaptive.” This option can be found under the Settings > Display & Sounds > Display menu. Changing this option to adaptive will make it so the Fire TV only switches to HDR when HDR content is played.This made all the difference! Now the picture is sharp, bright, and very clear.Secondly, I had a Fire TV Pendant, and a Firestick 2nd gen before that, and BOTH it and my Fire TV Pendant had a VERY annoying random scrolling issue where it would just take off and scroll rapidly in any direction, almost like the remote was stuck, or like you were pressing and holding one of the navigation buttons even though you were not even touching it. I tried putting in fresh batteries, and even had the remote replaced by Amazon, and it still did it with the new remote. It was driving me nuts. I searched online and couldn't find anyone who was having the same issue, so I was at the point where I thought it must be something I was doing, although I am convinced there was a bug in the former devices somewhere. I could sometimes make it happen simply by scrolling down rapidly on the home screen, then it would just take off like a jack rabbit. And it would also happen in any program, like Kodi, and others as well, so I know it was the devices themselves and not an application issue.So being hopeful that this Firestick 4K might be improved enough to get rid of that issue I took a chance and ordered one. The first thing I noticed is that the battery compartment is different, the batteries are in a single file, instead of side by side. That made me wonder if they had possibly done a total redesign of the remote and the internals, especially considering that it now contains volume controls for the TV. I am happy to report that so far, my luck has held out, and it has not done the rapid scrolling at all (so far). With the Firestick 2nd gen and Fire TV pendant it would happen almost every time I used it, at random times. So far this device has worked perfectly, with no hiccups. And it is VERY snappy, it is at least as fast as the pendant if not a little bit quicker. I am so glad I got this, and so far is the best Fire TV device I have owned to date. I am hoping they are slowly perfecting their products and making them better and better, and I am looking forward to enjoying this device without any scrolling glitches. Thanks Amazon!

title: So Easy, So Good, and So Complete Cable TV Is Truly At Risk...
body: I have an older Samsung 3-D Smart TV purchased for its other attributes that were bundled with 3-D only and the Smart part has long since been abandoned by Samsung making the acquisition of Disney+ near impossible and way overly complicated to access if I did manage to pick it up---answer: the newest generation of the Amazon's FireStick TV 4K.First, connectivity:Exactly everything I needed to connect the Fire Stick to my old Samsung was in the box. The thoughtfulness that went into the contents of the box is impressive, especially the HDMI pigtail that was included to provide maximum installation flexibility. Connection is a total no-brainer.Second, set up:At first I was concerned it was going to be a hassle but you just follow the on-screen prompts. The only sorta confusing part was pairing the Fire Stick remote with my old TV and my ZVOX Sound Base but once I realized that was what was going on, it was easy as pie even though I had to suss out the code numbers for my stuff out of the list provided on-screen by Fire Stick. You simply pick your TV model and Sound system (if you have one) and the Stick gives you a code number that you select and the rest happens automatically, including an simple operational test to vet the pairing. Voila, the Fire Stick remote controls my TV's power-on/off, selects the right video input/source HDMI port, and controls the volume level on my ZVOX Soundbase.It took all of three minutes to figure out how navigation works on the remote using the buttons and even less to figure out how to use the remote's built in Alexa voice control module to navigate. Depress the microphone button and talk to it telling it where you want to go in the menu(s) and/or what specifically you want (where to go or what you want to watch). Amazing!Third, performance. The Fire Stick just blows away the Samsung "Smart" modules in both my old Smart TV and newer Samsung Smart BlueRay/DVD player in streaming speeds, buffer capacity, and video playback speed and quality. A few seconds of trial and error navigation and the menu structure becomes almost intuitive and so easy to navigate. Finally, loading the Disney+ application was a no-brainer too as was selection of my subscription type and payment means. I happen to have an Amazon Prime membership and the content included with that membership, Netflix, and now Disney+ means I'll never run out of entertainment options.Lastly, my recommendation. Amazon's Fire Stick is so good I am truly considering dumping cable TV and will likely never purchase a "Smart" TV again. A simple one-time purchase of a good high gain HDTV antenna and I will have all the local TV channels I would possibly need and with Fire Stick TV system by Amazon all the free and premium services I can afford and could possibly want.

title: I'm finally a "cord cutter!"
body: The quick version of my review: Great if you’re looking to cut the cord, especially if you’re already a Prime member. We get all the TV viewing that we previously had through traditional cable, at roughly a 25% savings over traditional cable and internet service.The detailed review:I’m consistently a late adopter of new technology, but have been trying to convince my spouse to “cut the cord” for about two years. However, during a recent move, we spent 4 months temporarily in an apartment, which only allowed one cable/TV/internet provider, which was horribly overpriced. Based on that, my wife finally agreed to try “cutting the cord.” After looking at the various options, and we opted for the Amazon FireStick.Availabe information showed the FireStick had all the features we needed, with the added advantage of reviews touting the ease of setting up the remote to control both the TV and our soundbar. This would allow total control of our setup with just one remote. With traditional cable one of our biggest complaints, after the usual complaints about price and service interruptions, had been the difficulty of controlling the system with just one remote. My research indicated other streaming options might not be able to control everything off one remote. Also, because the FireStick is an Amazon product, and we are Prime members, it obviously makes viewing Prime offerings easy. However, it’s also worked very well with Netflix, PlayStation Vue, and Crackle apps. It's important to note we used the PlayStation Vue app for TV service, because we could get local programming, and it includes "cloud DVR" service, which meant we didn't need to have a separate DVR device.The FireStick has performed flawlessly with our Vizio TV and soundbar, although an older TV we used for a couple weeks, would not work with the soundbar. However, that is an issue of compatibility between the TV and the soundbar, not the FireStick. With our newer (although it is 6 years old) TV, everything worked great, and setup was easy. Occasionally there were some very minor glitches with streaming interruptions, but that was due to the poor internet service at the apartment, not the FireStick. After moving out of the apartment we switched to a different, and much better (along with less expensive) provider, and the FireStick has worked great.So in the end we have all the features we got with traditional cable, plus Netflix, through the use of the FireStick, and the previously mentioned apps, with a more user friendly interface (one remote for everything). This was accomplished at roughly a 25% cost savings.We are very pleased, and getting ready to buy another FireStick for the guest bedroom TV.

title: Recast and Fire Stick.  Much, much better than Tablo!
body: After struggling with Tablo DVR connectivity and poor-to-no performance issues for 3 months, I finally decided to try the Amazon Fire TV and TV recast system. It works Great!!! I originally went with Tablo to avoid extra hardware (ie. a fire stick at each TV) and trusting many “best of” reviews available out on the web, but at the low price of a few fire sticks vs. months of 100% frustration and time lost with Tablo never working, it is very worth it to make the switch to the fire TV system. Plus with Fire Recast I don’t have to pay the two subscriptions Tablo requires (one TV Guide and one to even make the Tablo software even remotely useable) ...but both of which are useless when the Tablo won’t even play a live tv stream, nor connect, nor play back a recorded stream, ...).The technical support at Tablo pretends to not understand that their firmware/software has severe issues which randomly cripples the devices operation. They focus instead on telling the customer they have poor reception. Well news flash Tablo: you’re always going to have fringe channels that any channel scan picks up which a device sold for the purpose of HDTV watching and recording which requires the device to be able to effectively deal with. And besides, the fringe channels weren’t even being watched or recorded so it is an even bigger firmware problem if fringe channels are causing a Tablo to lock up all the time.Amazon has the technology down! Fringe channels are almost nonexistent since switching over to Fire recast. Likely due to better antenna signal sensitivity and digital signal processing algorithms.Also haven’t had a single major issue with the Fire TV system for what’s going on a month now.Sometimes the image is a little fuzzy, but then shortly after it clears up. I assume this is due to the device adjusting to WiFi network conditions and both network and recast optimizing when they can. But it’s better than a device (like Tablo) that locks up and becomes unresponsive to where you can’t even change a channel or connect for that matter.The Tablo would always negotiate the WiFi connection rate (throughput as measured by my upgrade router) down to the lowest rate it could (6 Mbps in my case) and just get stuck there indefinitely or sometimes would reset the following day. At 6Mbps the Tablo would not stream anything and often even fail to connect. I upgraded my entire WiFi network trying to get Tablo to work, but everything else works incredibly great on both my old and new WiFi networks (ie. Fire cast/fire stick, stream Netflix, Amazon Prime, etc.) all over WiFi without any issues. It was also random if a power cycle was going to restore Tablo to a better WiFi throughput rate or not.

title: Cord Cutters DREAM, the BEST streaming device on the market, and its 4k.
body: The 4K Amazon Fire Tv Stick is not only the best media streaming device on the market, but one of the most affordable. You DO NOT NEED A 4K TV TO USE THE 4K FIRESTICK and the increased performance is well worth the additional $10!!! Packaged with the new voice remote with volume and power controls, the 4K amazon firestick allows you to control your firestick, tv, soundbar, and receiver depending on your setup. Say goodbye to the days of having to use 2 pr 3 remotes to properly control your streaming setup. Setup is a breeze, it takes about 5 minutes from plugging in, to connect to wifi, select what kind of TV and soundbar/receiver you have and your remote is ready. Its not an additional remote to go with your others, its the ONLY remote you need to control everything. This is truly unique. With Roku Ultra(2x the price, their highest end model)you get an additional remote, that can control ONLY the roku. With ChromeCast Ultra, you get no remote, only being able to control the device through an application which can be extremely annoying.The incremental upgrades aside from the remote from the previous firestick are also drastic and noticeable. The responsiveness is greatly improved, the 4k firestick packs almost twice as much processing power, and .5GB more RAM but improved DDR4.All the improvements stated above are noticeable when using the 4K Firestick. However, one area of improvement could be the layout of content. Amazon obviously wants to drive more users to their content, but their layout in some ways would make you believe the only option on the firestick is to view content through their sources. This is not the case, and with some small adjustments, users can re-order the applications that appear first to be the ones you not only use most, but want to be first. Watching liveTV through apps like HBOGO, PlaystationVUE, Plex and others is easy and performs great. And just as in other iterations of the firestick, you can still load 3rd party apps on the firestick after adjusting the setting to do so. So to all the Kodi users present and future out there, you can still side load it.All in all, for an additional $10 it is well worth it to get the 4k firestick over the normal 1080p firestick for the MASSIVE performance increases due to the better hardware. Even if you dont have a 4k TV, do yourself a favor and get the 4k stick so that when you do upgrade your TV, not only is the firestick capable of delivering you 4k content, but it can do it with speed. I highly recommend this product to EVERYONE who is interested in streaming. It is one of the most affordable and best streaming products on the market, everyone should have one!

title: Major Improvement Over My Old Fire TV
body: I picked up this streaming stick yesterday at Best Buy to replace my old First Generation Fire TV box. Needless to say this stick is a huge upgrade in software and usability over my old box. Faster response times in all apps and the new remote alone makes it worth upgrading.Once I hooked the stick up it went through an easy setup process to recognize my TV and also find how to operate my old Yamaha Digital Sound Projector to control volume and mute functions. The power button on the remote controls my TV off and on as well. This effectively eliminates the need for my receiver and TV remote for most things. This is why I said the remote alone makes it worth the purchase. If you already have a device that is compatible with the new remote you can buy it separately for $30. I should note that my 1st Gen Fire TV does not work with the new remote and would not pair with it as it is not compatible.Beyond the remote and visual updates for 4K TVs, everything else is the same from a software perspective. This means if you are a Prime or Prime Video subscriber you will find a lot of benefits from the way content is presented. It definitely favors more to the Amazon Prime members. What is less focused to Amazon is when you do voice searches where content will be favored to watch for free from apps installed. As an example, when I did a search for the movie Rocky I saw the first choice to watch is for free on my Sony Crackle app. This is in spite of the fact that you could also purchase or rent the movie from Amazon. What is great about this search is the number of apps that are included in the search. This is better then the Apple TV but less then the Roku searches on their devices. Yes, you will find more apps on Roku searches which could lead to opportunities to watch content for free or at a reduced price on multiple services. The visual appearance and voice search on the Fire TV however are far superior to Roku and this does not even include the extra functions built-in with Alexa. What got me into the Fire TV years ago was the voice search and it is still great today but not as advanced as Apple TV's Siri voice search.At this time I would put the Fire TV as the second best streaming box I have used. The Apple TV is still the best, but the gap is narrowing. Where the Apple TV exceeds is the outstanding advanced voice search functions with Siri and the TV App which pushes more diversity in content sources. However the Apple TV is expensive and if you are not an Apple person it is hard to beat the price and features of Fire TV in any form. This 4K stick is a great value for the price and features which is why I am giving it 5 stars.

title: Love it so far!
body: I bought this because my 65" Samsung UHD Smart TV is getting a bit too long in the tooth now to be able to access some among this enormous, and in my view rather unpleasant, proliferation of streaming services. I was an "early adopter" and and this is one of the pitfalls of that strategy, alas! The new services are not developing apps for a TV as old as mine, and unfortunately this includes a couple of biggies, Disney+ and CBS All Access, rendered mandatory by being the only sources now for their content, which include "The Mandalorian" and "Star Trek: Picard."But I digress. I opened this product and it was well-packaged and complete, except it does not come with wired Ethernet capability -- you need to purchase an additional attachment for that. Fortunately my 802.11ac router sits only about 18 inches from the device so I do not need it.When I first installed it to my HDMI #4 connection -- the other three are consumed by my UHD-DVD player, my HD-DVD player (Yes, I still have one of those!) and my A/V Amplifier -- on the back of my TV it came right up, but before I had subscribed to any service, when I was just exploring the menus and signing into my Amazon Prime account, a couple of times the screen went black and I would have to restart the entire system to bring it back up.But after I subscribed to a few services (like the aforementioned two and a couple of others) the problem has not recurred at all. It as been rock-solid stable and I have been able to program it into my Logitech Harmony unified remote control with no problems whatsoever.The picture and sound quality are truly excellent, and the response speed of operation over my wireless connection is superb, possibly even better than my TV or UHD-DVD player get over their wired connections. Aside from the glitches I mentioned above, setup was extremely straightforward and uncomplicated, with my only complaint being that the virtual keyboard that you have to use to login to services is arranged in alphabetical order (despite having the keys arrayed much like a traditional keyboard) rather than the more commonly familiar QWERTY. At least it defaults to letting you see the passwords as you type them, which saved a clumsy oaf like me a lot of time.I have not tried the voice commands or Alexa integration yet, which is why I gave them the middle rating of "3" -- they did not have a rating for "N/A." I assume, but I am not sure, that it will even integrate with the Echo Spot I have in front of the TV, allowing me to put away its own voice remote. I mean, the Spot can control my FiOS cable box so I assume it can control its own sister product.

title: Has easily replaced my Roku device
body: The sole reason I bought this item was so that I could actually watch Twitch on my TV without running it through a 3rd party app on my phone and screencasting it to my TV. The bonus is, this Fire Stick also includes every other app you can think of, Amazon Prime, Hulu, Netflix, PlutoTV, Disney+, and the list goes on. It even has browser apps you can download (which my Roku did not have). My biggest question in purchasing this was, would it actually plug into my TV considering these HDMI sticks are so large that they don't fit into some of the ports on the TV, because of the way the TV frame is beveled out. Luckily, this item comes with its own extension cable so that you can plug the cable into your TV and plug the fire stick into the cable. This does not degrade the quality at all.As far as performance goes, this thing is quick and responsive. Scrolling through menus is ultra fast. Setting up the remote was extremely easy after reading the instructions and going through the on-screen prompts, and now when I hit power on the remote it turns on/off my TV and my sound bar. That's very convenient, considering I was using 3 remotes before (Roku remote, TV remote, sound bar remote). The remote doesn't have an option to change the input on my tv, so I still need the TV remote for that, but that's on rare occasions when I need to flip it over to the DVD player or my Nintendo Switch. The Fire Stick has its own built in sound boost which I had to go in and turn off, because I found while it was boosting sound to make it feel louder and give it more bass, it was muffling the dialogue so much, I had trouble hearing what people were saying. This is probably due to my TaoTronics sound bar as it's not anything worthy of top notch quality sound.My only beef with this device is that sometimes when I power on my TV using the remote, it will say "no input device," and then I have to switch over to another input and then switch back to the Fire Stick using my TV remote and it will work correctly. I'm not sure what causes this, as the Fire Stick is still running and functioning normally - the TV just isn't recognizing it on power up. This happens about once every 10 to 15 power ups so it's not that big an issue, just a small annoyance.Overall, this product is an excellent source of entertainment as it has almost every app you can think of to watch TV or stream videos. There are occasional times when it goes on sale, so snag one when you see a price drop if you're worried about the cost.

title: OK I took the plunge and cut cable TV, this made it possible!
body: My cable TV bill bundle (TV, Internet and Phone) was over $228 a month!! I just could not justify paying that much for something we didn't use enough. So I looked for various things to take it's place.Well after researching quite a bit (over 4 months worth) I chose this Fire TV. I am so glad I did! We cut Cable TV and Cable Phone, we use Cell strictly now, (and no 911 is not an issue, you can call them and register the number for the address where you live, takes 5 minutes). So now all we have is cable internet, and it's less than half the cost.The Fire TV allows you to hook it up to your wired or wireless internet and stream media over it. You download "Apps" just like you use on your phone to it and play videos, movies, and music through it onto your TV.I wanted to make sure I could get local channels (Over the air was not an option because of where I live) so I looked at different apps. I settled on Playstaton Vue (no you don't have to own a PlayStation to use it). It is a TV streaming service that gives you choices of what you want to pay for (tiers) and also gives you local channels. It detects where you live by the internet signal and feeds local TV onto the list of channels you pick. IT's FAR cheaper than cable TV and quite frankly just as easy to use. Has a guide like a cable TV and even a DVR system to allow you to record shows to watch later, this is all on PlayStation Vue.Add apps (free) like YouTube, Netflix, Hulu and any others you can think of including the free ones (there is a way to jailbreak the FireTV and allow downloading of almost any app to it if you want) and you have a huge selection of TV and Movies to choose from. I now have one on each of the three TV's in the house. My son loves it on the TV in his room.All told with paying for Cable Internet and the streaming services I have (PlayStation Vue and NetFlix) I am paying around $110. That's less than half what I was paying.One thing to consider is what "level" of internet access you have. I already had raised my internet level of service (number of GIG's a month and speed) because I ran a lot of devices and internet stuff so I didn't have to up that, but you may want to see how much data you will use in a month and may need to up your internet level with your provider.Overall it's great and easy to install and use. Uses one of the HDMI ports on your TV. And it will even turn your TV on and off. Does require it's own electric plug for the power source.

title: LOVE my Fire Stick!!!
body: This is a favorite purchase of my family, and we ended up buying more for other members of the family. I love that we can get Prime Videos, as well as all other streaming services, TV channel apps, YouTube, and a variety of other apps. I recently tried the audiobook function, and it was a great feature to have. This has made life so much easier, and I love that you can use the voice recognition on the remote.Unfortunately, it does not work as well as we had hoped when trying to give directions through our Alexa/dot. It can pause well, and play again if we do it soon after pausing. One problem is that after it is paused for a while (maybe a few minutes, not very long at all), if you say "play," it won't play. It will be on a different screen, which is very frustrating. I have tried to raise and lower the volume with Alexa, with minimal success. The fast forward and rewind functions are the worst functions with Alexa - they either keep fast forwarding to the end of the movie/episode, or rewind sporadically. If I say "next episode," it seems to work every time. Unfortunately, if it is a TV show with the option to "skip intro," Alexa doesn't seem to recognize that command. I would like to see Alexa recognize many more variations of commands, so you don't have to keep a list of exactly how to say some thing for Alexa to operate properly. Perhaps if it doesn't understant what you meant, it could say "did you mean X?" - and then learn from your response so it knows what to do the next time.With that said, it is great to have to use when watching a movie or TV shows while you are multi-tasking. If I am making dinner or baking, and my hands are dirty or I am in the middle of something, I can have Alexa go to the next episode, etc. As mentioned above, volume changes and other things are hit-or miss, but would be even better if they worked consistently.One major revision they need is to have a light-up feature so you can hit a button and the buttons light up in the dark. After some use, you can get used to which keys are where by feel, but it would make life much easier if the buttons lit up in the dark - I'm sure this is a frequent complaint/request. Even the remote that comes with my cable box has that feature.Another problem is in the feature that allows you to use Alexa to change the input on your TV. It allows you to set it up to move right/left to a position to get to that input. However, it doesn't seem to work to take you back to the original input setting.

title: Couldn't Be Happier (sort of) With A Streaming Device
body: I used to use a Gigabyte Brix mini PC as my streaming/multimedia device, but would run into streaming issues and laggy webpage loading constantly. I bought a Firestick 4K and WOW... what a difference. The specialized hardware and software really makes a difference and in such a small, convenient package... what's not to like?Pro's:- Streams 4K buttery smooth over 5GHz wifi (through 2 walls even)- I can add 3rd party apps like Kodi and game emulators by enabling developer options- Learns the basic TV controls so I don't have to switch remotes when I want to turn on/off the TV or adjust volume (I did encounter an issue where it doesn't not sense my Sony audio receiver, however, so I still have to use a second remote for that)- Option to have it automatically linked to my amazon account makes setup a breeze, and you can get right to watching prime video and listening to music on amazon music- Dirt cheap for what you get. Bang for the buck is way up there.- Tiny device, takes up no space behind my receiver/TV's so it's practically invisible- Can have multiple units throughout my house with their own independent remote controlsCons:- No ability to remove categories/banners from home screen (you can customize your "Recents" and "Favorites", but you still have lots of apps and content listed that you will most likely never use and no way to remove them from the home screen)- Did not pick up that it was connected to my Sony surround receiver, so I still have to use a 2nd remote for volume in my living room. Not that big of a headache, but slightly disappointing.- No way to "mirror" or "sync" between firesticks. You'll have to download and set up each app from scratch for each unit in your house (minus the linking to your Amazon account). This means when I set up Kodi, I have to have each one configured to scan my media archives and there is no watch history between the instances. Also if you have content on one (music, videos, photos) you'll have to copy them to the others unless you just view them from a NAS or server storage device.- USB power cable could be a bit longer, my bedroom TV is mounted on the wall, but the closest outlet is down closer to the floor (as outlets usually are) so the cable is stretched pretty much to it's limit.Honestly the Pro's overwhelmingly outweigh the Cons for this device, especially with the new sale price. I have 3 of these in my house already and am very pleased so far.

title: I Wish I Got This Sooner!
body: For about 5 years I have used a Roku. It was fine. It worked. Until recently when it fell prey to forced retirement because Roku thought that they could force people to buy a new one by no longer supporting the old.I feel like I was in the streaming stone age.Pros:1. "Alexa, Turn Off the TV". I love being able to drop my remote and still control the tv. I am very disabled so a dropped item is often gone until my assistant comes and can bend. I have some tools to help this of course but having a back up is legitimately money saving.2. If I search the firestick it lists all the hits for a show. I don't have to manually search in Netflix, HBO, Tubi, and so on one at a time. I simply input my search (I am not always able to speak so added win to still type if needed) and off we go. Roku couldn't handle what I always felt was the point of a search feature at all.3. SPEED! Holy heck does everything load fast. Supposedly my Roku wa ls an equivalent device. Lies! I have a faster load time for everything and my Firestick remembers my log ins for more than 3 accounts.4. Improved Picture. I have a 4kish tv. It's a Fingerhut Manufactured set that's supposedly 4k. The Roku couldn't even handle 1080p HD. It was the HD one. I feel like my TV got new glasses.5. I am a clumsy person due to the mess that is mine spine. I sometimes hit the wrong button when pausing. If I close youtube instead of pausing to take the call? I just reopen it and the Firestick picks up immediately where we left off. The Roku? Sometimes would say the language or video wasn't available if this occured. Even after a reboot. I had to wait days sometimes to access the app again. This was definitely part of my decision to try something new.There's so much more I can say. I found 0 arenas where the Roku ecosystem actually beat out the Firestick. None. The remote has small bumps so visually impaired people can find the correct buttons. The shape is actually ergonomic. For some reason the Roku remote always ended up in my hand upside down. I don't have that issue with the Firestick.I knew I would be happy with access to Alexa as a part of the system but I expected everything else to be about the same because the reviews I found all said that they were. I am guessing these reviewers didn't spend much time with the devices because CNET and friends missed the huge divide in quality.

title: Pleasantly Surprised
body: Streaming technology took a giant leap in 2020 with multiple studios launching apps. The questions no one was really answering was would these apps support 4K UHD, HDR, HDR10, and Dolby Vision as well as Dolby Atmos?The 4K Fire Stick handles 4K UHD Dolby Vision/Dolby Atmos without a hitch. The only way I know to enjoy 4K UHD Dolby Vision and Dolby Atmos simultaneously is to connect the Fire Stick to a 4K UHD DV/DA capable AVR/processor input. For now, I enjoy Dolby Vision/HDR content on my LG C7 in 5.1 PCM surround and Dolby Atmos with my 1080P UHD projector via my Anthem 1120 AVR.I like how the 4K Fire Stick easily allows maintenance for each streaming app. Force stopping and clearing cache for each app after heavy usage ensures the stick operates smoothly without lag, buffering, or freezing. My suggestion is to keep available apps at a minimum to allow as much memory as needed for reliable operation.The UI format is acceptable. I do not care for the Recent list because I would just as soon organize my apps according to use which the Fire Stick allows. The Recent list row is just another thing I have to scroll through to get to an app I want. The new UI in Fire Stick Lite is a bit better in having your top 5 or 6 apps on the starting row.I like the 4K Fire Stick compared to the 4K ROKU Ultra. I used ROKU exclusively for many years, but the recent increase in streaming apps and failure for ROKU to offer AppleTV and HBOMAX at the time pushed me to Fire Stick. Once ROKU did get HBOMAX, it choked multiple times requiring restart (to clear cache?). Also, once apps are signed on with Fire Stick, nothing is lost unlike the ROKU which seemed to only keep up with the last 3 app sign-ins. Yes, there are work arounds for every issue, but can someone just make a product that meets demand without work arounds?Fire Stick 4K is the closest product to my demand. Unbeknownst to me, the smart TVs were a good idea that ultimately fails because TV companies want to sell TVs rather than support them. The whole reason I got a Fire Stick is because my LG C7 was not offering AppleTV or HBOMAX apps.I expect Amazon developers are working on higher memory capacity with the explosion of apps and proprietary content. The wireless aspect of their devices is desirable for those wanting TV anywhere. Other applications could use a 1GBe connection for media rooms/home theater.

title: Works Great ... And at a Very Reasonable Price
body: [[VIDEOID:fae5624ec997604878f76df9be0efaf1]] I have a 55" LG Smart TV that is barely a year old. It works great, but there are not a large variety of apps available from the LG storefront. Thus, I decided to get an Amazon Fire TV. Since I have a 4K TV I bought the 4K Amazon Fire TV. I am extremely happy with this purchase.Setup is a breeze. Simply plug the Amazon Fire TV into an HDMI port on the television. Attached the included power cord to the Amazon Fire TV and plug it into the power outlet. Put the two AAA batteries (included) into the remote and your Amazon Fire TV is ready for setup. To get to this point takes all of a minute (literally). Next, the on-screen menu walks you through a series of set up prompts such as preferred language, wifi network setup, popular apps to install, cursory Alexa voice recognition training and then your Amazon Fire TV is ready for use. To get to this point is less than 5 minutes.Navigation through the Amazon Fire TV home screen is simple and the interface is visually appealing. There are literally hundreds of apps that can be installed on the Amazon Fire TV. My experience is that it takes less than a minute to download an app. The home screen can be customized so that the order in which apps appear on the screen matches your use. For example, if you watch a lot of Netflix and Hulu you can move those apps so they are listed first among your apps on the home screen.The picture and sound quality is fantastic. The 4K video is stunning on my 4K TV. There is no lag or freezing. I do have high speed internet so I am sure that helps. Sound is a rich blend of bass, medium and treble.Please note that Amazon Fire TV does not support the HBO Max app. This is important to me since it is one of the apps I use regularly. Do a Google search and you will find numerous work-arounds that allow you to add HBO Max to your Amazon Fire TV. I did this and it works flawlessly. My understanding is that Amazon and Roku have blocked the HBO Max app from their services since they view it as direct competition ... you can Google this as well. My point is that your Amazon Fire TV is not going to fail if you add the HBO Max app so do not be concerned about adding this app if (like me) you have an HBO subscription.I am very pleased with my purchase and highly recommend the 4K Amazon Fire TV. Five stars.

title: A real review, real customer, not paid
body: The fire stick started my Alexa movement in my home. Where I once had a Roku ultra, which the earlier models didn't support 4k, so I upgraded. I haven't looked back since. It may sound trivial, however Alexa learned my TV and soundbar in a matter of seconds. I have since upgraded my lighting to smart bulbs, which makes my home feel like an episode of Star Trek. "Alexa turn on all the lights" and viola! All lights come on. Some people make the argument of how useful a cell phone has become, well I feel the new narrative is how we survived with out Alexa. My next upgrade is a Nest thermostat. The ease of use, integration, and cost to automate my home has been painless, cost effective and fun.This review was supposed to be about the fire stick, but Alexa is so much more than streaming device. So why I upgraded to the 4k stick to begin with, like I said, I had a Roku ultra, and my remote broke, so I bought a fire stick. The first thing I noticed was while watching 4k content, I couldn't game online. That was my first indication that I wasn't getting actual 4k content, I had to upgrade my router from an airport extreme to a Linksys 1730. That cleared up my bandwidth issues. Disney plus broadcasts in 4k UHD, which makes the movies amazing. If you are on the fence let me give you a few tid bits.1. If you are an apple only person, Siri is great, buy a an apple TV and enjoy, my personal opinion is that Amazon products are as good, same integration potential at a much lower cost. If having the most expensive device is your thing stay with apple.2. Google, is nearly the same, however feels and looks cheap to me. One notch for Google, the Google home will work with GE with out a smart plug, where Alexa will not. If you have already invested in Google products stay with Google, you will be happy, automation capabilities will be the same. Alexa premium is on par with Sonos, which Sonos is winning the race on soundbars due to copyrighted synching technology for multiple room streaming. Google premium is...eh at best.In closing, think about where you want to go once you get a voice command system, if automation isn't your thing the chrome cast, apple TV or fire stick will be fine, invest in what you are drawn to. I personally and overly satisfied with what Amazon has to offer, sleek design, ease of use, and great sound, I would recommend Amazon to a friend.

title: I had no idea I needed this in my life but now I am so happy
body: Let me start by saying that I am in my early-mid 30's and I fear that I am getting to that age where I cannot keep up with the latest technology anymore. I have a smart TV and was relatively content using various apps through said TV. The only issue I had was the receiver being a jerk at times and requiring a restart when switching apps (like going from Netflix to Hulu and suddenly losing audio). I just figured it was time to buy a new receiver.But then I learned of a streaming service called Shudder and it looked like everything I have ever wanted. Unfortunately, I had no way of accessing Shudder through my TV and so I did some research and found that Fire TV Stick was one option. I'll be honest here; I had no idea what a fire stick was, how it worked, if I needed to purchase other accessories or services to use it..... The good news is that I know how to use the Google machine and so I was able to learn that this fire stick was indeed a simple and straightforward means to access Shudder.I bought it with the intention of using it only to access Shudder since I was used to watching other apps directly through the TV itself and I am a creature of habit. Setting up the fire stick was super easy and upon seeing the well organized interface, I changed my mind instantly. I logged in to all of my usual apps via this fire stick and have been enjoying accessing them through one home page. I also discovered Amazon photos and that you can set up a screensaver the TV that will cycle through your photos so that your cats can admire photos of themselves on the big screen. Again, another feature I never knew I needed but now that I have it....I love it.As a bonus, accessing all of the streaming apps through the Fire stick has solved the issue I was experiencing with the receiver! No more sound issues when going from one app to another! I don't know if I can attribute this to the fire stick itself, but I mean, there were issues without the fire stick and now the issues are gone so..Finally, I will say the one and only negative thing I have experienced with the fire stick, and this is a super small thing. The app VUDU is not available through the Fire stick so I have to access that one through the TV. It's not a big deal really, and I don't know if that will someday be resolved anyway so I wouldn't hold it against the fire stick.

title: Easy set up, cool features, great value.
body: I already have a Vizio smart 4k TV but I find that its built-in OS isn't that great. I mean it works fine but it seemed a little generic. Plus it was missing some of the sports apps I use to stream (ESPN, NBC and FOX Sports). So when I saw the deal they had for the 4k stick I thought it was worth a buy. Probably one of the best buys I ever made.Pros: The voice feature is soo flipping cool. If you know what you're looking for or want (I.E. already know your favourite show is on prime or netflix) the Fire Stick will take you right to it. Its so handy telling it to rewind just by speaking (you do have to press the speech button though.)I notice that the picture and audio is more crisp than the OEM Vizio Apps. maybe it has to do with the wifi range extender it comes with thats able to have better signal to stream at higher quality constantly and while it isn't a humongous difference it is noticeable enough for me to use Fire Stick first than the apps the smart TV already comes with. The UI is very nice and premium looking.Cons: Its expected but since this is an amazon product it obviously pushes its content/apps first so sometimes its a little tricky to find other apps like netflix, hulu, etc. Also because of the whole Amazon vs. Google thing, if you're looking to integrate this into your overall smart home network then you kind of have to decide between sticking with Amazon's ecosystem or Google's. Fire Stick does not work with google smart home only with alexa. I have both but primarily use Google so its a little annoying having both Google and Amazon products in my house. It be nice to stick with one but I recognize that some amazon products are better than google and vice versa but if you're looking to incorporate this into you smart home ecosystem, try to stick with one brand for the best experience. If you're a heavy google user, maybe stay away from the fire stick.Also this should go without saying but adding a 4k stick to a non-4k TV does NOT turn it into a 4k TV. if you have a regular HDTV then go with the regular Fires stick.Overall this is a fantastic buy and further optimized my Smart TV by adding apps it didnt have natively and having the voice feature. If you're someone that isn't too happy with your Smart TV's OS/apps definitely consider adding a Fire Stick to it.

title: Can't Beat This With A Stick!
body: This 4k Firestick is da bomb, especially at Black Friday discount pricing!!! I’ve been a Dish Network subscriber for over a decade and have been looking to cut the cord (it’s a long cord to reach a satellite!) given annual price increases through the years. This new Firestick will be just the ticket for that as I’m looking to transition to YouTube TV most likely by end of 2019.The lady of the house believes that wireless EMF is problematic health-wise, so our setup required mating the Amazon Ethernet adapter with the Firestick for a fully wired solution. Ordered both at the same time and they worked great right out of the box: plug and play into our 10 year old Samsung 52” LCD TV. No muss, no fuss. The HD picture is fantastic as is the audio. Downloading/installing apps is quick and painless. The speed with which one can move from one app to another is nice and snappy. I have yet to experience a buffering problem with any of the content. My old Sony Blue-ray player/streaming appliance had excessively interminable content buffering as a “feature”. It’s so nice to be done with that.Some folks have mentioned the confusing interface of the apps on Firestick especially as compared to Roku. While I think the apps could probably be better organized, it’s not much of a hindrance. I’m used to using Amazon Prime which is busy-looking, so the Firestick interface isn’t much of an issue for me to navigate. The remote is simple and easy to use, making navigation a breeze. That’s especially true given the voice command ability.The next step for us will be to create a MOCA network to extend internet over coax to the other TVs in the house. This will require additional Firesticks and MOCA adapters, but that’s beyond the scope of this review. Gotta keep the wife happy.Finally, we must remember that we’re basically in the wild, wild west of media convergence. The fact that we have a solidly performing streaming platform such as Amazon’s Firestick is a great thing at this stage of the game. The one concern I have, and this is true with any content platform provider, is ceding control of available content choices. On a whim, any of these providers can add or remove content: here today, gone tomorrow. It’ll be interesting to see how all of this shakes out in the future.

title: I think I like my Roku better, but....
body: I have had my Roku for some time, not a long time, less than a year, but its familiar to me, so maybe that's why I prefer it. However, I have Alexa devices all over my house (dots, flexs, lights, switches, etc) and I rely on them for everything; so maybe my expectations were too high - but the biggest disappointment was how the Firestick did NOT integrate to my other Alexa devices.I honestly thought I'd open up my Alexa app after installing the FireStick and there would be an opportunity to 'add' it to my Alexa app, and therefore my Dots & Flexs…. nope, didn't have the option, didn't find it in a 'discover' and to use it I can't just speak to my wifi virtual no-touch devices - I literally have to hold the FireStick remote and push the button and only then can speak for the FireStick Alexa to hear me.But even then, its not like the regular Alexas that when I say 'turn off bedroom lamps' or 'dim my living room lights to 50%, that is it - its done. With this FireStick remote I have to say what I want then define it. Too much hassle compared to the other Alexas.Having said that, if you don't have a Dot or Flex, don't have the bulbs or switches, etc you are missing out. I can ask 'the air' as it were, 'what is the temp outside' or 'what is the weather going to be like today' or just about anything and I get an immediate, specific answer.UPDATE: still a bit of a challenge to navigate around this, but most would love WHY... because it has a zillion options of channels.With my Roku it was great, I had 3 major and some others that were minor add-ons ... but this thing comes packed with more channels than I’ll ever watch BUT IF you are a TV person... this is it.Now IF ONLY I could get my many Alexa dots and Flex to sync to it - that was the main reason I went with this... my house has Alexa devices ALL OVER IT, actually BOTH my houses. Can’t understand why when I got my first DOT it automatically recognized ALL my RING devices, but THIS made by same company (well amazon on both) it fails. It would be so amazing to be able to tell my Alexa to shut my TV off or turn my TV on or to be able to turn to a certain show or channel without me having to use the remote control. ONLY MAJOR DRAWBACK. But still I wouldn’t trade it

title: Much better than Android TV
body: I just bought my parents a 65in Sony Bravia TV (KD65X750H) with the Android TV operating system. The Android TV OS is ok, but not great. I loved that I could download Kodi directly from the Google Play store, but the TV's processor had a hard time maintaining voice sync on 1080p x264 movies. In addition, the TV was constantly dropping the Wi-Fi connection and failed to sign in to my Google account for the first minute or so the TV was turned on every day. Very annoying and from a Google search, not a unique issue (something that's been a problem for a couple of years now, with no fix from Sony yet). Also, while this is not Sony or Android TV's fault, my mother loves Amazon Music and it's not available on the Google Play store unless you have a Shield TV, and the reviews on that version of the app are bad anyway.I noticed last week that the 4K Fire TV was half off AND offered a split payment system with payments spread out and no interest. I figured for 6 or 7 dollars a month I had nothing to lose. They have the older Fire TV sticks in their bedrooms so this new 4K version came pre-registered - with their Wi-Fi password already configured. I found that a little unsettling at first but whatever, it's a pain to enter with a remote so the more I thought about it, this is actually a cool feature.I setup all their favorite apps (including Amazon Music) in 10 minutes, super smooth. They're also really excited about all the great recommendations for movies they may like on Prime Video and the free TV and movies from IMDB.I was able to quickly setup Kodi using the Downloader app (the Kodi apk direct download is the first result in a Google search via Downloader's built-in web browser). The quad core processor in the 4K Fire TV stick rips through their Movie library updates way faster than the processor in the Bravia TV. It also has zero issue playing x264 and x265 files with perfect voice sync all the way up to 4K resolution. Also happy to report zero issue maintaining a Wi-Fi connection. They're able to stream 4K content from 40ft away (through a wall) perfectly, on a gigabit broadband package using the 2.4 Wi-Fi band.The 4K Fire TV stick has proven to be the missing ingredient to them enjoying their new TV. It's truly awesome.

title: Was Really Excited for this Product
body: When the device is working, it is outstanding. Unfortunately, I have had a few issues with mine that make it almost unusable. Here are my issues:1. When I use the supplied HDMI extension adapter, I get no sound at all from the device. When I remove the extender, the sound is fine. The problem is the Firestick covers the neighboring HDMI port preventing me from using it with another device.2. The Firestick has been crashing without any kind of error. It just freezes up for a second and reboots.3. The Firestick drops WiFi signal after about 10 minutes of play time. When I reboot it, it picks up WiFi again. Initially, the WiFi shows strong signal strength. The TV is about 15 from my Wireless router. Other devices in the area have no problem. I would use the extender to help with signal strength if I could get sound while using it.Here are some postives:1. For the few minutes that the Firestick works, it is really awesome. I love the remote. I find it to be very intuitive.2. I'm an Amazon customer, so I like how the device interacts with my other devices.3. Setup was crazy easy once I realized that the extender was interfering with the sound. When I plugged the Firestick straight into the TV, setup was a breeze. It's hard to get past the volume setup when there are volume glitches.4. The menu navigation is very easy to work through.5. Picture quality is fantastic!!!Note: I'm gonna keep trying to work with it. Hopefully the device will get better. If it does, I will update the review gladly.Update: There are days that the device works great. Then there are days when the device or horribly unstable. It has to be reset constantly to get it to work. When it is reset, it may work for about 30 minutes, and then crash again. If it doesn't crash completely, it loses sound, or just stops playing the movie and returns to the home screen. The device has been really bad unfortunately.Update: I reached out to Amazon to see if there was anything that could be done to improve the device. They ran a test on the device remotely and found it to be defective. They immediately sent me another device that works perfectly. So glad that Amazon stands by their products. Bravo!

title: How to make a tv smart
body: Great product to make your not so smart tv smarter. I had several TVs that did not have smart apps built in and when I cut the cord I needed a solution that did not involve a cellphone to turn things off and on because not everyone in my family can learn new tricks. I had chromecast on a few which worked but it meant you had to pull out a phone to start to stream something. This fixes that and provides a remote for the old school folks.The remote integration works really well and is easy to setup. I have used it with sound bars TVs and multiple brands of amplifiers successfully. It almost makes the universal remote worthless and with just a TV and soundbar it would. It works through walls and cabinets and functions great with my harmony smart remotes as well. Picture quality has been great. I have my own 5g wifi router and google fiber so bandwidth has never been an issue getting higher quality streaming than with my hardwired chromecast at times.The voice functionality takes some getting used to and only works with specific applications like hulu and Amazon's services.
